### Step 2: Define Propulsion Velocity

If the user propels at velocity $v_{prop}$ (m/s):

$$
v_{prop} = \frac{\text{stroke length} \times \text{strokes/min}}{60}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Mechanical Power Output

$$
P_{mech} = F_{prop} \cdot v_{prop}
$$

### Step 4: Account for Human Muscle Efficiency

The human muscular system operates at an efficiency of approximately $\eta_{muscle} \approx 0.20$ to $0.25$ for sustained activity. The **metabolic power** required is:

$$
P_{metabolic} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{muscle}}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

Given:
- Total weight: $W = 80 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 = 784.8 \text{ N}$
- $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$
- $\theta = 4.76°$ (ADA ramp)
- $v_{prop} = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$
- $\eta_{muscle} = 0.22$

**Calculate rolling resistance:**

$$
F_{rr} = 0.010 \times 784.8 = 7.848 \text{ N}
$$

**Calculate grade component:**

$$
W \sin\theta = 784.8 \times \sin(4.76°) = 784.8 \times 0.083 = 65.14 \text{ N}
$$

**Total propulsive force:**

$$
F_{prop} = 7.848 + 65.14 = 72.99 \text{ N}
$$

**Mechanical power:**

$$
P_{mech} = 72.99 \times 1.0 = 72.99 \text{ W}
$$

**Metabolic power required:**

$$
P_{metabolic} = \frac{72.99}{0.22} \approx 331.8 \text{ W}
$$

> This is approximately **3.3 METs** (Metabolic Equivalent of Task), well within sustained activity range but indicating the significant physiological demand of manual wheelchair ramp climbing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: FIM Scoring Logic with Worked Example

A 45-year-old female with right hemiplegia following stroke is evaluated. The following FIM scores are observed across 18 items:

$$
\vec{S} = \{5, 6, 4, 7, 5, 4, 3, 5, 6, 7, 5, 4, 6, 7, 5, 4, 5, 6\}
$$

### Step 1: Sum All 18 Scores

$$
\sum S_i = 5 + 6 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 5 + 6 + 7 + 5 + 4 + 6 + 7 + 5 + 4 + 5 + 6
$$

Adding sequentially:

$$
5 + 6 = 11
$$

$$
11 + 4 = 15
$$

$$
15 + 7 = 22
$$

$$
22 + 5 = 27
$$

$$
27 + 4 = 31
$$

$$
31 + 3 = 34
$$

$$
34 + 5 = 39
$$

$$
39 + 6 = 45
$$

$$
45 + 7 = 52
$$

$$
52 + 5 = 57
$$

$$
57 + 4 = 61
$$

$$
61 + 6 = 67
$$

$$
67 + 7 = 74
$$

$$
74 + 5 = 79
$$

$$
79 + 4 = 83
$$

$$
83 + 5 = 88
$$

$$
88 + 6 = 94
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FIM Total} = 94}
$$

### Step 2: Interpretation

Since $94 \in [18, 126]$, the score is valid. A score of **94 out of 126** corresponds to **"Modified Dependence / Supervision"** — the patient can perform most ADLs but requires assistance with certain motor and cognitive tasks, and is a candidate for outpatient rehabilitation with assistive devices.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Assistive Devices

| Device | Typical User | Energy Demand | Cost Range (USD) | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual Wheelchair** | Bilateral lower limb loss | High | $100–$1,500 | Short-distance, upper-body-strong users |
| **Powered Wheelchair** | Quadriplegia, severe weakness | Low (battery) | $2,000–$15,000 | Long-distance, weak users |
| **Smart Wheelchair** | Cognitive + motor impairment | Very Low | $5,000–$30,000 | Severe disabilities, hospital use |
| **Axillary Crutches** | Temporary unilateral disability | Moderate | $30–$100 | Short-term rehabilitation |
| **Lofstrand (Forearm) Crutches** | Long-term bilateral use | Moderate | $50–$200 | Long-term mobility |
| **Walker (Rollator)** | Elderly, balance issues | Low | $50–$400 | Indoor, slow-pace mobility |
| **Cane (Single-point)** | Mild balance impairment | Minimal | $20–$80 | Mild gait instability |
| **Powered Exoskeleton** | SCI (T6–L1) | Battery + assisted | $50,000–$150,000 | Gait rehabilitation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAAT Model — Human Activity Assistive Technology Framework

This block-level architecture flow depicts the engineering interaction between the user, the activity, the assistive device, and the environmental context — the core of rehabilitation assistance design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Human User with Impairment] --> A[Activity or Task Demanded]
    A --> AT[Assistive Technology Device]
    AT --> C[Contextual Environment]
    C --> O[Functional Outcome and Independence]
    O -.Feedback Loop.-> H
    AT -.Performance Feedback.-> A
    H -.User Preference and Ability.-> AT
    C -.Environmental Constraints.-> AT

    style H fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#2C5AA0,color:#000000
    style A fill:#FFF4E0,stroke:#B07000,color:#000000
    style AT fill:#E0FFE5,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFE8F0,stroke:#A02C5A,color:#000000
    style O fill:#F0E8FF,stroke:#5A2CA0,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Assistive Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ASSISTANCE IN REHABILITATION ENGINEERING] --> B[Physical Devices]
    A --> C[Sensory Devices]
    A --> D[Cognitive Devices]
    A --> E[Environmental Modifications]
    A --> F[Personal Human Assistance]

    B --> B1[Mobility Aids: Wheelchair Walker Crutch Cane]
    B --> B2[Prosthetics: Upper Lower Limb]
    B --> B3[Orthotics: Brace Splint Support]
    B --> B4[ADL Aids: Dressing Feeding Bathing]

    C --> C1[Visual Aids: Braille Screen Readers White Cane]
    C --> C2[Hearing Aids: Cochlear Implant Hearing Aid]
    C --> C3[Tactile Aids: Vibrating Alert Systems]

    D --> D1[Memory Aids: Smart Wristbands Digital Reminders]
    D --> D2[Learning Aids: Speech to Text Software]
    D --> D3[Behavioral Aids: Routine Cueing Apps]

    E --> E1[Architectural: Ramps Elevators Wide Doors]
    E --> E2[Smart Home: ECU Voice Control Automation]
    E --> E3[Workspace Adjustments: Adjustable Desks Ergonomic Chairs]

    F --> F1[Service Animals: Guide Dog Mobility Dog]
    F --> F2[Personal Care Assistants]
    F --> F3[Intervenors and Interpreters for Deaf Blind]

    style A fill:#2C5AA0,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#2C5AA0,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFF4E0,stroke:#B07000,color:#000000
    style D fill:#FFE8F0,stroke:#A02C5A,color:#000000
    style E fill:#E0FFE5,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000000
    style F fill:#F0E8FF,stroke:#5A2CA0,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Functional Independence Measure (FIM) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Patient Performs Task] --> Q1{Does patient require helper contact?}
    Q1 -->|No helper| Q2{Does patient use device or extra time?}
    Q2 -->|No| F7[Score 7: Complete Independence]
    Q2 -->|Yes| F6[Score 6: Modified Independence]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{What percentage of effort does helper provide?}
    Q3 -->|Less than 25 percent| F4[Score 4: Minimal Assistance]
    Q3 -->|25 to 50 percent| F3[Score 3: Moderate Assistance]
    Q3 -->|50 to 75 percent| F2[Score 2: Maximal Assistance]
    Q3 -->|More than 75 percent| F1[Score 1: Total Assistance]
    Q1 -->|Helper only nearby no contact| F5[Score 5: Supervision]

    style S fill:#E0FFE5,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000000
    style F7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000000
    style F6 fill:#B8F0B8,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000000
    style F5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B07000,color:#000000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per KTU 2024 Scheme]`

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**Define "Assistive Technology" as per the KTU Rehabilitation Engineering framework. List any four categories of assistive devices with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Assistive Technology (AT) is any item, piece of equipment, software program, or product system that is used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of persons with disabilities. (As per the Assistive Technology Act and KTU 2024 syllabus.)

**Four Categories with Examples (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Mobility Aids:** Manual wheelchair (e.g., for paraplegic patients).
2. **Prosthetic Devices:** Trans-tibial prosthesis (below-knee artificial limb).
3. **Sensory Aids:** Cochlear implant (for severe sensorineural hearing loss).
4. **Cognitive Aids:** Smart pillbox with audio reminder (for dementia patients).

> Alternative categories: Orthotic devices, ADL aids, AAC devices, Environmental Control Units (ECU).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**Explain the Functional Independence Measure (FIM). What is the range of possible FIM scores and what does a score of "3" represent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**FIM Definition (1 mark):**
The Functional Independence Measure (FIM) is an 18-item, 7-level ordinal scale assessment tool used to measure the patient's functional status and need for assistance in Activities of Daily Living (ADL).

**Score Range (1 mark):**
Each of the 18 items is scored from 1 to 7. Therefore, the total FIM score ranges from **18 to 126**.

**Meaning of Score 3 (1 mark):**
A score of **3 represents "Moderate Assistance"**, meaning the patient performs **50–75% of the task**, and the helper contributes the remaining **25–50% of the effort**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly write that FIM total ranges from 1 to 7. The **total FIM is the SUM across 18 items, hence 18–126**. The 1–7 scale applies to each individual item. Examiners deduct **½ mark** for this confusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - End Semester Evaluation (ESE), as per KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1]`
> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer ANY ONE of the following (A or B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**"Mobility assistance forms the cornerstone of rehabilitation engineering for individuals with locomotor disabilities."**

**(a)** Classify mobility assistance devices in detail, providing examples, target users, and biomechanical considerations for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the critical tip-over angle for a wheelchair ascending an incline, and explain how anti-tip wheels are designed based on this derivation. A wheelchair has a combined CoG located 12 cm behind the rear axle at a height of 55 cm from the ground. Compute the critical tip-over angle. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Mobility Assistance Devices (7 Marks)**

Mobility assistance devices are classified based on the **level of support provided** and the **user's functional capacity**:

**1. Canes (1 Mark)**
- **Types:** Single-point, quad (four-point), offset, hemi-cane.
- **Target Users:** Mild balance impairment, hemiplegia.
- **Biomechanical Note:** Cane is held in the hand **opposite to the affected leg**, providing a wider base of support and reducing hip abductor moment by approximately 30–40%.

**2. Crutches (1 Mark)**
- **Axillary Crutches:** Used for short-term rehabilitation (fractures, post-surgery). Underarm pads transmit force through the rib cage — improper use can cause brachial plexus injury ("crutch palsy").
- **Lofstrand (Forearm) Crutches:** For long-term users. Force transmitted through forearm cuff; energy-efficient gait.
- **Platform Crutches:** For users who cannot grip (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis).

**3. Walkers (1 Mark)**
- **Standard Walker:** Four legs, no wheels; maximum stability but slow gait (lifting required).
- **Rollator (Two-Wheeled Walker):** Front wheels, rear glides; preferred for elderly.
- **Four-Wheeled Rollator:** Includes seat, brakes, basket.

**4. Manual Wheelchairs (1.5 Marks)**
- **Standard Manual:** Self-propelled via handrims. Typical propulsion speed 0.7–1.2 m/s.
- **Transport Wheelchair:** Pushed by attendant; smaller rear wheels.
- **Sports Wheelchair:** Lightweight, cambered wheels for basketball, racing.
- **Biomechanical Energy:** Manual propulsion consumes 3–8 METs depending on terrain; upper limb overuse injuries (rotator cuff, carpal tunnel) are common in long-term users.

**5. Powered Wheelchairs (1.5 Marks)**
- Driven by electric motors and batteries (typically 24V, 50–75 Ah).
- Controlled via joystick, sip-and-puff, head array, or chin control.
- **Power Wheelchair Categories:** K001–K009 per US Medicare classification.

**6. Smart Wheelchairs (1 Mark)**
- Equipped with sensors (ultrasonic, LiDAR, vision) for obstacle avoidance.
- Used by users with cognitive impairment who cannot safely operate a powered chair.

#### **Part (b) — Derivation and Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**
[As derived in Section 3.1, present the full derivation with the equation:]

$$
\boxed{\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left( \frac{d_{CoG}}{h} \right)}
$$

[State clearly: When the line of action of weight passes through the rear contact point, the wheelchair is at the verge of tipping backward.]

**Numerical Computation (2 Marks):**
Given: $d_{CoG} = 12 \text{ cm} = 0.12 \text{ m}$ and $h = 55 \text{ cm} = 0.55 \text{ m}$.

$$
\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left( \frac{0.12}{0.55} \right) = \arctan(0.2182)
$$

$$
\theta_{tip} \approx 12.32°
$$

**Anti-Tip Wheel Design (1 Mark):**
Anti-tip wheels are small auxiliary wheels mounted on a lever arm extending **rearward** from the wheelchair frame. They engage the ground when the CoG vector moves behind the rear wheel contact, preventing backward tipping. Their placement is at a distance such that the new effective tipping axis is shifted rearward, reducing the critical angle for any given incline.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**"The choice between prosthetic and orthotic assistance is fundamental to rehabilitation engineering practice."**

**(a)** Differentiate between **prosthetic** and **orthotic** devices. Discuss the levels of lower-limb amputation and corresponding prosthetic options. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 60 kg patient with a unilateral below-knee amputation is fitted with a SACH (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel) foot prosthesis. Estimate the energy expenditure during walking at 0.8 m/s and compare it with the metabolic cost for a non-amputee. Discuss the engineering improvements in modern energy-storing feet (ESF). **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Prosthetic vs. Orthotic; Lower Limb Amputation Levels (7 Marks)**

**Prosthetic vs. Orthotic (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Prosthetic | Orthotic |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Replaces a **missing** body part | Supports an **existing** body part |
| **Example** | Artificial leg, prosthetic hand | Knee brace, ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) |
| **Goal** | Substitute absent function | Restore or augment impaired function |
| **Attachment** | Suspended via socket, suction, or strap | Strapped or hinged to limb |
| **Indications** | Amputation, congenital limb deficiency | Weakness, deformity, instability |

**Levels of Lower Limb Amputation (4 Marks — 1 each for major levels):**

1. **Foot Amputation:** Includes toe, transmetatarsal, Lisfranc, Chopart. Prosthesis: Custom foot orthosis or partial foot prosthesis (e.g., silicone toe filler).

2. **Transtibial (Below-Knee, BK):** Amputation between knee and ankle. Prosthesis: PTB (Patellar Tendon Bearing) socket + SACH or ESF foot. Most common level (~60% of all lower-limb amputations).

3. **Transfemoral (Above-Knee, AK):** Amputation between hip and knee. Prosthesis: Quadrilateral or ischial containment socket + knee unit (single-axis, polycentric, hydraulic, microprocessor-controlled like C-Leg) + SACH/ESF foot.

4. **Hip Disarticulation / Hemipelvectomy:** Rare, severe cases. Prosthesis: Canadian-type hip disarticulation prosthesis with locked hip and knee.

#### **Part (b) — Energy Expenditure Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Metabolic Cost Calculation (3 Marks):**
Given: $m = 60 \text{ kg}$, $v = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$.

For a non-amputee of similar mass, walking metabolic rate at 0.8 m/s ≈ **3.5 METs** ≈ **$\approx 240 \text{ W}$** metabolic power.

For a unilateral BK amputee walking at the same speed, the metabolic cost is **~25–40% higher**, i.e., ≈ **4.5 METs** ≈ **$\approx 310 \text{ W}$** metabolic power.

$$
\text{Increase in Energy Cost} = \frac{P_{amputee} - P_{normal}}{P_{normal}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{310 - 240}{240} \times 100\% \approx 29.2\%
$$

**SACH Foot (2 Marks):**
The SACH foot has a rigid keel and a compressible heel wedge (cushion). It absorbs shock at heel strike and simulates plantarflexion through heel compression. However, it stores no kinetic energy during late stance — it is a passive device with limited energy return (~5–10%).

**Energy-Storing Feet (ESF) (2 Marks):**
Modern ESF prostheses (e.g., **Carbon Fiber Flex-Foot, Össur Cheetah**) use carbon composite springs that deform during stance and **recoil** during push-off, returning **>80% of stored elastic energy**. They reduce metabolic cost by 10–15% compared to SACH, increase push-off power, and improve gait symmetry. Engineering design considers **stiffness tuning** based on user weight, activity level, and foot size.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing FIM total range (18–126) with item range (1–7):** Deducts **½ mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always state units (N, m/s, W, °, MET).
> 3. **Not specifying the side for cane use:** Cane goes on the **sound (unaffected) side**, not the weak side. Examiners frequently test this.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps:** The full derivation of $\theta_{tip}$ must include the moment-balance step. Skipping straight to the answer loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up prosthetics and orthotics:** Examiners award **zero marks** if the student conflates these in a 14-mark question.
> 6. **Wrong ADA ramp ratio:** The standard is **1:12**, not 1:10 or 1:15. Deduct 1 mark for misquotation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Recall Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Assistance** = External devices, environmental modifications, and human/technical support enabling disabled persons to perform tasks they could not otherwise do.
- ☐ The **HAAT Model** (Cook & Hussey) integrates **H**uman, **A**ctivity, **A**ssistive Technology, **C**ontext.
- ☐ The **ICF Model** classifies functioning into **Body Functions**, **Activities**, **Participation**.

### Categories of Assistance (Mnemonic: MS-COP-AEC)
- ☐ **M**obility (wheelchair, walker, cane, crutch)
- ☐ **S**ensory (Braille, screen reader, hearing aid, cochlear implant)
- ☐ **C**ognitive (smart reminders, AAC)
- ☐ **O**rthotic (brace, splint, AFO, KAFO)
- ☐ **P**rosthetic (artificial limb: BK, AK)
- ☐ **A**DL (dressing, feeding, bathing aids)
- ☐ **E**nvironmental Control (ECU, smart home)
- ☐ **C**ommunication (AAC, eye gaze, BCI)

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ FIM Total: **18 to 126**
- ☐ Wheelchair tip-over: $\theta_{tip} = \arctan(d_{CoG}/h)$
- ☐ ADA ramp slope: **1:12** (≈ 4.76°)
- ☐ Rolling resistance: $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot W$
- ☐ Ramp ascent force: $F_{ramp} = W\sin\theta + F_{rr}\cos\theta$
- ☐ Muscle efficiency: **$\eta_{muscle} \approx 20$–$25\%$**

### Critical Numerical Values
- ☐ ADA minimum door width: **32 inches (813 mm)**
- ☐ Normal walking speed: **1.4 m/s**
- ☐ Manual WC speed: **0.7–1.2 m/s**
- ☐ Borg RPE scale range: **6 to 20**

### Exam-Winning Distinctions
- ☐ **Prosthetic** = replaces **missing** part; **Orthotic** = supports **existing** part.
- ☐ **Cane** held on **sound side** (opposite to affected leg).
- ☐ **FIM item range 1–7**, **FIM total range 18–126**.
- ☐ **SACH foot** stores minimal energy; **ESF** returns >80% stored energy.
- ☐ **BK amputation** = transtibial (most common); **AK amputation** = transfemoral (requires knee unit).

### Quick Mnemonics
- ☐ **MS-COP-AEC** — Eight categories of assistance.
- ☐ **"Prosthesis replaces what's gone; Orthosis supports what's there."**
- ☐ **"FIM is for Function, not Feeling."** (Don't confuse FIM with pain scales.)
- ☐ **"1:12 — Ramps, 1:20 — Public entries, 32 inches — Doors."** (ADA trio.)

> **Final Exam Tip:** When writing 14-mark answers, **always use a labeled diagram (even a hand-drawn one)** for the HAAT model or the tip-over geometry. KTU examiners award **½ to 1 mark** for well-drawn, labeled diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Technology # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Rehabilitation Technology **Rehabilitation Technology** is the systematic application of engineering principles, scientific knowledge, and technological innovations to design, develop, and deploy devices, equipment, and systems that assist individuals with disabilities in achieving maximum functional independence, social participation, and improved quality of life. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Rehabilitation Engineering is a multi-disciplinary field that integrates *biomedical engineering*, *mechanical design*, *electronics*, *computer science*, and *clinical rehabilitation sciences* to produce **Assistive Technology (AT)** solutions. **Assistive Technology (AT)** — as defined by the *Assistive Technology Act* and adopted in KTU modules — refers to any item, piece of equipment, software program, or product system that is used to **increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities** of persons with disabilities. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Think of rehabilitation technology like a **bridge**: > A person with a disability is standing on one side of a river (a functional limitation), and the activities of daily life lie on the other side. Rehabilitation technology acts as the **bridge** — it can be a simple wooden plank (a walking cane) or a complex steel suspension bridge (a powered wheelchair with neural control). The *engineer* doesn't change the person; instead, the engineer **builds a path** between their current ability and the goal activity. | Generic Engineering | Rehabilitation Engineering | |---|---| | Builds for the average user | Builds for a **spectrum** of unique users | | Optimization for performance | Optimization for **function + safety** | | Mass production | Often **customized or personalized** | | Cost-driven decisions | **Need-driven** + ethical decisions | ## 1.3 Key Terminology — WHO Framework (ICF) The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** is the standard WHO framework adopted by KTU for understanding disability technology needs. > [!NOTE] > **Three Core ICF Terms (Mandatory for KTU 2-Mark & 14-Mark Answers):** - **Impairment:** A *loss or abnormality* in body structure or physiological function (e.g., amputation of a limb, loss of vision). - **Activity Limitation:** Difficulties an individual may have in *executing tasks or actions* (e.g., cannot walk independently, cannot write). - **Participation Restriction:** Problems an individual may experience in *involvement in life situations* (e.g., cannot attend school, cannot access workplace). ## 1.4 Historical Evolution (KTU Expected Chronology) | Era | Milestone | |---|---| | **Pre-1900s** | Crude wooden prosthetics, basic wheelchairs | | **1920s** | First motorized wheelchair (London) | | **1940s** | Modern prosthetics emerged post-WWII (Veterans' need) | | **1960s** | Microprocessor-controlled devices conceptualized | | **1980s** | Personal computers used for AAC (Augmentative & Alternative Communication) | | **1990s** | Digital hearing aids, myoelectric arms | | **2000s** | Powered exoskeletons, BCI prototypes | | **2010s–Present** | 3D-printed prosthetics, AI-based assistive devices, smart wheelchairs | ## 1.5 Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) Rehabilitation technology is developed and delivered by a **multidisciplinary team**: - **Rehabilitation Engineer** — designs and modifies devices - **Physiatrist** (Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation doctor) - **Physiotherapist / Occupational Therapist** - **Prosthetist / Orthotist** - **Speech-Language Pathologist** - **User / Caregiver** — the most important member # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Technology (Cook & Hussey Model) The **Cook & Hussey Model of Assistive Technology** is a foundational KTU-recommended framework. Technology for rehabilitation is classified into **three hierarchical levels**: 1. **No-Tech / Low-Tech Solutions** — Human assistance, simple modifications, paper-based tools 2. **Mid-Tech Solutions** — Mechanical, simple electronic devices (e.g., manual wheelchair, magnifying glass) 3. **High-Tech Solutions** — Computerized, microprocessor-controlled, AI-integrated devices (e.g., powered wheelchair, smart prosthetics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule:** *Always start with the lowest level of technology that meets the user's need (Least Restrictive Technology Principle).* This minimizes cost, complexity, and training burden. ## 2.2 The HAAT Model (Human – Activity – Assistive Technology) The **HAAT Model**, developed by *Albert Cook and Susan Hussey*, is the most important conceptual model in KTU Module 1. The model states that the success of any assistive technology depends on the **dynamic interaction of four components**: - **H (Human):** The user with their skills, abilities, and limitations - **A (Activity):** The task or goal to be accomplished (e.g., eating, writing) - **AT (Assistive Technology):** The device or system - **C (Context):** The physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment > The system's effectiveness is governed by the equation: > **Performance = f(H, A, AT, C)** ## 2.3 Models of Disability (Critical for KTU) | Model | Core Idea | Technology Implication | |---|---|---| | **Medical Model** | Disability is a *problem of the individual* caused by disease | Technology aims to **fix** the individual (e.g., prosthesis) | | **Social Model** | Disability is *caused by societal barriers* | Technology aims to **remove environmental barriers** (e.g., ramps, screen readers) | | **Biopsychosocial Model (ICF)** | Disability is a *complex interaction* of health condition + environmental + personal factors | Technology is **context-aware** and user-centered | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A 14-mark question will likely ask you to **compare and critique** these three models. Memorize definitions + give one technology example for each. ## 2.4 ISO 9999 Classification of Assistive Products The **ISO 9999:2022** standard classifies assistive products into **10 major classes**: 1. Aids for **personal medical treatment** 2. Aids for **training in skills** 3. Aids for **personal mobility** 4. Aids for **household activities** 5. Aids for **communication and information** 6. Aids for **handling objects and devices** 7. Aids for **environmental improvement** 8. Aids for **employment and vocational training** 9. Aids for **recreation and leisure** 10. Aids for **managing incontinence / personal care** ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes all quantitative formulas needed in Module 1 numerical/problem-type questions. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | FIM (Functional Independence Measure) Total Score | $FIM_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{18} FIM_i$ | 18 items × (1 to 7 scale) = **18–126** | Quantifies disability level | | 2 | Barthel Index | $BI = \sum_{j=1}^{10} w_j \cdot s_j$ | $w_j$ = weight, $s_j$ = score (0, 5, 10, 15) | Max = **100** (full independence) | | 3 | Cost-Effectiveness Ratio | $CER = \frac{C_{device} + C_{training}}{QALY_{gained}}$ | Currency per **QALY** | Decision-making for procurement | | 4 | Device Abandonment Rate | $DAR = \frac{N_{abandoned}}{N_{issued}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Quality of AT service | | 5 | Device Acceptance Rate | $DAR_{acc} = \frac{N_{used \ge 6\,mo}}{N_{issued}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Technology success metric | | 6 | Mean Device Usage Time | $\bar{T} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} t_k$ | Hours / Days | Sustainability indicator | | 7 | Wheelchair Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{P_{output}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Performance of propulsion | | 8 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Sensors) | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) | Quality of bioelectric signals | | 9 | Joint Range of Motion (ROM) | $ROM = \theta_{max} - \theta_{min}$ | **Degrees (°)** | Prosthetic design | | 10 | Ergonomic Fit Score | $EFS = 1 - \frac{\vert L_{user} - L_{device} \vert}{L_{device}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Customization quality | > **Note on Units:** Always state **units** in KTU numerical answers. Marks are deducted for "dimensionless numerical answers without labels". ## 2.6 Universal Design Principles (Ron Mace, 1997) The **7 Principles of Universal Design** that any rehabilitation engineer should follow: 1. Equitable Use 2. Flexibility in Use 3. Simple and Intuitive 4. Perceptible Information 5. Tolerance for Error 6. Low Physical Effort 7. Size and Space for Approach and Use # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the HAAT System Performance Function The HAAT model states that assistive technology performance is a function of four interacting components. Let us formalize this mathematically. ### Step 1: Define each component as a measurable vector We model each component as a vector of measurable features: $$\vec{H} = (h_1, h_2, h_3, \ldots, h_p)$$ where $h_i$ = cognitive, motor, sensory, and psychosocial attributes of the human user. $$\vec{A} = (a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_q)$$ where $a_j$ = sub-tasks composing the activity (e.g., grasping, lifting, releasing). $$\vec{AT} = (at_1, at_2, at_3, \ldots, at_r)$$ where $at_k$ = device parameters (mass, power, control gain, ergonomics). $$\vec{C} = (c_1, c_2, c_3, \ldots, c_s)$$ where $c_m$ = context parameters (temperature, lighting, terrain, social support). ### Step 2: Define the Performance Function The overall system performance is given by: $$P = f(\vec{H}, \vec{A}, \vec{AT}, \vec{C})$$ ### Step 3: Linear Approximation for Engineering Analysis For tractable analysis, a first-order (linear) approximation is used: $$P \approx \alpha_0 + \sum_{i} \alpha_i h_i + \sum_{j} \beta_j a_j + \sum_{k} \gamma_k \, at_k + \sum_{m} \delta_m c_m$$ where $\alpha_0, \alpha_i, \beta_j, \gamma_k, \delta_m$ are empirically determined weight coefficients. ### Step 4: Interpretation in KTU Examination Context - If $P$ is **maximized**, the device-human-context system is optimally matched. - A sudden drop in $P$ with respect to any single variable signals a **mismatch**. - Engineers can use this formulation to **redesign** $\vec{AT}$ for fixed $\vec{H}$ and $\vec{A}$. ## 3.2 Derivation of the FIM (Functional Independence Measure) Total The FIM is a standard clinical scoring tool in rehabilitation. ### Step 1: FIM Levels FIM uses a **7-point ordinal scale** for each of 18 activities: | Score | Level | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 7 | Complete Independence | No helper, in timely, safe manner | | 6 | Modified Independence | Uses a device, no helper | | 5 | Supervision | Helper nearby, no physical contact | | 4 | Minimal Assistance | Helper touches subject ≤ 25% effort | | 3 | Moderate Assistance | Helper touches subject ≤ 50% effort | | 2 | Maximal Assistance | Helper touches subject ≤ 75% effort | | 1 | Total Assistance | Helper touches subject > 75% effort | ### Step 2: Total FIM Score $$FIM_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{18} FIM_i$$ ### Step 3: Numerical Worked Example A patient has FIM scores: $(5, 6, 7, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7, 6, 4, 5, 6, 7, 5, 6, 4, 3, 5)$ Sum the values: $$\begin{aligned} FIM_{total} &= 5+6+7+4+3+6+5+7+6+4+5+6+7+5+6+4+3+5 \\ &= 94 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Clinical Interpretation - **126** → Complete independence - **108–125** → Modified independence - **72–107** → Moderate assistance - **36–71** → Severe assistance - **18–35** → Total assistance Our patient's score of **94** indicates **Moderate Assistance** is required — engineer should design an assistive device that targets the sub-activities where $FIM_i \le 4$. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Wheelchair Propulsion Efficiency ### Step 1: Define Power $$P_{input} = F_{push} \cdot v_{hand}$$ where $F_{push}$ is the force applied by hand (N) and $v_{hand}$ is hand velocity (m/s). $$P_{output} = F_{rolling} \cdot v_{chair} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v_{chair}$$ where $\mu_r$ is the rolling resistance coefficient, $m$ is total mass (user + chair), and $g = 9.81\,m/s^2$. ### Step 2: Efficiency $$\eta = \frac{P_{output}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$$ ### Step 3: Numerical Example A 70 kg person in a 12 kg wheelchair (m = 82 kg), $\mu_r = 0.012$, $v_{chair} = 1.2\,m/s$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{output} &= 0.012 \times 82 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \\ &= 11.59\,W \end{aligned}$$ If $F_{push} = 18\,N$ and $v_{hand} = 1.05\,m/s$: $$\begin{aligned} P_{input} &= 18 \times 1.05 = 18.9\,W \\ \eta &= \frac{11.59}{18.9} \times 100\% \\ &= 61.32\% \end{aligned}$$ This 61.32% efficiency quantifies how much of the user's metabolic energy reaches the chair as forward motion — a key engineering design metric. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technology Selection Decision Tree | User Need | Recommended Tech Level | Engineering Action | |---|---|---| | Mild visual impairment | Low-tech (magnifier) | Optical design, ergonomic fit | | Severe motor impairment (quadriplegia) | High-tech (powered WC + sip-and-puff) | Mechatronics, sensor integration | | Communication limitation (ALS) | Mid-tech (AAC board) → High-tech (eye-tracker) | Signal processing, UI design | | Lower limb amputation | Mid-tech (prosthesis with socket) | Biomechanics, gait analysis | | Cognitive disability | Low-tech (picture schedules) | UX design, HCI | ## 3.5 Derivation of Cost-Effectiveness for AT Procurement ### Step 1: Define QALY A **Quality-Adjusted Life Year (QALY)** combines length and quality of life: $$QALY = \sum_{y=1}^{Y} u(y)$$ where $u(y)$ is the utility weight (0 to 1) of life in year $y$. ### Step 2: Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (ICER) $$ICER = \frac{C_{AT} - C_{no\,AT}}{QALY_{AT} - QALY_{no\,AT}}$$ ### Step 3: Example - Without device: utility = 0.5 for 5 years → QALY = 2.5 - With device: utility = 0.75 for 5 years → QALY = 3.75 - Device cost = ₹5,00,000 $$ICER = \frac{5,00,000 - 0}{(3.75 - 2.5) \times 1} = \frac{5,00,000}{1.25} = \text{₹ } 4,00,000 \text{ per QALY}$$ If the threshold is ₹5,00,000 per QALY (WHO-CHOICE), this device is **cost-effective** — a standard KTU interpretation. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HAAT Model — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR H[Human
User Profile] --> P[Performance
Outcome] A[Activity
Task Goal] --> P AT[Assistive Technology
Device] --> P C[Context
Environment] --> P P --> R[Rehabilitation
Outcome] R -.Feedback.-> H R -.Feedback.-> AT R -.Feedback.-> C ``` ## 4.2 ICF Framework — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TB HC[Health Condition] --> B[Body Functions
and Structures] HC --> A[Activity] HC --> PA[Participation] B --> A A --> PA EF[Environmental Factors] -.Modulates.-> B EF -.Modulates.-> A EF -.Modulates.-> PA PF[Personal Factors] -.Modulates.-> B PF -.Modulates.-> A PF -.Modulates.-> PA ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Technology Classification ```mermaid flowchart TB AT[Assistive Technology Pyramid] --> LT[Low Tech] AT --> MT[Mid Tech] AT --> HT[High Tech] LT --> L1[Cane, Crutches] LT --> L2[Manual Wheelchair] LT --> L3[Reach Extender] MT --> M1[Myoelectric Arm] MT --> M2[Power Wheelchair] MT --> M3[Digital Hearing Aid] HT --> H1[BCI Controlled Cursor] HT --> H2[Smart Prosthetic with AI] HT --> H3[Powered Exoskeleton] ``` ## 4.4 Multidisciplinary Team Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB U[User and Caregiver] --> ENG[Rehabilitation Engineer] ENG --> PHY[Physiatrist] ENG --> PT[Physiotherapist] ENG --> OT[Occupational Therapist] ENG --> PO[Prosthetist Orthotist] ENG --> SLP[Speech Language Pathologist] ENG --> TECH[Technician] PHY --> DEV[Assistive Device] PT --> DEV OT --> DEV PO --> DEV SLP --> DEV TECH --> DEV DEV --> U ``` ## 4.5 Universal Design — Engineering Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Identify Need] --> B[User Centred Design] B --> C[Prototype v1] C --> D[Usability Testing] D --> E{Meets UD Criteria?} E -- No --> F[Redesign] F --> C E -- Yes --> G[Clinical Trial] G --> H{Outcome Validated?} H -- No --> F H -- Yes --> I[Deployment and Training] I --> J[Follow up and Re assessment] ``` ## 4.6 Technology Service Delivery Model (KTU Expectation) ```mermaid flowchart TB REF[Referral] --> AS[Assessment by MDT] AS --> SP[Specification of AT] SP --> SL[Selection of Device] SL --> TR[Training of User] TR --> FU[Follow up and Maintenance] FU --> MON[Outcome Monitoring] MON --> MOD[Modification or Replacement] MOD -.If needed.-> SL ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Assistive Technology as per the WHO framework. List any two classifications of assistive technology.** **Model Answer:** Assistive Technology (AT) is an umbrella term covering *any device, equipment, software, or product system* that is used by persons with disabilities to perform functions that they would otherwise be unable to do, or to improve the ease and safety with which those functions are performed. As per the WHO GATE initiative, AT aims to bridge the gap between a person's functional limitation and their desired participation in daily activities. **Two classifications:** - **By technological complexity** — Low-tech, Mid-tech, High-tech - **By ISO 9999** — 10 major classes (mobility, communication, household, etc.) *(Word count: ~80 — ideal for a 3-mark answer)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Impairment, Disability, and Handicap with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Term | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Impairment** | Loss or abnormality of body structure/function | Loss of an arm (amputation) | | **Disability** | Restriction in performing a task | Inability to lift objects with the missing arm | | **Handicap** | Disadvantage in social role | Cannot participate in a cricket match (as defined in the older ICIDH model) | In the modern **ICF framework**, "handicap" has been replaced by **"participation restriction"** to remove negative connotations. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **HAAT Model** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how the four components interact to determine assistive technology outcomes. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three models of disability** (Medical, Social, Biopsychosocial). For each model, give one example of a corresponding rehabilitation technology. ### Model Answer (a) The **HAAT Model** was proposed by Cook and Hussey (1995) as a systems framework for understanding and designing assistive technology. It identifies four interacting components that together determine the success of any AT intervention. **The four components are:** 1. **Human (H):** The person using the AT, characterized by their motor, sensory, cognitive, and psychosocial abilities. For a child with cerebral palsy, this includes muscle tone, joint range, vision, hearing, attention span, and motivation. 2. **Activity (A):** The specific goal or task to be accomplished — e.g., writing, eating, walking, communicating. Activities are broken down into sub-tasks during engineering analysis. 3. **Assistive Technology (AT):** The device itself — its hardware, software, controls, and ergonomics. Examples: powered wheelchair, myoelectric hand, screen reader. 4. **Context (C):** The physical, social, cultural, and institutional setting in which the AT is used. A wheelchair designed for indoor use may fail in a rural muddy terrain. The **Performance** of the system is given by: $$P = f(H, A, AT, C)$$ The model is **dynamic**: a change in one component forces re-evaluation of others. For example, a growing child (H) requires periodic redesign of the prosthetic (AT) and retraining in the activity. **Diagram:** ``` ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ ▼ │ ┌─────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌──────┐ │ │ Human │───▶│Activity│◀───│ AT │ │ └────┬────┘ └────────┘ └──┬───┘ │ │ │ │ └──────► Context ◀─────────┘ │ │ │ └──────────► Performance ─────┘ ``` ### Model Answer (b) **1. Medical Model (Rehabilitation perspective of the 20th century)** Disability is viewed as a *personal tragedy* caused by disease, trauma, or health condition. The focus is on **curing or correcting** the individual through medical intervention and devices. - *Technology example:* Prosthetic limb for an amputee — aims to "restore" function. **2. Social Model (Emerging in the 1970s, UK disability movement)** Disability is not the problem of the person; it is caused by **society's failure to remove barriers** (physical, attitudinal, institutional). The person is *disabled by society*, not by their body. - *Technology example:* Ramp + lift at a public building, screen-reader software for visually impaired — these change the **environment**, not the person. **3. Biopsychosocial Model (WHO ICF, 2001)** Disability is a *complex interaction* between health condition, body function, activity, participation, and contextual factors. It integrates the strengths of both the medical and social models. - *Technology example:* A smart wheelchair that adapts to the user's fatigue level (medical need) and the indoor/outdoor terrain (social-environmental context), with cloud-based usage analytics for therapists (context-aware service delivery). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the three models as overlapping descriptions. The examiner expects **clear differentiation** with **distinct technology examples**. Marks are lost when students repeat the same example across models. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **ISO 9999 classification** of assistive products. List any **6 major classes** with two examples each. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Universal Design Process** as applied to a rehabilitation device of your choice. State the **7 Principles of Universal Design**. ### Model Answer (a) **ISO 9999:2022 — International Classification of Assistive Products** is a comprehensive standard maintained by the International Organization for Standardization. It classifies assistive products into **10 major classes** based on their **functional purpose**, not on the user group. **Six Major Classes with Examples:** | Class | Title | Example 1 | Example 2 | |---|---|---|---| | 04 | Aids for personal medical treatment | Insulin pen | Dialysis machine | | 05 | Aids for training in skills | Braille learning kit | Gait trainer | | 06 | Aids for personal mobility | Manual wheelchair | Powered scooter | | 12 | Aids for household activities | Adaptive cutlery | Wheelchair-accessible stove | | 15 | Aids for communication & information | AAC device | Screen reader software | | 18 | Aids for home modification | Ramps | Grab bars | **Engineering Significance:** ISO 9999 allows engineers, clinicians, and policy makers to use a **common language** when specifying, procuring, and reimbursing assistive products. ### Model Answer (b) **Universal Design (UD)** is the design of products and environments in such a way that they can be used by *all people, to the greatest extent possible, without the need for adaptation or specialized design.* **The 7 Principles of Universal Design (Ron Mace, 1997):** 1. **Equitable Use** — Useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities 2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of preferences and abilities 3. **Simple and Intuitive Use** — Easy to understand, regardless of experience or knowledge 4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates necessary information effectively 5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards from accidental or unintended actions 6. **Low Physical Effort** — Used efficiently and comfortably with minimum fatigue 7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Appropriate size and space for approach, reach, and use **Block Diagram — Universal Design Process for a Smart Walking Cane:** ``` [Identify User Group: Elderly, Visually Impaired, Hemiplegic] │ ▼ [Empathize with Users through Interviews and Observation] │ ▼ [Define Functional Requirements: Stability, Feedback, Alertness] │ ▼ [Generate Design Concepts] │ ▼ [Build Prototype with Obstacle Sensors and Ergonomic Grip] │ ▼ [Usability Testing with Diverse User Groups] │ ▼ [Iterate Design Based on Feedback] │ ▼ [Final Universal Product Released] │ ▼ [Periodic Re-assessment and Improvement] ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfall:** Students often *list* the 7 principles without explaining the design *process*. A 7-mark answer requires both the **principles** AND the **engineering process workflow** with a chosen device. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - **Rehabilitation Engineering** = Application of engineering to assist people with disabilities. - **AT (Assistive Technology)** definition must be quoted from the WHO/AT Act — partial marks for paraphrasing only. - **ICF Three Terms** — Impairment → Activity Limitation → Participation Restriction. - **Cook & Hussey HAAT Model** — Human, Activity, AT, Context — performance is a function of all four. - **Three Models of Disability** — Medical, Social, Biopsychosocial — each needs a *distinct* technology example. - **ISO 9999:2022** classifies AT into **10 major classes**; memorize 6 for 14-mark questions. - **Universal Design = 7 Principles** (Ron Mace, 1997). - **Quantitative Tools** — FIM (18–126), Barthel Index (0–100), QALY, ICER, η% efficiency. - **Least Restrictive Technology Principle** — start with low-tech before escalating. - **Multidisciplinary Team** — Engineer is *one* member among many; the **user is the most important**. - **Universal Design ≠ Accessibility** — UD is *inclusive from the start*; accessibility is *retro-fit*. - **Exam Tip:** When asked to "discuss", always include a *definition + comparison + example + diagram*. Diagrams earn at least 2 marks. - **Numerical Rule:** Always write **units** and **formula substitution** in numerical answers.

topics.title: and Environment (PHAATE) Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** and Environment (PHAATE) Model # MODULE 1 — Person, Human Activity, Assistive Technology, Tools, and Environment (PHAATE) Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **PHAATE Model** is a holistic, person-centered conceptual framework used in Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (AT) service delivery. It is an acronym that systematically integrates five interdependent domains: > [!IMPORTANT] > **PHAATE = Person + Human Activity + Assistive Technology + Tools + Environment** The model was developed to ensure that assistive technology solutions are not prescribed in isolation, but rather designed after evaluating the **dynamic interaction** between the user, their functional goals, the devices, the tools used to perform activities, and the physical-social context in which the activities occur. The framework is formally articulated in the literature on **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT)** and extended to **PHAATE** to incorporate the contextual environmental factors that influence AT adoption, abandonment, or success. ### 1.2 Component-Wise Expansion | Letter | Component | Definition | |--------|-----------|------------| | **P** | **Person** | The individual with impairment, disability, or functional limitation. Includes physical, cognitive, sensory, and emotional attributes. | | **HA** | **Human Activity** | The desired functional task or daily living activity the person wishes to perform (e.g., eating, walking, communicating). | | **A** | **Assistive Technology** | Any device, equipment, or system that helps the person perform the activity (wheelchair, hearing aid, prosthetic limb). | | **T** | **Tools** | The instruments, software, or interface mechanisms used to operate the AT (joystick, switch, software app). | | **E** | **Environment** | The physical, social, cultural, institutional, and attitudinal context in which the activity is performed. | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee Shop" Example** > > Imagine a person with limited hand mobility (the **Person**) who wants to drink coffee (**Human Activity**). They use a specially designed weighted cup with a lid and large handle (**Assistive Technology**). To hold it, they use an adaptive grip aid (**Tools**). Now, consider whether they can use this cup in a small, cramped café booth versus a spacious home kitchen, or whether cultural attitudes at the café make them feel comfortable using it (**Environment**). > > If the AT is technically perfect but the environment is hostile or inaccessible, the device **fails its purpose**. PHAATE exists precisely to capture this multi-dimensional reality. ### 1.4 Why PHAATE Matters in KTU Syllabus - **Person-First Philosophy**: Aligns with the **United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD)**. - **Outcome-Based Design**: Matches KTU 2024's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) mandate to assess devices against real functional outcomes rather than technical specs alone. - **Interdisciplinary Bridge**: Connects **biomedical engineering**, **occupational therapy**, **clinical rehabilitation**, and **universal design**. ### 1.5 Historical Lineage The model evolved through three intellectual milestones: 1. **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** — proposed by **Cook and Hussey**, foundational text in AT. 2. **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model** — by **Marcia Scherer**, emphasizing person-AT fit. 3. **PHAATE Extension** — added **Environment** and **Tools** explicitly to address real-world implementation gaps observed in clinical practice. ### 1.6 KTU Cognitive Level Tagging | Bloom's Level | Application to PHAATE | |---------------|------------------------| | **Remember** | List the 5 components of PHAATE | | **Understand** | Explain the interaction between Person and Environment | | **Apply** | Use PHAATE to design an AT solution for a given disability | | **Analyze** | Differentiate HAAT vs. PHAATE | | **Evaluate** | Justify AT selection using PHAATE for a case study | | **Create** | Develop a new AT intervention plan integrating all 5 domains | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ## 2.1 Structural Architecture of the PHAATE Model The PHAATE model is **not linear** — it is a **multi-axial interactive system**. Each component exerts influence on every other, creating a **feedback-rich ecosystem**. A change in any one component (e.g., progressive disease affecting the Person) cascades through all other domains. ### 2.2 The Five Domains — Exhaustive Breakdown #### **P — Person Domain** The Person domain encompasses all intrinsic characteristics of the user: - **Physical attributes**: Muscle strength, joint range of motion (ROM), cardiopulmonary endurance, posture. - **Sensory attributes**: Visual acuity, auditory thresholds, proprioception, tactile sensitivity. - **Cognitive attributes**: Memory, executive function, learning capacity, problem-solving. - **Psychosocial attributes**: Motivation, self-efficacy, cultural background, family support, socioeconomic status. - **Functional status**: Often quantified using standardized scales such as the **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** — scored from **18 (total dependence)** to **126 (complete independence)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Metric — FIM Score** > > $$\text{FIM Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{18} \text{Motor Subscore}_i + \sum_{j=1}^{5} \text{Cognitive Subscore}_j$$ > > Each item is rated on a **7-point ordinal scale** (1 = Total Assistance, 7 = Complete Independence). Maximum = **126**. #### **HA — Human Activity Domain** Activities are classified using the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** by the WHO: | ICF Category | Examples | Relevance to PHAATE | |--------------|----------|---------------------| | **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** | Bathing, dressing, toileting, feeding | Core rehab targets | | **Instrumental ADL (IADL)** | Cooking, shopping, managing finances | Higher-order independence | | **Work & Productivity** | Employment, education | Vocational rehab | | **Leisure & Social Participation** | Sports, hobbies, community engagement | Quality of life | | **Communication** | Speaking, writing, using phone | Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) | #### **A — Assistive Technology Domain** Classified by the **ISO 9999:2022** standard into hierarchical categories: 1. **Assistive products for personal mobility** (wheelchairs, walkers, prosthetic legs). 2. **Assistive products for personal care and safety** (bath chairs, grab rails). 3. **Assistive products for communication and information** (AAC devices, screen readers). 4. **Assistive products for manipulation** (reachers, adapted cutlery). 5. **Assistive products for environmental improvement** (ramps, smart home controls). #### **T — Tools Domain** Tools are the **interface layer** between the Person and the AT. They include: - **Input interfaces**: Switches, joysticks, sip-and-puff controls, eye-trackers, brain-computer interfaces (BCI). - **Output interfaces**: Displays, speakers, haptic feedback motors, robotic actuators. - **Software tools**: Operating system accessibility features (screen magnification, voice recognition), AT apps. - **Fabrication tools**: 3D printers, CNC machines — relevant for custom AT prototyping in engineering curricula. #### **E — Environment Domain** The most often overlooked yet most critical domain. Classified per the **ICF Environmental Factors**: | Sub-domain | Engineering Examples | |------------|----------------------| | **Physical environment** | Ramps, doorway widths (≥ **900 mm** for wheelchair access per ADA), tactile flooring | | **Technological environment** | Power infrastructure, network connectivity for IoT-AT devices | | **Social environment** | Family support, peer networks, disability awareness | | **Institutional environment** | Healthcare policies, insurance coverage, AT funding schemes (e.g., **ADIP scheme** in India) | | **Cultural environment** | Stigma, gender norms, religious considerations | | **Attitudinal environment** | Public perception, employer bias | ### 2.3 The PHAATE Interaction Matrix Each pair of components interacts. The 10 unique pairwise interactions are: | Interaction Pair | Description | Engineering Implication | |------------------|-------------|--------------------------| | **Person ↔ Activity** | What the person *wants/wants* to do | Goal-setting in rehab plan | | **Person ↔ AT** | Can the person physically/cognitively operate the device? | Ergonomic design, training needs | | **Person ↔ Tools** | User interface design, motor control requirements | HCI, control engineering | | **Person ↔ Environment** | Accessibility, mobility, social acceptance | Universal design | | **Activity ↔ AT** | Does the AT actually enable the *specific* activity? | Functional task analysis | | **Activity ↔ Tools** | Are the tools appropriate for the activity context? | Activity-specific customization | | **Activity ↔ Environment** | Where will the activity realistically occur? | Contextual assessment | | **AT ↔ Tools** | Compatibility of hardware/software interfaces | Systems integration | | **AT ↔ Environment** | Can the AT survive the environment (waterproof, dustproof)? | IP rating (e.g., **IP54**, **IP67**) | | **Tools ↔ Environment** | Does the environment support the tool's infrastructure? | Power, connectivity | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Application | |---|------------------|------------|-------------| | 1 | **FIM Total Score** | $\text{FIM} = \sum_{i=1}^{18} m_i + \sum_{j=1}^{5} c_j$ | Functional independence assessment | | 2 | **Barthel Index** | $\text{BI} = \sum_{k=1}^{10} s_k$ where $s_k \in \{0,5,10,15\}$ | ADL scoring (0–100) | | 3 | **AT Adoption Rate** | $\text{Adoption \%} = \dfrac{N_{\text{used}}}{N_{\text{prescribed}}} \times 100$ | Measure of PHAATE effectiveness | | 4 | **Wheelchair Access Width** | $W_{\text{door}} \geq 900 \text{ mm}$ | Environmental compliance | | 5 | **Ramp Slope Ratio** | $\dfrac{H}{L} \leq \dfrac{1}{12}$ (ADA) | Accessibility engineering | | 6 | **Heart Rate Reserve (HRR)** | $\text{HRR} = \text{HR}_{\max} - \text{HR}_{\text{rest}}$ | Physical capacity in Person domain | | 7 | **Target Heart Rate** | $\text{THR} = \text{HR}_{\text{rest}} + k \cdot \text{HRR}$, $k \in [0.4, 0.85]$ | Exercise prescription | | 8 | **Range of Motion (ROM)** | $\theta_{\text{active}}, \theta_{\text{passive}}$ in degrees | Joint mobility assessment | | 9 | **IP Rating Format** | $\text{IPXY}$ (X = solids, Y = liquids) | Environmental protection of AT | | 10 | **Cost-Effectiveness Ratio** | $\text{CER} = \dfrac{C_{\text{AT}}}{\text{QALY gained}}$ | Health economics in rehab | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip**: In Part B answers, always reference at least **one standardized scale** (FIM, Barthel, ICF) to demonstrate person-domain assessment rigor. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The PHAATE framework is the **de facto design philosophy** in: - **Smart wheelchair design** (e.g., powered wheelchairs with intelligent obstacle avoidance). - **Prosthetic limb engineering** (myoelectric arms with multi-modal feedback). - **AAC device development** (text-to-speech apps for speech-impaired users). - **Smart home automation** for elderly and disabled populations. - **Universal Design** projects (inclusive playgrounds, accessible public transport). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Application Procedure Since the PHAATE model is conceptual-engineering rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" below is an **analytical workflow** that translates theory into engineering practice — the format KTU board examiners reward in 14-mark answers. ## 3.1 The PHAATE Assessment Algorithm — Step-by-Step This is the systematic procedure a rehabilitation engineer follows when evaluating any AT need: ### **Step 1: Person Domain Assessment** Gather intrinsic user data. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Person Profile} = \{ &\text{Age}, \text{Gender}, \text{Diagnosis}, \\ &\text{ROM}_{\text{all joints}}, \text{MMT scores}, \\ &\text{Cognitive MMSE score}, \\ &\text{Visual Acuity (LogMAR)}, \\ &\text{Hearing Threshold (dB HL)}, \\ &\text{Psychosocial Status (PHQ-9 score)}\} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic**: A complete baseline is mandatory before any AT prescription. Without knowing the person's residual capacities, no interface design is feasible. ### **Step 2: Human Activity Domain Identification** Conduct a **functional task analysis** using the ICF framework. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Activity Set} = \{ A_1, A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_n \} \end{aligned} $$ Each activity $A_i$ is decomposed into micro-tasks: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{\text{dressing}} = \{ &\text{Reach shirt}, \text{Grasp shirt}, \text{Pull over head}, \\ &\text{Fasten buttons}, \text{Adjust fit}\} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic**: Each micro-task has its own sensory, motor, and cognitive demands. AT prescription must target *specific* failed micro-tasks. ### **Step 3: Assistive Technology Matching** Apply the **Matching Person and Technology (MPT)** framework to select candidate AT: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AT Candidate Set} = \{ \text{AT}_1, \text{AT}_2, \ldots, \text{AT}_k \} \end{aligned} $$ For each candidate, compute a **Person-Technology Fit Score (PTFS)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PTFS}_j = w_1 \cdot F_{\text{physical}} + w_2 \cdot F_{\text{cognitive}} + w_3 \cdot F_{\text{sensory}} + w_4 \cdot F_{\text{psychosocial}} \end{aligned} $$ where the weights satisfy: $$ \begin{aligned} w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic**: Different users prioritize different AT attributes. A cognitively impaired user may prioritize simplicity ($w_2 \uparrow$), while a physically impaired but cognitively intact user prioritizes motor performance ($w_1 \uparrow$). ### **Step 4: Tools Domain Configuration** Specify the human-machine interface (HMI) components: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tool Set} = \{ \text{Input device}, \text{Output device}, \text{Software} \} \end{aligned} $$ Apply the **Fitts's Law** for target selection accuracy in interface design: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{movement}} = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) \end{aligned} $$ where $D$ is distance to target, $W$ is target width, $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants. **Logic**: Tools must be ergonomically matched to the person's residual motor capacity. Fitts's Law quantifies the speed-accuracy tradeoff. ### **Step 5: Environment Domain Audit** Score the environmental factors using the **Craig Hospital Inventory of Environmental Factors (CHIEF)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CHIEF Total} = \sum_{m=1}^{25} (F_m \times M_m) \end{aligned} $$ where $F_m$ is frequency (0–4) and $M_m$ is magnitude of barrier (0–2) for each of 25 items. **Logic**: A high CHIEF score indicates environmental barriers that may neutralize even the best AT prescription. Engineers must address these via **universal design interventions**. ### **Step 6: Integration & Iterative Refinement** The full PHAATE assessment produces a **Multidimensional AT Prescription**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PHAATE Output} = f(P, HA, A, T, E) \end{aligned} $$ After trial use, compute the **AT Adoption Success Rate**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Success Rate} = \dfrac{N_{\text{AT adopted after 6 months}}}{N_{\text{AT prescribed}}} \times 100\% \end{aligned} $$ Industry benchmark: Successful long-term adoption typically falls between **60%–80%**. Lower rates indicate failures in one or more PHAATE domains. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Illustrative Case Walkthrough **Case**: A 35-year-old male with **C6 spinal cord injury** (quadriplegia, partial hand function) wishes to return to office work. | PHAATE Step | Assessment Output | |-------------|-------------------| | **Person** | Motor: weak grip (MMT grade 3/5), intact cognition (MMSE 28/30), partial sensation below injury level | | **Activity** | Computer-based office work (writing emails, spreadsheets, presentations) | | **AT** | Voice recognition software + adaptive keyboard + mouth-operated mouse | | **Tools** | USB sip-and-puff controller; Dragon NaturallySpeaking software; large-key silicone overlay | | **Environment** | Office has stairs (barrier), no accessible toilet (barrier), supportive employer (facilitator) | **PHAATE Outcome**: Successful return to work. Environment was later modified with a **lift and ramp** ($H/L = 1/15$, compliant with ADA), addressing the environmental barrier post-prescription. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PHAATE Core Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **central role of the Person** surrounded by the four interaction domains, with explicit arrows showing bi-directional feedback: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHAATE_CORE["PHAATE Interaction Ecosystem"] PERSON["P - Person
User with disability
Physical, Cognitive,
Psychosocial profile"] ACTIVITY["HA - Human Activity
ADL, IADL, Work,
Leisure, Communication"] ATECH["A - Assistive Technology
Wheelchair, Prosthetic,
AAC, Smart device"] TOOLS["T - Tools
Switches, Joystick,
Software, Interfaces"] ENV["E - Environment
Physical, Social,
Institutional, Cultural"] PERSON -->|defines goals for| ACTIVITY ACTIVITY -->|requires| ATECH ATECH -->|operated via| TOOLS TOOLS -->|feedback to| PERSON ACTIVITY -->|occurs within| ENV ENV -->|facilitates or hinders| ATECH ENV -->|shapes| PERSON TOOLS -->|adapted to| ACTIVITY ATECH -->|chosen by| PERSON PERSON -->|adapts to| ENV end style PERSON fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style ACTIVITY fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style ATECH fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style TOOLS fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style ENV fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 HAAT vs. PHAATE — Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HAAT_MODEL["HAAT Model (Cook and Hussey)"] H1["Human"] --> A1["Activity"] A1 --> T1["Assistive Technology"] T1 -.-> H1 end subgraph PHAATE_MODEL["PHAATE Model (Extended)"] P2["Person"] --> HA2["Human Activity"] HA2 --> AT2["Assistive Technology"] AT2 --> TT2["Tools"] TT2 --> E2["Environment"] E2 -.-> P2 E2 -.-> HA2 E2 -.-> AT2 E2 -.-> TT2 end HAAT_MODEL -->|Extension adds T and E| PHAATE_MODEL style H1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style T1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style P2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style HA2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style AT2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style TT2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000 style E2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 PHAATE Engineering Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Clinical Referral
Identify Person and Need"] --> B["Step 1: Person Domain
Clinical and Functional Assessment"] B --> C["Step 2: Activity Domain
ICF-based Task Analysis"] C --> D["Step 3: AT Domain
Match Device to Activity"] D --> E["Step 4: Tools Domain
Configure Interface and HMI"] E --> F["Step 5: Environment Domain
Audit Barriers and Facilitators"] F --> G{"Step 6: All Domains
Compatible?"} G -->|No| H["Iterate and Re-assess
Identify Conflict Domain"] H --> B G -->|Yes| I["Step 7: Trial Prescription
User Training"] I --> J["Step 8: Follow-up
at 1, 3, 6 months"] J --> K{"AT Adoption
Successful?"} K -->|Yes| L["Outcome: Long-term Use
Document in PHAATE Record"] K -->|No| M["Re-enter Cycle
Investigate Abandonment Cause"] style A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style M fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style G fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style K fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the PHAATE model. List its five components with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **PHAATE Model** is a person-centered conceptual framework in Rehabilitation Engineering that systematically integrates five interrelated domains to guide Assistive Technology (AT) prescription and design. - **P — Person**: The individual with disability (e.g., a person with spinal cord injury). - **HA — Human Activity**: The desired functional task (e.g., self-feeding). - **A — Assistive Technology**: The enabling device (e.g., a powered wheelchair). - **T — Tools**: The interface mechanism (e.g., a joystick controller). - **E — Environment**: The context of use (e.g., an accessible building with ramps). > Full marks awarded only when **all five components** are listed with **one distinct example each** and the definition explicitly mentions the **integration** aspect. *(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: components list; 1 mark: examples.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between the HAAT model and the PHAATE model. Why was the environment added?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | HAAT Model | PHAATE Model | |---------|------------|--------------| | Components | Human, Activity, AT | Person, HA, AT, Tools, Environment | | Tool layer | Implicit | Explicit "Tools" domain | | Environment | Not separately modeled | Explicit and dominant domain | | Scope | Device-centric | Holistic and context-aware | **Justification for adding Environment**: Clinical evidence showed **AT abandonment rates of 30%–50%** were largely attributable to **environmental barriers** (inaccessible buildings, social stigma, lack of institutional support). The PHAATE extension forces engineers to audit and address these factors, improving real-world adoption. *(1 mark: tabular differentiation; 1 mark: explicit reasoning; 1 mark: quantitative justification.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Describe in detail the **five domains of the PHAATE model** with relevant examples from rehabilitation engineering practice. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the PHAATE framework, **design and justify an AT intervention plan for a 60-year-old retired teacher with bilateral knee osteoarthritis (OA)** who wishes to continue gardening and short-distance community travel independently. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Answer for Question A** #### **Part (a) — Seven Marks Detailed Solution** **Introduction (1 mark):** The PHAATE model is a holistic, person-centered framework integrating Person, Human Activity, Assistive Technology, Tools, and Environment for optimal AT service delivery. **Domain-wise Explanation (5 marks, 1 each):** 1. **Person Domain**: Encompasses physical, cognitive, sensory, and psychosocial attributes. Example: A child with cerebral palsy having spastic quadriplegia, normal cognition, but impaired gross motor function (GMFCS Level IV). 2. **Human Activity Domain**: Functional goals classified per WHO-ICF. Example: Independent ambulation in school corridors (Activity & Participation domain of ICF). 3. **Assistive Technology Domain**: ISO 9999-classified devices. Example: A posterior walker with forearm supports to enable upright ambulation in the above child. 4. **Tools Domain**: Interface and operational mechanisms. Example: Hand-grip sensors with force-sensitive resistors providing haptic biofeedback during walker use. 5. **Environment Domain**: Physical, social, institutional context. Example: School corridor must have level flooring, adequate width (≥ **1500 mm** for two-way wheelchair traffic), and disability-friendly washrooms. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The PHAATE framework ensures that no domain is overlooked, leading to AT solutions with higher adoption rates and functional outcomes. #### **Part (b) — Seven Marks Design Solution** | PHAATE Domain | Assessment | Intervention | |---------------|------------|--------------| | **Person** | Bilateral knee OA, pain on weight-bearing (VAS 7/10), reduced ROM (flexion limited to 90°), otherwise healthy | — | | **Human Activity** | Gardening (kneeling tasks) and community travel (200 m – 1 km) | — | | **AT** | — | **(i)** Knee-ankle-foot orthoses (KAFO) with unloading hinges. **(ii)** Lightweight folding rollator with seat and brakes. **(iii)** Long-handled ergonomic gardening tools. | | **Tools** | Standard rollator grip causes hand pain | Customized foam-padded grips; arthritis-friendly garden tool handles with thick silicone sleeves | | **Environment** | Uneven garden terrain; community lacks curb cuts | Recommend raised garden beds (waist height); coordinate with municipality for curb-cut installation (per RPwD Act 2016, India) | **Engineering Justification (1 mark):** The rollator's seat allows rest intervals to manage pain-induced fatigue. Raised garden beds eliminate the need to kneel, addressing the ROM limitation. Both interventions are **environment-modifying** in addition to device-prescribing, demonstrating full PHAATE compliance. --- ### **Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **significance of the Environment domain** in the PHAATE model. Discuss at least **four sub-categories** with engineering interventions for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **22-year-old university student with total blindness** wishes to pursue engineering studies. Apply the PHAATE model to recommend a comprehensive AT ecosystem. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Answer for Question B** #### **Part (a) — Seven Marks Solution** **Significance (1 mark):** The Environment domain determines whether an AT device functions in real-world conditions. It is the most critical domain for AT abandonment prevention. **Four Sub-categories with Engineering Interventions (5 marks, 1.25 each):** 1. **Physical Environment** — Ramp construction (slope $\leq 1/12$ per ADA), tactile paving, accessible door handles (lever-type, not knob-type), adequate lighting ($\geq$ **300 lux** for visually impaired reading). 2. **Technological Environment** — Reliable power backup for powered AT (UPS systems), Wi-Fi mesh networks for IoT-based AT in rural areas, charging infrastructure at public buildings. 3. **Social Environment** — Awareness campaigns, peer-support mobile apps, disability sensitization modules in workplaces, design of inclusive public transport apps. 4. **Institutional Environment** — Implementation of **Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPwD) Act 2016** in India, AT funding schemes (ADIP, DDRS), insurance coverage mandates, university disability support offices. 5. **Cultural Environment** *(bonus 0.5 mark if cited)* — Addressing gender-based stigma for women with disabilities, designing culturally sensitive prosthetics (e.g., skin-tone matching, religious garment compatibility). **Conclusion (1 mark):** Engineering solutions must extend beyond the device to the environment to ensure functional equity. #### **Part (b) — Seven Marks Comprehensive AT Ecosystem** | PHAATE Domain | Detailed Recommendation | |---------------|-------------------------| | **Person** | Congenitally blind, otherwise neurotypical, high academic motivation, strong family support | | **Activity** | Engineering coursework — reading textbooks, writing assignments, lab work, navigating campus | | **AT** | Screen reader (JAWS/NVDA), refreshable Braille display (e.g., Focus 40), Braille embosser, accessible scientific calculator (Orbit Speakout), tactile 3D-printed models of engineering diagrams | | **Tools** | Smart glasses with optical character recognition (OCR) like OrCam MyEye, voice-to-text dictation software (Dragon), audio-based graphing tools | | **Environment** | Tactile signage across campus, audio-described laboratory equipment, prioritized assistance from disability support office, accessible PDFs mandated for all course materials, peer note-takers | **Justification (1 mark):** The holistic integration ensures academic equity. Note that AT alone (JAWS, Braille display) is **insufficient without** a **braille-embossed accessible library** and **faculty training** — both Environment-domain interventions. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PHAATE Answers:** > > 1. **Conflating "Tools" with "AT"** — Many students treat Tools and AT as the same. Tools are the **interface layer** (joystick, switch), while AT is the **functional device** (wheelchair, prosthetic). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion. > > 2. **Ignoring the Environment Domain** — In case-study questions, omitting environmental barriers (stairs, social stigma, policy gaps) signals **incomplete PHAATE application**. Deducted: **1–2 marks**. > > 3. **Writing Generic Definitions** — Avoid textbook-style memorized answers. Examiners expect **engineering-specific examples** (KAFO, AAC devices, FIM scores). Generic answers cap at **60–70% of marks**. > > 4. **No Standardized Reference** — Always anchor your Person-domain assessment to **FIM, Barthel Index, MMSE, or ICF codes**. Omitting these costs the **application-level marks** under Bloom's Taxonomy. > > 5. **Missing Conclusion/Synthesis** — A 14-mark answer without a concluding paragraph integrating all five domains appears **incomplete**. Deducted: **1 mark**. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - ✅ **PHAATE** = **P**erson + **H**uman **A**ctivity + **A**ssistive Technology + **T**ools + **E**nvironment. - ✅ PHAATE evolved from **HAAT (Cook and Hussey)** by adding explicit Tools and Environment domains. - ✅ **Person-centered**, not device-centered — the user is at the core of all design decisions. - ✅ Person domain must be quantified using **FIM (18–126)**, **Barthel Index (0–100)**, **MMSE**, or **ICF codes**. - ✅ AT classification follows **ISO 9999:2022** standard categories. - ✅ Tools domain includes **input/output interfaces, software, and fabrication tools** (e.g., 3D printers). - ✅ Environment has **5+ sub-domains**: physical, technological, social, institutional, cultural, attitudinal. - ✅ **AT abandonment rates** of 30%–50% are primarily due to **environment-domain failures**. - ✅ Engineering accessibility benchmarks to memorize: - Door width $\geq$ **900 mm** (ADA) - Ramp slope $\leq$ **1:12** - Corridor width $\geq$ **1500 mm** (two-way wheelchair) - Lighting $\geq$ **300 lux** (visually impaired) - IP rating for outdoor AT: **IP54 minimum**, **IP67** for submersible use. - ✅ Real-world applications: smart wheelchairs, AAC devices, prosthetics, smart homes, universal design projects. - ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme expects **interdisciplinary answers** linking biomedical engineering with social sciences, policy, and HCI. - ✅ Always end Part B answers with a **synthesis statement** connecting all five domains. - ✅ Indian context: Reference **RPwD Act 2016** and **ADIP scheme** for institutional-environment examples. --- > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — PHAATE MODEL** > *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Telerehabilitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Telerehabilitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Telerehabilitation (TR)** is a branch of *telehealth* and *rehabilitation engineering* that delivers remote rehabilitation services to individuals with physical, cognitive, or communication disabilities using **Information and Communication Technologies (ICT)**. According to the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the **American Telemedicine Association (ATA)**, telerehabilitation is formally defined as:

> *"The delivery of rehabilitation services via information and communication technologies, including but not limited to, evaluation, diagnosis, monitoring, intervention, supervision, education, and consultation, to bridge the gap between patients and clinicians across geographical, temporal, and socio-economic barriers."*

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT864)** Module 1, telerehabilitation is positioned as the convergence of **biomedical instrumentation**, **IoT-based wearables**, **computer vision**, **virtual reality (VR)**, and **cloud computing** to extend the reach of physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy beyond the conventional clinic walls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates students to differentiate telerehabilitation from conventional rehabilitation, identify its architectural building blocks, and evaluate the role of sensor networks, wearable technology, and remote monitoring platforms. Pay close attention to the distinction between *synchronous*, *asynchronous*, and *hybrid* telerehabilitation models.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Virtual Therapy Room"

Imagine a patient named **Ananthu**, a 58-year-old stroke survivor living in a remote village in **Wayanad, Kerala**. He has lost partial motor function on his right side and requires daily physiotherapy.
