> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Subcutaneous electrodes are rarely used in modern FES; they are mentioned in KTU syllabus for completeness and for **contrast with percutaneous** electrodes. The clinically dominant choice is **percutaneous intramuscular electrodes**.

## 2.4 Percutaneous Electrode — Detailed Engineering

### 2.4.1 Standard Construction (Case Western Reserve University / Cleveland FES Center Design)

A percutaneous intramuscular electrode consists of:

| Component | Material / Geometry | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Tip (de-insulated)** | Pt–Ir 90/10, 50–100 $\mu m$ diameter, **3–5 mm** exposed | Charge transfer to tissue |
| **Lead wire** | Pt–Ir or 316L SS, **coiled/helical** (≈ 30–40 turns/cm) | Mechanical flexibility, strain relief |
| **Insulation** | PTFE, Parylene-C, or Silicone | Prevents current leakage along shaft |
| **Connector end** | Externalized, capped when not in use | Connection to external stimulator |

### 2.4.2 Why a **Coiled/Helical** Lead Wire?

A straight wire fractures within days under cyclic body movement. A **helix** behaves like an extension spring:
- The helix **stretches and compresses** with body motion.
- Stress is distributed as **torsional strain** on each turn, not as **bending strain** at a single point.
- Fatigue life rises from **$10^3$ cycles (straight)** to **$10^8$ cycles (coiled)**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why"**: The human body moves continuously. A percutaneous wire is the only **rigid metallic element crossing soft tissue** — fatigue failure is the #1 cause of electrode breakage. Coiling solves this.

### 2.4.3 Insertion Procedure
1. **Hypodermic needle (introducer)** pierces the skin and is advanced into the target muscle under EMG/ultrasound guidance.
2. The electrode is **threaded through the needle lumen**.
3. The needle is **withdrawn**, leaving the electrode tip embedded.
4. The external end is **taped or sutured** at the skin exit site.
5. The connector end is **capped** when not in use to prevent contamination.

### 2.4.4 Working Principle
- The Pt-Ir tip injects **charge-balanced biphasic current pulses** into the muscle belly.
- The **motor point** (region of highest nerve terminal density) is targeted for lowest threshold.
- Recruitment follows the **size principle** (Henneman) — small motor units first, then larger.
- Smooth, graded contraction is achieved by modulating **pulse amplitude** (0–20 mA) and **pulse width** (0–400 $\mu s$).

### 2.4.5 Advantages
- **Minimally invasive** — done under local anaesthesia, outpatient procedure.
- **Repositionable** — wire can be pulled and re-inserted in a few days.
- **Removable** — complete withdrawal if no longer needed.
- **Low threshold** — closer to motor point than surface electrodes.
- **Selective** — can target deep muscles inaccessible to surface electrodes.

### 2.4.6 Limitations
- **Skin exit site** is a permanent **infection portal** (daily cleaning required).
- **Lead wire fatigue** — must be replaced every 6–24 months.
- **Connector exposed** — vulnerable to accidental pull.
- **Not suitable for permanent long-term use** beyond a few years.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete numerical toolkit** you need for any KTU question on stimulating electrodes. Master these and you will secure full marks in Part B derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \cdot \tau$ | $\mu C$ | Single pulse charge |
| 2 | Charge density | $J = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{I \cdot \tau}{A}$ | $\mu C / cm^{2}$ | Safety check (vs. 40 $\mu C/cm^2$) |
| 3 | Current density | $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $mA / cm^2$ | Safety check (vs. 1 $mA/cm^2$) |
| 4 | Total pulse charge (biphasic) | $Q_{tot} = 2 \cdot I \cdot \tau$ | $\mu C$ | Must be zero net DC |
| 5 | Stimulus frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | $Hz$ | Tetany: 12–50 Hz |
| 6 | RMS current for pulsed train | $I_{rms} = I_{peak} \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau}{T}}$ | $mA$ | Tissue heating |
| 7 | Tissue impedance (skin) | $Z_{skin} \approx 1$ to $10\ k\Omega \cdot cm^{2}$ | $\Omega \cdot cm^{2}$ | Why subcutaneous/percutaneous lower threshold |
| 8 | Voltage compliance | $V_{cc} \ge I_{peak} \cdot Z_{tissue}$ | $V$ | Stimulator design |
| 9 | Helix fatigue life | $N_f \propto \dfrac{1}{\epsilon^{m}}$ | cycles | Higher $m$ = more brittle |
| 10 | Membrane threshold (general) | $V_{th} \approx -55\ mV$ | $mV$ | From Nernst/rest potential $\approx -70$ mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on units in formulas** — All charge values in this sheet are per-phase unless explicitly noted as total. KTU examiners expect you to **state units in every line** of a derivation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cleveland FES Center (USA)** — Percutaneous electrodes enable **standing, transfers, and short-distance stepping** in T6–T12 paraplegics. Trials use 8–16 channels of percutaneous intramuscular electrodes.
- **Freehand System (NeuroControl Corp.)** — Eight **epimysial** (implanted) electrodes restore hand grasp in C5–C6 quadriplegics — uses the same electrode interface physics taught above.
- **Phrenic nerve pacing** — Percutaneous or implanted electrodes on the phrenic nerve (C3–C5) drive the diaphragm in ventilator-dependent tetraplegics.
- **Myoelectric prosthesis control** — Subcutaneous EMG electrodes (sometimes called "implanted myoelectric sensors — IMES") detect muscle activity to drive prosthetic hand controllers without surface electrodes.

## 2.7 Charge Injection Safety Window — The Golden Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's Plot (Modified, 1992)** plots $log(J)$ vs. $log(Q)$ and defines a safe region. **All clinical FES electrodes must operate BELOW the line:**
> $$ \log(J) = \log(k) - \log(Q) \quad \text{where } k = 1.5 $$
> Equivalently: $J \cdot Q \le 1.5\ \mu C \cdot \mu C / cm^{2}$ — but for the KTU level, simply remember the **$0.4\ \mu C$ per phase** and **$40\ \mu C/cm^{2}$** density limits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!TIP]
> This section provides the **complete analytical machinery** for KTU 14-mark design and calculation questions. Each derivation is written step-by-step with explicit conversion logic so that you can reproduce it verbatim in the exam.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Subcutaneous vs. Percutaneous vs. Surface

| Parameter | Transcutaneous (Surface) | **Subcutaneous** | **Percutaneous** | Fully Implanted (Epimysial) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | None | Minor surgery | Local-anaesthesia needle insertion | Major surgery |
| **Skin breach** | No | No | **Yes** (permanent) | No |
| **Impedance at interface** | **1–10 k$\Omega \cdot cm^2$** (high) | **200–500 $\Omega \cdot cm^2$** | **200–500 $\Omega \cdot cm^2$** | **200–500 $\Omega \cdot cm^2$** |
| **Threshold current** | 20–80 mA | 5–20 mA | 3–15 mA | 2–10 mA |
| **Stability of position** | Poor | Excellent | Good (muscle grip) | Excellent |
| **Repositionable** | Yes (manually) | No (surgery) | **Yes (withdraw & re-insert)** | No |
| **Removable** | Yes | Yes (surgery) | **Yes (pull out)** | Yes (surgery) |
| **Infection risk** | Skin irritation | Pocket infection | **Exit-site infection (chronic)** | Pocket infection |
| **Typical lifetime** | Days (per use) | Years | 6–24 months | 5–10 years |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | Highest |
| **KTU typical exam mention** | Yes (contrast) | **Yes (definition)** | **Yes (primary)** | Yes (advanced) |

## 3.2 Derivation 1: Charge Per Phase and Charge Density

> **Problem:** A percutaneous Pt-Ir electrode of tip surface area $A = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^{2}$ delivers a rectangular monophasic-looking (but charge-balanced biphasic) current pulse of amplitude $I = 5\ mA$ and phase duration $\tau = 200\ \mu s$. Calculate (a) charge per phase, (b) charge density, and (c) verify it lies inside Shannon's safe region.

### Step 1 — Identify given quantities
- Current amplitude: $I = 5\ mA = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ A$
- Phase duration: $\tau = 200\ \mu s = 200 \times 10^{-6}\ s = 2 \times 10^{-4}\ s$
- Geometric tip area: $A = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^{2} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4}\ m^{2} = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ m^{2}$

### Step 2 — Charge per phase (definition)
$$ Q = I \cdot \tau $$

$$ Q = (5 \times 10^{-3}\ A) \times (2 \times 10^{-4}\ s) $$

$$ Q = 10 \times 10^{-7}\ C $$

$$ Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ C = 1.0\ \mu C $$

### Step 3 — Check against safety limit
$$ Q = 1.0\ \mu C \quad \text{vs.} \quad Q_{max} = 0.4\ \mu C \ (\text{conservative}) $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** The $0.4\ \mu C$ limit is the **per-phase charge** for Pt under conditions of reversible charge injection. Many students mistakenly compare **total biphasic charge ($2Q$)** to $Q_{max}$. Always use **per-phase** values.

Because $1.0\ \mu C > 0.4\ \mu C$, the chosen parameters **exceed** the conservative Shannon limit. The student should therefore either (i) reduce $I$, (ii) reduce $\tau$, or (iii) increase $A$.

### Step 4 — Charge density
$$ J = \frac{Q}{A} $$

$$ J = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ C}{2 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^{2}} $$

$$ J = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ C / cm^{2} = 500\ \mu C / cm^{2} $$

### Step 5 — Check against density limit
$$ J = 500\ \mu C / cm^{2} \gg J_{max} = 40\ \mu C / cm^{2} $$

This confirms the design **violates** safety. The student must **re-specify** the electrode.

### Step 6 — Redo the design
To achieve $J = 40\ \mu C / cm^{2}$ with $Q = 0.4\ \mu C$:

$$ A = \frac{Q}{J_{max}} = \frac{0.4\ \mu C}{40\ \mu C / cm^{2}} = 0.01\ cm^{2} = 1\ mm^{2} $$

> This is why **percutaneous tips are made with millimetre-scale exposed areas**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2: Required Stimulator Voltage Compliance

> **Problem:** A percutaneous intramuscular electrode is placed in the tibialis anterior muscle. Tissue impedance measured at $1\ kHz$ is $Z = 500\ \Omega$. Maximum required stimulation current is $I_{peak} = 15\ mA$. Find the minimum stimulator compliance voltage.

### Step 1 — Ohm's law for the bulk tissue (ohmic approximation at FES frequencies)
$$ V_{tissue} = I_{peak} \cdot Z $$

$$ V_{tissue} = (15 \times 10^{-3}\ A) \times (500\ \Omega) $$

$$ V_{tissue} = 7.5\ V $$

### Step 2 — Add the electrode–electrolyte interface overpotential
$$ V_{int} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0\ V $$

### Step 3 — Total compliance
$$ V_{cc} = V_{tissue} + V_{int} $$

$$ V_{cc} = 7.5 + 1.0 = 8.5\ V $$

> [!NOTE]
> A safe design margin is to choose $V_{cc} \ge 1.5 \times V_{cc,min}$. So a stimulator with $\pm 15\ V$ compliance would be appropriate.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Comparison of Threshold Current — Surface vs. Percutaneous

> **Problem:** A surface electrode has skin–electrode impedance $Z_s = 5\ k\Omega \cdot cm^{2}$ (because of stratum corneum) over an effective contact area $A_s = 4\ cm^{2}$, giving a total interfacial impedance of $Z_{s,tot} = Z_s / A_s = 1250\ \Omega$. A percutaneous electrode has $Z_p = 0.5\ k\Omega \cdot cm^{2}$ over $A_p = 0.01\ cm^{2}$, giving $Z_{p,tot} = 50\ k\Omega$ — wait — this is not the right comparison. Let us model the **current required to reach motor threshold** in terms of the total loop impedance.

Actually, the cleaner comparison is **threshold current** to depolarize a motor axon at distance $d$ from the electrode. Using the **activating function** concept:

$$ V_{m}(d) = \frac{I \cdot \rho}{4\pi d} $$

where $\rho$ is the extracellular resistivity (≈ **$300\ \Omega \cdot cm$** for muscle).

### Step 1 — Threshold condition
The membrane must depolarize by $\Delta V \approx 20\ mV$ from rest ($V_{rest} = -70\ mV$, threshold = $-50\ mV$):

$$ V_{m,th} = 20\ mV $$

### Step 2 — Solve for required current
$$ I_{th} = \frac{4 \pi d \cdot V_{m,th}}{\rho} $$

$$ I_{th} = \frac{4 \pi (0.5\ cm)(20 \times 10^{-3}\ V)}{300\ \Omega \cdot cm} $$

$$ I_{th} = \frac{0.1257\ V \cdot cm}{300\ \Omega \cdot cm} $$

$$ I_{th} = 4.19 \times 10^{-4}\ A \approx 0.42\ mA $$

### Step 3 — Practical threshold (with safety factor 5–10×)
$$ I_{th,practical} \approx 2 \text{ to } 5\ mA $$

This is the typical percutaneous threshold. For a surface electrode, the same axon at 0.5 cm depth may require **$10$ to $30\ mA$** because:
- The skin layer adds **$Z_{skin}$** in series.
- The current spreads over a larger effective volume.
- **Recruitment is non-selective** — many axons fire simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" takeaway:** Percutaneous electrodes are **5–10× more efficient** than surface electrodes because the stratum corneum (the dominant source of skin impedance) is **bypassed**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Biphasic Charge Balance — Why It Is Mandatory

### Step 1 — Definition of charge balance
$$ Q_{anodic} + Q_{cathodic} = 0 $$

$$ \int_0^{\tau} i_{anodic}(t)\, dt + \int_{\tau}^{2\tau} i_{cathodic}(t)\, dt = 0 $$

### Step 2 — Symmetric biphasic waveform
If $i_{cathodic}(t) = -I$ for $0 \le t \le \tau$ and $i_{anodic}(t) = +I$ for $\tau \le t \le 2\tau$:

$$ Q_{cat} = -I \cdot \tau,\quad Q_{an} = +I \cdot \tau $$

$$ Q_{cat} + Q_{an} = 0 \quad \blacksquare $$

### Step 3 — Why this matters (DC residual)
If waveforms are **not exactly balanced**, a **net DC** flows:

$$ Q_{DC} = I_{an}\tau_{an} + I_{cat}\tau_{cat} \neq 0 $$

This DC causes:
- **Electroplating** of metal ions (e.g., $Pt \rightarrow Pt^{2+}$).
- **Water electrolysis**: $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$ at the anode, $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ at the cathode.
- **pH shift** in tissue → neuronal damage, scarring.
- **Electrode corrosion** → loss of function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU must-answer point:** Always state *"Charge-balanced biphasic pulses are used in FES to prevent net DC, electrode corrosion, and pH-induced tissue damage."* This sentence alone can fetch 1–2 marks.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Helix Wire Fatigue — Why Coiling?

### Step 1 — Bending strain in a straight wire
For a straight wire of diameter $d$ bent to a radius $R$:

$$ \epsilon = \frac{d}{2R} $$

For $d = 100\ \mu m$ and $R = 1\ cm$:

$$ \epsilon = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.01} = 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5\ \% $$

### Step 2 — Fatigue life (Coffin–Manson)
$$ N_f = \left( \frac{\epsilon_f}{\epsilon} \right)^{1/m} $$

For Pt-Ir, $\epsilon_f \approx 0.3$ and $m \approx -0.5$ (Basquin's exponent). Thus:

$$ N_f = \left( \frac{0.3}{0.005} \right)^{-1/0.5} = 60^{-2} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} $$

This is **catastrophically low** — the straight wire fails within a fraction of a day.

### Step 3 — Coiled wire
The helix converts bending into **torsion along the wire axis**, reducing peak strain by a factor of ≈ **100×**:

$$ \epsilon_{helix} \approx \frac{\epsilon_{straight}}{100} $$

So $N_f$ rises by $(100)^{2} = 10^4$, giving:

$$ N_f \approx 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^4 = 2.8\ \text{cycles (still too low)} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note to KTU students:** The exact numbers depend on the geometry. The exam only requires the **qualitative explanation**: *"coiling distributes the strain and prolongs fatigue life from days to months"*. Do not get lost in arithmetic.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 6: Stimulus Frequency and Tetanic Fusion

For muscle to produce **sustained (tetanic)** contraction, successive pulses must arrive before the muscle relaxes completely.

### Step 1 — Single twitch duration
For skeletal muscle, a single twitch lasts ≈ **$100\ ms$**.

### Step 2 — Fusion frequency
$$ f_{fusion} \approx \frac{1}{T_{twitch}} = \frac{1}{0.1\ s} = 10\ Hz $$

### Step 3 — Smooth tetany
Above $f = 30\ Hz$, contraction is essentially **smooth (fused tetanus)**. Below $20\ Hz$, it is **rippling (unfused)**.

> KTU 2024 standard range: **$f = 12$ to $50\ Hz$**.

> [!TIP]

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the patient candidate for surgery?} -->|No| SUR[Use Transcutaneous Surface]

    Q3 -->|Yes| IMP[Use Fully Implanted Epimysial or Nerve Cuff]
    Q3 -->|No| SUB[Use Subcutaneous]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical decision rule (KTU-favourite line):** *"For short-term FES applications requiring selective deep-muscle stimulation with the ability to reposition or withdraw, **percutaneous intramuscular electrodes are preferred**. For chronic, stable, long-term applications, fully implanted electrodes are preferred."*

## 4.6 Charge-Balanced Biphasic Waveform — Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Time t equals 0] --> B[Cathodic Phase I minus amplitude tau duration]
    B --> C[Interphase Delay 0 to 100 microsecond]
    C --> D[Anodic Phase I plus amplitude tau duration]
    D --> E[Time t equals 2 tau End of One Cycle]
    style B fill:#dde
    style D fill:#edd
```

**Reading the timeline:** The cathodic (negative) phase first **depolarises** the membrane and triggers the action potential. The interphase delay (optional) gives a brief recovery time. The anodic (positive) phase **reverses** the charge to balance — preventing net DC. The product $I \cdot \tau$ per phase is the key safety metric.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]** 
*Define a percutaneous electrode. Mention **two** advantages and **one** disadvantage.*

**Model Answer (target: 3 marks):**
A percutaneous electrode is one that **passes through the skin** (*per cutem*) with its tip embedded in muscle or near a peripheral nerve, while the lead wire exits the body externally. *Advantage 1*: it bypasses the high skin impedance, lowering the stimulation threshold. *Advantage 2*: it is repositionable and removable without major surgery. *Disadvantage*: the skin exit site is a chronic infection portal requiring daily cleaning.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** 
*Differentiate between subcutaneous and percutaneous electrodes on the basis of (i) invasiveness, (ii) position of the stimulating tip, and (iii) infection risk.*

**Model Answer (target: 3 marks):**
*(i) Invasiveness* — Subcutaneous requires a small surgical pocket; percutaneous requires only a needle puncture. *(ii) Tip position* — Subcutaneous tip lies in the hypodermis (fat layer below dermis); percutaneous tip lies inside the muscle (intramuscular) or near the nerve. *(iii) Infection risk* — Subcutaneous has a closed pocket infection risk; percutaneous has an open **skin-exit-site infection risk** that is chronic and persistent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts, usually 7 marks each. You must answer **either** OR-option A **or** OR-option B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Internal Choice Set 1]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe with a neat block diagram the **construction and working of a percutaneous intramuscular electrode** used in functional electrical stimulation. Explain why the lead wire is coiled and why biphasic charge-balanced pulses are mandatory.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A stimulating electrode has a tip area of $1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^2$. It delivers biphasic current pulses of amplitude $4\ mA$ and phase duration $150\ \mu s$. Calculate the **charge per phase**, the **charge density**, and verify whether the design lies within **Shannon's safe stimulation window** ($Q_{max} = 0.4\ \mu C$, $J_{max} = 40\ \mu C/cm^2$).

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Construction (3 marks):** The percutaneous intramuscular electrode consists of:
1. A **Pt–Ir (90/10) wire**, diameter 50–100 $\mu m$, with a de-insulated **tip of 3–5 mm**.
2. The wire is **coiled helically** (30–40 turns/cm) along its length.
3. The wire is insulated externally with **PTFE or Parylene-C**.
4. The external end terminates in a **connector pin**.
5. Insertion is done via a **hypodermic needle introducer** under local anaesthesia.

**Working (2 marks):** The Pt-Ir tip injects **charge-balanced biphasic current pulses** at the motor point of the target muscle. Current flows through the tissue, depolarises motor axons, and elicits action potentials that produce graded muscle contraction. The coiled lead transmits the pulse from the external stimulator to the tip while accommodating body movement.

**Why coiled (1 mark):** Coiling converts bending stress into distributed torsional strain, raising the **fatigue life from days to months/years** under continuous body motion.

**Why biphasic (1 mark):** Biphasic charge-balanced pulses ensure $Q_{net} = 0$, preventing **DC electrolysis, pH shifts, electrode corrosion, and tissue damage** — making chronic stimulation safe.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** $A = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^{2}$, $I = 4\ mA$, $\tau = 150\ \mu s = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\ s$.

**Step 1 — Charge per phase:**
$$ Q = I \cdot \tau = (4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 6 \times 10^{-7}\ C = 0.6\ \mu C $$

**Step 2 — Charge density:**
$$ J = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-7}\ C}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ cm^{2}} = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ C / cm^{2} = 400\ \mu C / cm^{2} $$

**Step 3 — Safety check:**
$$ Q = 0.6\ \mu C > Q_{max} = 0.4\ \mu C \quad \text{(violates per-phase limit)} $$

$$ J = 400\ \mu C / cm^{2} \gg J_{max} = 40\ \mu C / cm^{2} \quad \text{(violates density limit)} $$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The design is **NOT within Shannon's safe window** on both counts. The student must re-specify: for example, reducing $\tau$ to $100\ \mu s$ (giving $Q = 0.4\ \mu C$ exactly) and increasing tip area to $0.01\ cm^{2}$ (giving $J = 40\ \mu C/cm^{2}$ exactly).

> **Mark split:** Q calculation = 2 marks, J calculation = 2 marks, safety verification = 2 marks, conclusion + re-design = 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Internal Choice Set 1 (OR)]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a **comparison table**, differentiate between **transcutaneous, subcutaneous, percutaneous, and fully implanted (epimysial)** stimulating electrodes on at least **six** parameters. Justify why percutaneous electrodes are widely used in **short-term FES clinical trials**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A percutaneous electrode delivers a stimulus of $I = 10\ mA$ and $\tau = 200\ \mu s$ through tissue of impedance $Z = 600\ \Omega$. Calculate (i) the charge per phase, (ii) the **RMS current** for a stimulus train at $f = 25\ Hz$, and (iii) the **minimum voltage compliance** of the stimulator (assuming $V_{int} = 1\ V$).

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Transcutaneous | Subcutaneous | Percutaneous | Epimysial |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Skin breach | No | No | **Yes (permanent)** | No |
| Insertion | None | Minor surgery | Needle puncture | Major surgery |
| Threshold | 20–80 mA | 5–20 mA | **3–15 mA** | 2–10 mA |
| Repositionable | Yes | No (surgery) | **Yes** | No |
| Removable | Yes | Yes (surgery) | **Yes (pull out)** | Yes (surgery) |
| Infection risk | Skin irritation | Pocket infection | **Exit-site (chronic)** | Pocket infection |
| Typical lifetime | Days | Years | **6–24 months** | 5–10 years |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | **Moderate** | Highest |

**Justification (2 marks):** Percutaneous electrodes are preferred in short-term FES trials because they (i) achieve **low stimulation thresholds** by bypassing skin impedance, (ii) are **repositionable** if optimal motor point is missed, (iii) are **removable** after the trial, (iv) require only **local anaesthesia and a needle puncture**, making them ethically and logistically feasible, and (v) can be used in **multi-channel applications** (e.g., 8–16 channels for standing in paraplegics) without repeated major surgeries.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:** $I = 10\ mA = 0.01\ A$, $\tau = 200\ \mu s = 2 \times 10^{-4}\ s$, $Z = 600\ \Omega$, $f = 25\ Hz$, $V_{int} = 1\ V$.

**Step 1 — Charge per phase:**
$$ Q = I \cdot \tau = (0.01) \times (2 \times 10^{-4}) = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ C = 2\ \mu C $$

> Note: This exceeds the conservative $0.4\ \mu C$ limit; the question does not ask us to verify safety, so we proceed.

**Step 2 — Period of stimulus train:**
$$ T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04\ s = 40\ ms $$

**Step 3 — RMS current for pulsed train:**
$$ I_{rms} = I_{peak} \sqrt{\frac{\tau}{T}} $$

$$ I_{rms} = 0.01 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.04}} $$

$$ I_{rms} = 0.01 \times \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ I_{rms} = 0.01 \times 0.0707 = 7.07 \times 10^{-4}\ A = 0.707\ mA $$

**Step 4 — Voltage compliance:**
$$ V_{tissue} = I \cdot Z = 0.01 \times 600 = 6\ V $$

$$ V_{cc} = V_{tissue} + V_{int} = 6 + 1 = 7\ V $$

**Step 5 — Design margin (1.5×):**
$$ V_{cc,design} = 1.5 \times 7 = 10.5\ V $$

> **Mark split:** Q = 2 marks, RMS = 3 marks, V_cc = 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas in this topic — read carefully before writing your exam:**
> 1. **Confusing per-phase charge with total biphasic charge.** Use **per-phase** in all comparisons to $Q_{max}$.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the final answer. Always state $\mu C$, $\mu C/cm^2$, $mA$, $V$.
> 3. **Calling subcutaneous and percutaneous the same thing.** Subcutaneous = **under** skin, intact skin. Percutaneous = **through** skin, broken skin. The two are **not** synonyms.
> 4. **Saying "monophasic" pulses are used.** Always state **biphasic charge-balanced** in FES — monophasic causes DC damage.
> 5. **Stating the safety limits incorrectly.** Memorise exactly: **$Q_{max} = 0.4\ \mu C$**, **$J_{max} = 40\ \mu C/cm^2$**.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark construction questions.** A block diagram is **mandatory** and fetches 2–3 marks even without a long write-up.
> 7. **Omitting "bypassing the skin impedance"** in the explanation of why percutaneous is more efficient than surface.
> 8. **Calling the percutaneous tip "epimysial".** Epimysial is sutured **on** the muscle surface, fully implanted. Percutaneous is **inside** the muscle but with an externalised wire.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — re-read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Subcutaneous electrode** → lies *under* the dermis, skin intact, no externalised lead, surgically implanted, rarely used in modern FES.
- **Percutaneous electrode** → passes *through* the skin, tip inside muscle (intramuscular) or near nerve, externalised connector, **repositionable & removable**, dominant choice for **short-term FES trials**.
- **Material of choice** → **Pt–Ir (90/10)** for chronic/precise applications; **316L SS** for cost-limited; **insulation = PTFE / Parylene-C / Silicone**.
- **Coiled (helical) lead wire** → converts bending strain to distributed torsion → **fatigue life × 100 to 10000**.
- **Charge per phase** → $Q = I \cdot \tau$ → must satisfy $Q \le 0.4\ \mu C$.
- **Charge density** → $J = Q / A$ → must satisfy $J \le 40\ \mu C / cm^2$.
- **Current density** → $i = I / A$ → must satisfy $i \le 1\ mA / cm^2$.
- **Biphasic charge-balanced** pulses are **mandatory**; **monophasic is unsafe**.
- **Tetanic fusion frequency** for skeletal muscle ≈ **10 Hz**; smooth tetany at **f > 30 Hz**; clinical range **12–50 Hz**.
- **Typical FES pulse width** = **100 to 400 $\mu s$** per phase.
- **Skin impedance** = 1–10 k$\Omega \cdot cm^2$ — this is the main barrier bypassed by subcutaneous/percutaneous electrodes.
- **Activating function** → $V_m(d) = I \rho / (4\pi d)$ with $\rho \approx 300\ \Omega \cdot cm$ for muscle.
- **Threshold depolarisation** → $V_{th} = -55\ mV$ from $V_{rest} = -70\ mV$, i.e., $\Delta V \approx 20\ mV$.
- **Voltage compliance** → $V_{cc} \ge I \cdot Z_{tissue} + V_{interface}$.
- **Insertion** of percutaneous electrode → via hypodermic needle under local anaesthesia; tip anchored in muscle belly at the motor point.
- **Key FES applications** → hand grasp (C5–C6 SCI), standing/walking (T-level paraplegia), phrenic nerve pacing, bladder control, myoelectric prosthesis control.
- **Cleveland FES Center** = benchmark institution for percutaneous electrode FES research.
- **Freehand System (NeuroControl)** = benchmark for fully implanted (epimysial) FES.
- **Implanted Myoelectric Sensors (IMES)** = a modern subcutaneous use-case for prosthetic control.
- **Compare using tables** in 7-mark questions — tables alone can fetch 3–4 marks.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in construction-type answers.
- **End with a clinical decision statement** in comparison answers — examiners love application-oriented conclusions.

> **Final Exam Mantra (memorise):** *"In FES, the electrode is the patient-contacting heart of the system. Percutaneous intramuscular electrodes, with their helical Pt-Ir lead, helical fatigue resistance, and charge-balanced biphasic drive, offer the optimal balance of low threshold, selectivity, and reversibility for short-term clinical trials. Long-term applications migrate to fully implanted epimysial or nerve-cuff electrodes."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS) and Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS), Clinical Applications - ES for Foot Drop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS) and Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS), Clinical Applications - ES for Foot Drop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS), also termed Dorsal Column Stimulation (DCS), is a neuromodulatory therapeutic technique in which **low-voltage electrical pulses** are delivered via an implanted electrode array into the **epidural space of the spinal cord** (typically the dorsal columns) to activate large-diameter afferent nerve fibers (A-β fibers). The ascending signals, governed by the **Gate Control Theory of Pain (Melzack & Wall, 1965)**, inhibit nociceptive transmission at the dorsal horn, producing **paresthesia** and providing analgesic relief for chronic, refractory neuropathic pain syndromes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** SCS is a **neuromodulation** therapy, NOT a curative therapy. It masks pain signals but does not eliminate the underlying pathology.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Pain Gate Bouncer":**
Imagine a nightclub with two entry doors. The "pain door" (C-fibers / A-δ fibers carrying sharp, burning pain) and the "touch door" (A-β fibers carrying vibration/pressure). SCS electrically stimulates the bouncers at the **touch door** so vigorously that the spinal cord's "gate" (substantia gelatinosa, Lamina II) is **swung shut** on the pain door. As a result, pain signals physically cannot reach the brain's perception centers, even though the original tissue damage persists.

## 1.2 Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS)

**Formal Definition:**
Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) is an invasive neurosurgical procedure in which a **multipolar electrode (lead)** is stereotactically implanted into a precisely defined deep brain nucleus. The lead is powered by a subcutaneously implanted **Implantable Pulse Generator (IPG)**, delivering continuous, programmable, high-frequency electrical pulses to modulate pathological neuronal firing patterns in disorders of the basal ganglia-thalamo-cortical loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard DBS Targets & Indications:**
>
> | Target Nucleus | Primary Clinical Indication |
> |---|---|
> | Subthalamic Nucleus (STN) | Parkinson's Disease, advanced |
> | Globus Pallidus interna (GPi) | Parkinson's Dyskinesia, Dystonia |
> | Ventral Intermediate Nucleus (Vim) of Thalamus | Essential Tremor |
> | Anterior Limb of Internal Capsule / Nucleus Accumbens | Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) |

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Brain Pacemaker":**
Think of the basal ganglia as an orchestra where the "tremor section" (thalamus) keeps playing out of sync. DBS acts like a **highly precise metronome tapping the violins (STN/GPi)**, forcing the entire orchestra back into tempo. The music is the same, but the chaotic tremolo effect is dampened.

## 1.3 Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) for Foot Drop

**Formal Definition:**
Foot Drop is a neuromuscular gait pathology characterized by the **inability to dorsiflex the foot** during the swing phase of gait, typically arising from upper motor neuron lesions (stroke, MS, SCI, cerebral palsy). **FES for foot drop** is a corrective neuroprosthetic intervention in which **timed electrical stimulation** is applied to the **common peroneal (fibular) nerve** (or directly to the tibialis anterior muscle), producing active dorsiflexion synchronized with the gait cycle, thereby eliminating the need for a passive ankle-foot orthosis (AFO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Commercial "Benchmarks" of FES for Foot Drop (Know these for KTU exams):**
> 1. **WalkAide** — Single-channel surface stimulator with tilt-sensor (no heel switch).
> 2. **Bioness L300** — Wireless cuff-based surface stimulator with foot sensor.
> 3. **ActiGait (Ottobock)** — Implanted peroneal stimulator with external control unit.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Automated Door-Light":**
A person with foot drop has a "bulb" in the leg that won't light up automatically when the heel lifts. The FES device is a **motion-sensing smart switch** — when it detects the heel leaving the floor, it instantly sends a small current to flash the bulb (peroneal nerve → tibialis anterior), lifting the foot just in time for the swing phase. When the heel strikes the ground again, the switch turns the light off, allowing the foot to drop safely for the stance phase.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Neural Stimulation Frequency Bands:** Sensory (1–100 Hz) | Motor (1–50 Hz) | Brain (10–250 Hz)
- **Typical SCS Frequency:** **40–60 Hz** (tonic) | **500–10,000 Hz** (high-frequency sub-perception)
- **Typical DBS Frequency:** **>100 Hz** (high-frequency, **130–185 Hz** standard)
- **Foot Drop FES Parameters:** **20–50 Hz** | PW **100–300 μs** | Amplitude **15–50 mA**
- **Tissue Impedance (epidural):** **~500–1500 Ω** for SCS
- **Charge per Phase Safety Limit (Shannon Limit):** **log(D) + log(Q) ≤ 1.85**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS) — Operational Subsystems

The SCS system operates on **four integrated subsystems**:

1. **Pulse Generator (IPG):** A battery-powered (or rechargeable) titanium-housed electronic circuit producing biphasic, charge-balanced constant-current pulses. Modern IPGs are MRI-conditional (up to 1.5T/3T).
2. **Lead Wire (Electrode Array):** Typically 8–16 platinum-iridium (Pt-Ir) contacts in a percutaneous or paddle configuration. Paddle leads offer directional steering and lower migration rates.
3. **Programming System:** External clinician telemetry unit to set pulse width, frequency, amplitude, electrode configuration (anode/cathode polarity).
4. **Patient Controller:** Handheld device allowing on/off control and limited parameter adjustment within clinician-set safety envelopes.

### 2.1.1 The Gate Control Theory — Why SCS Works

> The theory postulates that activation of large-diameter **A-β fibers** (touch/proprioception) excites inhibitory interneurons in **Lamina II (substantia gelatinosa)** of the dorsal horn. These interneurons release GABA and enkephalins, which **hyperpolarize projection neurons in Lamina V**, preventing nociceptive C-fiber and A-δ-fiber signals from ascending via the spinothalamic tract to the thalamus and somatosensory cortex.

### 2.1.2 SCS Stimulation Parameters

- **Pulse Width:** **30–450 μs** (typical: 210–450 μs for tonic SCS)
- **Frequency:** **40–60 Hz (tonic)** | 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 10 kHz (HF-SCS, sub-perception)
- **Amplitude:** **0.5–12 mA** (constant current mode preferred)
- **Electrode Configuration:** Monopolar, bipolar, or multipolar with fractionalized current steering

### 2.1.3 Clinical Applications of SCS

- Failed Back Surgery Syndrome (FBSS) — **FDA approved 1995**
- Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS Types I & II)
- Refractory angina pectoris
- Peripheral ischemic limb pain
- Arachnoiditis, radiculopathy
- Non-surgical refractory back/leg pain

## 2.2 Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) — Operational Subsystems

### 2.2.1 Components of a DBS System

1. **Electrode (Lead):** Quadripolar Pt-Ir electrode (4 contacts, 1.5 mm contact length, 0.5–1.5 mm spacing) — **Model 3387 (Medtronic)** for STN targeting.
2. **Extension Wire:** Tunneled subcutaneously connecting the lead to the IPG.
3. **Implantable Pulse Generator (IPG):** Placed in infraclavicular subcutaneous pocket. Single-channel or dual-channel (for bilateral stimulation).
4. **Patient Programmer:** Telemetry-based handheld device.

### 2.2.2 Surgical Workflow

> The procedure is performed using **stereotactic neurosurgery** with a Leksell frame, guided by:
> - Pre-operative **MRI (T2-weighted)** for nucleus localization
> - Intra-operative **microelectrode recording (MER)** to confirm physiological target
> - Intra-operative **macro-stimulation testing** for clinical effect assessment and side-effect thresholds

### 2.2.3 DBS Stimulation Parameters

- **Frequency:** **>100 Hz** (standard 130–185 Hz)
- **Pulse Width:** **60–200 μs**
- **Amplitude:** **1–5 V** (constant voltage) or 1–4 mA (constant current)
- **Mode:** Continuous (most common), cycling, or interleaving for multi-target

### 2.2.4 Mechanism of Action

The exact mechanism remains debated but the dominant hypotheses are:

- **Inhibition Hypothesis:** High-frequency DBS inhibits abnormal bursting neurons.
- **Excitation Hypothesis:** DBS activates afferent/efferent axons, overriding pathological oscillations.
- **Information Lesion Hypothesis:** DBS replaces pathological low-frequency oscillations with regularized high-frequency output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For PD tremor suppression, DBS frequency **must exceed 100 Hz**; below this, stimulation may worsen tremor. This is a high-frequency-dependence principle examiners frequently test.

### 2.2.5 Clinical Applications of DBS

- Parkinson's disease (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, motor fluctuations)
- Essential tremor
- Dystonia (primary generalized)
- Epilepsy (responsive neurostimulation — RNS)
- Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (HDE approval)
- Treatment-resistant depression (investigational)
- Tourette syndrome (investigational)
- Chronic pain (ventral posterolateral/medial thalamic stimulation)

## 2.3 ES for Foot Drop — Subsystem Architecture

### 2.3.1 FES Foot Drop System Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Controller** | Microprocessor-based unit generating biphasic pulses |
| **Trigger Source** | Force-sensing resistor (FSR) heel switch OR tilt/inclinometer |
| **Electrodes** | Surface (carbon-rubber, hydrogel) OR implanted (e.g., ActiGait cuff) |
| **Stimulating Nerve** | Common peroneal (fibular) nerve at fibular head |
| **Target Muscle** | Tibialis anterior (primary dorsiflexor) + peroneals (evertors) |
| **Power Source** | 9 V battery / rechargeable Li-ion |

### 2.3.2 Gait Cycle Synchronization Logic

The FES device must synchronize stimulation with the **gait cycle** (~1.0–1.4 s normal cadence):

- **Heel-Off (Gait Cycle ~60%):** Sensor detects heel rise → Stimulator ON → Tibialis anterior contracts → Foot dorsiflexes
- **Heel-Strike (Gait Cycle ~0%):** Sensor detects heel contact → Stimulator OFF → Foot relaxes for weight acceptance

### 2.3.3 Stimulation Parameters for Foot Drop

- **Waveform:** Asymmetric biphasic (charge-balanced)
- **Pulse Width:** **100–300 μs**
- **Frequency:** **20–50 Hz** (typically 25–33 Hz to avoid tetanic fatigue)
- **Amplitude:** **15–50 mA** (surface); 1–5 mA (implanted)
- **Ramp-Up Time:** **50–200 ms** (smooth onset)
- **Ramp-Down Time:** **50–200 ms** (smooth offset)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Critical Equations for Exam Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Master Equations Every Rehabilitation Engineer Must Know:**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = I \times t$ | Charge per phase | Coulombs (C) |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Charge density | μC/cm² |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | mA/cm² |
| 4 | $I_{th} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}$ | Strength-Duration (Weiss) equation | mA |
| 5 | $\log(D) + \log(Q) \leq 1.85$ | Shannon Safety Limit | Dimensionless |

**Strength-Duration Curve Derivation Logic:**

The strength-duration equation describes the **threshold current** required to depolarize a neuron of membrane time constant $\tau$. Rheobase ($I_{rh}$) is the minimum current for an infinitely long pulse. Chronaxie ($t_c$) is the pulse width at twice the rheobase.

> **Rheobase ($I_{rh}$):** Minimum current amplitude needed to excite a nerve when pulse duration is very long.
> **Chronaxie ($t_c$):** Pulse duration at which the threshold current is **exactly twice the rheobase**. It is the most clinically used index of nerve excitability.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SCS Engineers** design biocompatible, MRI-safe hermetically-sealed IPGs and current-steering algorithms.
- **DBS Engineers** develop directional leads (segmented electrodes) for shaped stimulation fields to avoid side-effects.
- **FES Foot Drop Engineers** focus on miniaturization, sensor fusion (IMU + FSR), and closed-loop control to reduce falls in stroke patients — a market projected at **>\$2 billion annually** in assistive gait devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Strength-Duration Equation (Lapicque / Weiss Model)

The **strength-duration curve** is foundational for both SCS electrode design and FES parameter selection. We derive it rigorously.

### 3.1.1 The Cable Equation of a Nerve Membrane

Consider a cylindrical axon modeled as a **membrane capacitance $C_m$** in parallel with a **membrane resistance $R_m$**. The membrane time constant is:

$$
\tau = R_m \cdot C_m
$$

Applying Kirchhoff's current law, the total membrane current has two components: a **capacitive current** (charging) and an **ionic leakage current** (resistive).

$$
I_m(t) = \frac{V_m}{R_m} + C_m \cdot \frac{dV_m}{dt}
$$

### 3.1.2 Solving the Differential Equation

For a **constant stimulating current pulse** $I$ applied at $t = 0$ with initial condition $V_m(0) = 0$, the first-order ODE solution is:

$$
V_m(t) = I \cdot R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

> **Interpretation:** The membrane voltage rises **exponentially** toward its asymptote $I \cdot R_m$ with time constant $\tau$.

### 3.1.3 Threshold Condition

The neuron fires when $V_m$ reaches the **threshold potential $V_{th}$**:

$$
V_{th} = I_{th} \cdot R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

Solving for the **threshold current $I_{th}$**:

$$
I_{th} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}
$$

> **Defining Rheobase:** As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term $\to 0$, so $I_{th}$ approaches its minimum value:

$$
I_{rh} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m}
$$

### 3.1.4 The Final Strength-Duration Equation

Substituting $I_{rh}$ back into the threshold equation:

$$
I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}
$$

This is the **Weiss Equation** (1901), universally cited in clinical FES and SCS literature.

### 3.1.5 Deriving the Chronaxie Relation

Setting $I_{th} = 2 \cdot I_{rh}$ and solving for $t = t_c$:

$$
2 \cdot I_{rh} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t_c/\tau}}
$$

$$
2 \left(1 - e^{-t_c/\tau}\right) = 1
$$

$$
1 - e^{-t_c/\tau} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
e^{-t_c/\tau} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_c = \tau \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \, \tau}
$$

> **Clinical Interpretation of Chronaxie:**
> - Large myelinated A-β fibers: $t_c \approx 30\text{–}70$ μs
> - Smaller A-δ fibers: $t_c \approx 100\text{–}200$ μs
> - FES foot drop uses PW = **~300 μs** to ensure recruitment of motor fibers, slightly above chronaxie to minimize total charge.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: FES Foot Drop Charge Calculation

> **Problem:** A foot-drop FES device delivers a biphasic, charge-balanced pulse at **$I = 25$ mA** with a **pulse width $t = 300$ μs** through a surface electrode of area **$A = 9$ cm²** (3 cm × 3 cm). Compute (a) charge per phase, (b) charge density, (c) current density, and (d) verify against the Shannon safety limit assuming a 33 Hz repetition rate.

### Step (a) — Charge per Phase

$$
Q = I \times t = 25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} \times 300 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
Q = 7.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{C} = 7.5 \,\mu\text{C}
$$

### Step (b) — Charge Density

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{7.5 \,\mu\text{C}}{9 \,\text{cm}^2} = 0.833 \,\frac{\mu\text{C}}{\text{cm}^2}
$$

### Step (c) — Current Density

$$
J = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{25 \,\text{mA}}{9 \,\text{cm}^2} = 2.78 \,\frac{\text{mA}}{\text{cm}^2}
$$

### Step (d) — Shannon Safety Verification

Shannon (1992) defined the **safe-stimulation envelope** using charge per phase $Q$ and charge density $D$. The limit is:

$$
\log(D) + \log(Q) \leq 1.85
$$

Note: $D$ and $Q$ must be in their standard units ($\mu$C/cm² and $\mu$C respectively). Computing:

$$
\log(0.833) + \log(7.5) = (-0.079) + (0.875) = 0.796
$$

Since $0.796 \leq 1.85$ ✓, the stimulation is **within safe limits**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: SCS vs. DBS vs. FES Foot Drop

| Parameter | **SCS** | **DBS** | **FES Foot Drop** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Epidural surgical implant | Stereotactic intracranial implant | Surface (non-invasive) or implanted cuff |
| **Target Tissue** | Dorsal columns of SC | STN / GPi / Vim (deep nuclei) | Common peroneal nerve / TA muscle |
| **Typical Frequency** | 40–60 Hz (tonic) | 130–185 Hz | 20–50 Hz |
| **Pulse Width** | 210–450 μs | 60–200 μs | 100–300 μs |
| **Amplitude** | 0.5–12 mA | 1–5 V / 1–4 mA | 15–50 mA (surface) |
| **Mechanism** | Neuromodulation (Gate Control) | Modulation of basal ganglia oscillations | Direct motor unit recruitment → functional movement |
| **Trigger** | Continuous (patient on/off) | Continuous (24/7) | Heel-switch / tilt-sensor / finite state machine |
| **Primary Outcome** | Pain reduction (≥50%) | Tremor/rigidity reduction | Improved gait symmetry, speed, fall prevention |
| **Power Source** | Rechargeable IPG (10 yr) | Non-rechargeable IPG (3–5 yr) | External battery (rechargeable) |
| **MRI Compatibility** | MRI-conditional 1.5/3T | MRI-conditional 1.5T (specific protocols) | N/A (external) |
| **Risk Profile** | Lead migration, IPG site pain | Intracranial hemorrhage (~1–2%) | Skin irritation, electrode fatigue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Observe the **inverse relationship between target depth and stimulation frequency** — deeper structures (DBS) require higher frequencies to override pathological oscillations, while peripheral nerve/muscle FES uses lower frequencies closer to natural motor unit firing rates.

## 3.4 Closed-Loop FES for Foot Drop — State Machine

A modern FES foot drop device (e.g., Bioness L300 Go) operates on a **4-state finite state machine** synchronized to the gait cycle:

$$
\text{STATE}_{1} \rightarrow \text{STATE}_{2} \rightarrow \text{STATE}_{3} \rightarrow \text{STATE}_{4} \rightarrow \text{STATE}_{1}
$$

- **STATE 1 — Heel-Strike (Loading Response, 0–10% GC):** FSR pressure > threshold → Stim OFF, foot relaxed
- **STATE 2 — Mid-Stance (10–30% GC):** FSR pressure still high → Stim OFF
- **STATE 3 — Heel-Off (30–60% GC):** FSR pressure drops → Stim RAMP-UP over 100 ms
- **STATE 4 — Swing Phase (60–100% GC):** No heel contact → Stim ON, dorsiflexion maintained

The trigger hysteresis is typically set at **~10%** of FSR range to prevent rapid oscillation during slow walking.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS) and Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) in terms of (a) target site, (b) typical frequency range, and (c) primary clinical application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **SCS** | **DBS** |
|---|---|---|
| Target site | Epidural space over dorsal columns of spinal cord | Deep brain nuclei (STN, GPi, Vim) |
| Frequency | 40–60 Hz (tonic) | 130–185 Hz (high frequency) |
| Primary application | Chronic neuropathic pain (FBSS, CRPS) | Movement disorders (Parkinson's, tremor, dystonia) |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any three commercial FES systems available for the correction of foot drop and state the nerve stimulated in each case.**

**Model Answer:**
The three commercial FES systems for foot drop are:
1. **WalkAide** (Innovative Neurotronics) — Single-channel surface stimulator; stimulates the **common peroneal nerve**.
2. **Bioness L300** (Bioness Inc.) — Wireless cuff-based surface system; stimulates the **common peroneal nerve**.
3. **ActiGait** (Ottobock) — Implanted cuff electrode; stimulates the **common peroneal nerve** directly.

> In all three systems, the **common peroneal (fibular) nerve** is the stimulation target, producing contraction of the **tibialis anterior** and **peroneus muscles**, which are the primary dorsiflexors/evertors of the foot.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Describe in detail the principle, electrode placement, stimulation parameters, and clinical applications of Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** SCS operates on the **Gate Control Theory of Pain** (Melzack & Wall, 1965). Electrical stimulation of **A-β fibers** in the dorsal columns activates inhibitory interneurons in the **substantia gelatinosa (Lamina II)** of the dorsal horn, releasing GABA and enkephalins that hyperpolarize projection neurons in **Lamina V**. This "closes the gate" on nociceptive C and A-δ fiber transmission, preventing pain signals from ascending via the spinothalamic tract to the thalamus and somatosensory cortex.

**Electrode Placement:** A **percutaneous lead (cylindrical)** or **paddle lead (surgical laminotomy)** with 8–16 Pt-Ir contacts is advanced into the **epidural space** under fluoroscopic guidance, typically positioned at the **cervical or thoracic level** corresponding to the dermatome of pain origin (commonly T7–T10 for lower back/leg pain).

**Stimulation Parameters:**
- Waveform: Biphasic, charge-balanced
- Pulse width: 210–450 μs
- Frequency: 40–60 Hz (tonic), 1 kHz, 5 kHz, 10 kHz (HF sub-perception)
- Amplitude: 0.5–12 mA (constant current)
- Configuration: Monopolar/bipolar with current steering

**Clinical Applications:**
- Failed Back Surgery Syndrome (FBSS)
- Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS)
- Refractory angina pectoris
- Peripheral ischemic pain
- Arachnoiditis, chronic radiculopathy

**(b) Explain the principle of operation and components of Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) system used for Parkinson's disease. State any two clinical targets and their corresponding indications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** DBS delivers high-frequency (>100 Hz) electrical pulses to specific deep brain nuclei via stereotactically implanted electrodes. The high-frequency stimulation **overrides pathological low-frequency beta-band oscillations** in the basal ganglia-thalamo-cortical circuit, effectively acting as an "**information lesion**" that suppresses abnormal firing patterns. The mechanism is hypothesized to involve inhibition of hyperactive neurons (e.g., STN) and disruption of pathological synchrony.

**Components:**
1. **Quadripolar lead electrode** (Pt-Ir, 4 contacts, Model 3387/3389) implanted in the deep brain nucleus.
2. **Extension wire** tunneled subcutaneously.
3. **Implantable Pulse Generator (IPG)** placed in the infraclavicular pocket.
4. **Patient programmer** for on/off control.
5. **Clinician programmer** for parameter setting via telemetry.

**Two Clinical Targets & Indications:**
- **Subthalamic Nucleus (STN):** Primary target for **advanced Parkinson's disease** (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, motor fluctuations). Allows >50% reduction in levodopa dose.
- **Ventral Intermediate Nucleus (Vim) of Thalamus:** Primary target for **essential tremor** and **Parkinsonian tremor**.

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) system for correction of foot drop. Discuss the gait-cycle synchronization logic. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of FES Foot Drop System:**

```
Heel Switch (FSR Sensor) ──► Microcontroller ──► Pulse Generator
                                                          │
Tilt Sensor (IMU) ──────────► Microcontroller            │
                                                          ▼
                                       Surface Electrodes (cathode at fibular head,
                                                          anode 5 cm distal)
                                                          │
                                                          ▼
                                       Common Peroneal Nerve ──► Tibialis Anterior
                                                          │
                                                          ▼
                                       Foot Dorsiflexion (Swing Phase)
```

**Working:** A **force-sensing resistor (FSR) heel switch** inside the patient's shoe detects pressure changes during walking. When the heel lifts off the ground (initiating the swing phase), the FSR signal drops below a preset threshold, and the **microcontroller** triggers a **biphasic, charge-balanced pulse train** from the pulse generator. This current is delivered via **surface electrodes** placed over the **common peroneal nerve at the fibular head**, activating the **tibialis anterior** muscle, which contracts to dorsiflex the foot. When the heel strikes the ground again, the FSR signal rises, and stimulation is turned OFF to allow weight-bearing.

**Gait-Cycle Synchronization Logic:**

| Gait Phase | % of Cycle | FSR Reading | Stimulus Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heel-Strike / Loading Response | 0–10% | HIGH | OFF |
| Mid-Stance | 10–30% | HIGH | OFF |
| Heel-Off / Terminal Stance | 30–60% | DROPS | RAMP-UP |
| Swing Phase | 60–100% | LOW | ON |

Ramp-up time = **50–200 ms** ensures smooth onset; ramp-down time = **50–200 ms** ensures smooth offset.

**(b) Derive the strength-duration equation. A stimulating electrode delivers a current of 30 mA with a pulse width of 250 μs. If the rheobase current is 6 mA, calculate the membrane time constant and the chronaxie of the nerve. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.) The strength-duration equation is:

$$
I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}
$$

**Given:**
- $I_{th} = 30$ mA
- $I_{rh} = 6$ mA
- $t = 250 \,\mu\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the equation:**

$$
30 = \frac{6}{1 - e^{-250 \times 10^{-6}/\tau}}
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the exponential term:**

$$
1 - e^{-250 \times 10^{-6}/\tau} = \frac{6}{30} = 0.2
$$

$$
e^{-250 \times 10^{-6}/\tau} = 0.8
$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
-\frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{\tau} = \ln(0.8) = -0.2231
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for τ:**

$$
\tau = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2231} = 1.12 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{s} = 1.12 \,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Chronaxie:**

$$
t_c = \tau \cdot \ln(2) = 1.12 \,\text{ms} \times 0.693 = 0.776 \,\text{ms} = 776 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The relatively long chronaxie (~776 μs) suggests **partially demyelinated or small-diameter fibers** consistent with a chronic neuropathy. Clinically, the stimulation pulse width of 250 μs is well **below the chronaxie**, meaning the device is operating efficiently with reasonable charge delivery.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the chronaxie relationship:** Students often write $t_c = \tau$ instead of $t_c = \tau \cdot \ln(2)$. The factor of **0.693** is the *most commonly missed** mark**.
> 2. **Unit errors in FES calculations:** Always convert μs to seconds ($1 \,\mu\text{s} = 10^{-6}$ s) before substituting.
> 3. **Misidentifying the gait-cycle trigger:** The heel switch is the **most clinically used** trigger; tilt sensors are an alternative but examiners prefer heel-switch references.
> 4. **Incorrect target nucleus for DBS in PD:** The **STN** is the most common target, NOT the GPi (which is preferred for dystonia).
> 5. **Pulse waveform omission:** Always specify "**biphasic, charge-balanced**" in FES — monophasic pulses cause DC electrolysis and tissue damage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

### **Spinal Cord Stimulation (SCS)**
- ✅ Target: **Dorsal columns of spinal cord** (epidural)
- ✅ Mechanism: **Gate Control Theory** (Melzack & Wall, 1965)
- ✅ Frequency: **40–60 Hz** (tonic) | HF: 1 kHz, 5 kHz, 10 kHz
- ✅ Pulse Width: **210–450 μs**
- ✅ Application: **Chronic neuropathic pain** (FBSS, CRPS)
- ✅ Lead types: **Percutaneous (cylindrical)** vs **Paddle (surgical)**

### **Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS)**
- ✅ Target Nuclei: **STN** (PD), **GPi** (Dystonia), **Vim** (Tremor)
- ✅ Frequency: **>100 Hz** (130–185 Hz typical)
- ✅ Pulse Width: **60–200 μs**
- ✅ Surgical: **Stereotactic** with MER
- ✅ Risks: **Intracranial hemorrhage (~1–2%)**
- ✅ Mechanism: **Information Lesion Hypothesis**

### **FES for Foot Drop**
- ✅ Stimulated nerve: **Common peroneal (fibular)**
- ✅ Target muscle: **Tibialis anterior**
- ✅ Trigger: **Heel switch (FSR)** or **Tilt sensor (IMU)**
- ✅ Parameters: **20–50 Hz**, PW **100–300 μs**, Amplitude **15–50 mA**
- ✅ Commercial systems: **WalkAide, Bioness L300, ActiGait**
- ✅ Waveform: **Biphasic, charge-balanced** with **ramp-up/ramp-down**

### **Universal Stimulation Equations**
- ✅ $Q = I \times t$ (charge per phase)
- ✅ $I_{th} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}$ (strength-duration)
- ✅ $t_c = \tau \cdot \ln(2)$ (chronaxie)
- ✅ $\log(D) + \log(Q) \leq 1.85$ (Shannon safety limit)

### **High-Yield One-Liners for KTU Viva**
- "SCS treats pain; DBS treats movement; FES foot drop treats gait."
- "Chronaxie = 0.693 × Time constant."
- "DBS needs high frequency (>100 Hz); low frequency worsens tremor."
- "FES foot drop must synchronize to gait cycle — heel switch ON at swing."
- "Shannon limit ensures safe charge delivery — k = 1.85."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultralow Power Electronics for SCS and DBS - Biomimetic Design of Neural Prostheses - Tissue-Device Interface - Neuromodulation through Stimulation - Sensory Restoration - Motor Restoration - Integrated Neural Prostheses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 2 — Neural Prostheses: Power, Interface & Integrated Design

## 1.1 Formal Definition & Scope (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neural Prostheses** are *bidirectional or unidirectional bioelectronic devices* that substitute, modulate, or restore the function of a damaged portion of the nervous system by means of **controlled electrical, optical, or chemical stimulation** of excitable neural tissue, or by **recording neural activity** for feedback control.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT864), Module 2 demands an integrated understanding of **seven tightly-coupled sub-topics**:

1. **Ultralow Power Electronics for SCS (Spinal Cord Stimulation) and DBS (Deep Brain Stimulation).**
2. **Biomimetic Design of Neural Prostheses** — emulating the natural neural code.
3. **Tissue–Device Interface** — the electrochemical/biological boundary.
4. **Neuromodulation through Stimulation** — the biophysical mechanism of action.
5. **Sensory Restoration** — restoring sight, hearing, touch.
6. **Motor Restoration** — restoring limb, bladder, diaphragm control.
7. **Integrated Neural Prostheses** — closed-loop, multi-modal systems.

### 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The City Power Grid"**
> Think of the human nervous system as a **city's electrical grid**. A *neural prosthesis* is a **smart sub-station** that:
> 1. **Listens** to the traffic (recordings) on some lines.
> 2. **Re-routes or injects** current (stimulation) on damaged lines.
> 3. Does so using a **tiny battery** (ultralow power electronics) that must last **years**, not hours.
> 4. Connects to the city's copper wiring (tissue) via **specialized transformers** (the tissue–device interface) that prevent sparking, corrosion, and short-circuits.

---

### 1.2 Ultralow Power Electronics for SCS and DBS

**Formal Definition:** *Implantable, hermetically-sealed electronic subsystems designed to deliver precisely-controlled stimulation pulses with **sub-milliwatt average power dissipation**, typically < **100 µW** for chronic DBS and < **1 mW** for modern SCS devices, while operating from a primary cell or rechargeable Li-ion source for 5–10 years.*

**Why ultralow power matters (Key KTU point):**
- A typical DBS system delivers 130 Hz, 60–90 µs, ~3 mA pulses for **chronic therapy** in Parkinson's disease.
- Battery replacement requires **surgical explantation** → risk of infection, brain tissue trauma, and cost.
- Energy efficiency directly determines **device longevity** and **patient safety** (heating < 2 °C tissue rise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Metric:** Tissue heating is governed by the **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)** and the **power density** at the electrode-tissue interface. The IEEE C95.1 / ICNIRP limit is **SAR ≤ 10 W/kg** averaged over 10 g of tissue; implantable devices must stay orders of magnitude below this.

### 1.3 Biomimetic Design of Neural Prostheses

**Definition:** *Design philosophy wherein the prosthesis replicates the **spatiotemporal pattern, amplitude, and frequency** of natural neural activity, so that the central nervous system (CNS) interprets the artificial input as physiological rather than as a noxious or meaningless signal.*

### 1.4 Tissue–Device Interface

**Definition:** *The region — typically 100 nm to 1 mm — where the **electrode** (an electronic conductor, e.g., Pt-Ir, TiN, PEDOT:PSS) meets the **electrolyte** (interstitial fluid + neural tissue), forming an **electrode-electrolyte interface** characterized by the **Helmholtz double layer**, **Faradaic reactions**, and a **specific impedance** (typically 1–10 kΩ at 1 kHz for microelectrodes).*

### 1.5 Neuromodulation through Stimulation

**Definition:** *The alteration of nerve activity through targeted delivery of a stimulus (electrical, optical, or chemical) to specific neural sites, producing long-term therapeutic benefits (e.g., pain suppression, tremor reduction) via **suppression**, **excitation**, or **block** of action potential propagation.*

### 1.6 Sensory Restoration

**Definition:** *Replacement of lost sensory input by delivering patterned electrical stimuli to surviving neural structures:*
- **Cochlear implants (CI)** → auditory nerve.
- **Retinal prostheses (e.g., Argus II, PRIMA)** → retinal ganglion cells.
- **Cortical visual prostheses** → V1 / lateral geniculate nucleus.
- **Somatosensory cortex stimulation** → for touch and proprioception in amputees.

### 1.7 Motor Restoration

**Definition:** *Restoration of voluntary or functional movement by:* 
- **FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation)** — stimulating paralyzed muscle.
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — decoding motor intent from M1 / premotor cortex.
- **Brain–Machine–Spinal Interfaces** — bridging brain and spinal cord after injury.

### 1.8 Integrated Neural Prostheses

**Definition:** *Closed-loop, multi-modal systems combining sensing (recording), processing (DSP/ML), and actuation (stimulation) on a single implant with bidirectional telemetry, exemplified by the BrainGate, NeuroPace RNS, and Medtronic Percept PC systems.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strength–Duration Curve (Weiss–Lapicque Law)

This is the **single most important equation** for Module 2. It defines the threshold current required to excite a nerve as a function of pulse width.

**Theoretical Steps:**

1. Nerve membrane behaves as an **RC circuit** (membrane resistance $R_m$ and capacitance $C_m$ in parallel).
2. To reach threshold voltage $V_{th}$, the stimulating current $I$ must deposit charge $Q = I \cdot t$ such that:

$$V_{th} = \frac{I \cdot R_m \cdot t}{t + \tau_m} \quad \text{where } \tau_m = R_m C_m$$

3. Setting $I = I_{th}$ and solving for $I_{th}$ as a function of $t$:

$$I_{th}(t) = I_{rh} \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$$

where:
- $I_{rh}$ = **Rheobase current** (minimum DC current to excite) — units: **Amperes (A)**.
- $\tau_{ch}$ = **Chronaxie** (pulse width at which threshold = 2·Rheobase) — units: **seconds (s)**.
- $t$ = pulse width.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** *Rheobase* = the **base flow** of a river; *Chronaxie* = the **time** it takes. For mammalian myelinated axons: $\tau_{ch} \approx 30{-}500$ µs; $I_{rh} \approx 0.1{-}10$ mA.

**Engineering Utility:** The chronaxie dictates the **energy-optimal pulse width**. The minimum energy point on the strength–duration curve is at $t = 1.255 \cdot \tau_{ch}$.

---

## 2.2 Charge Per Phase & Charge Density

To prevent **Faradaic electrochemical damage** (pH shift, metal dissolution, toxic product generation), the **charge per phase** must remain below the **kasser-petersen safe limit**:

$$Q_{phase} = I \cdot t_{pulse} \quad [\text{Coulombs, C}]$$

**Charge Density (a true KTU favorite):**

$$k_c = \frac{Q_{phase}}{A_{electrode}} = \frac{I \cdot t_{pulse}}{A} \quad \left[\mu C / cm^2\right]**

**Safety limits (Shannon-Packenjarow):**

$$k = \log_{10}(k_c) + \log_{10}(k_d) \quad \text{must be} \le 1.85$$

where $k_d$ is charge density in $\mu C / cm^2/phase$ × pulse-train phase, and $k_c$ is $\mu C / cm^2$ × (frequency in Hz). Exceeding this causes **tissue damage**.

---

## 2.3 Power Budget of an Implanted Pulse Generator (IPG)

The **average power** consumed by the IPG is:

$$P_{avg} = V_{supply} \cdot I_{supply,avg} \cdot \eta^{-1} + P_{leak} + P_{telem}$$

For a **capacitively-coupled, biphasic, current-controlled** stimulator:

$$P_{stim,avg} = I_{stim}^2 \cdot Z_{tissue} \cdot t_{pulse} \cdot f_{stim}$$

For DBS typical: $I = 3$ mA, $Z \approx 1$ kΩ, $t = 60$ µs, $f = 130$ Hz:

$$P_{stim} = (3 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 60 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 130 = 7.02 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 70.2 \text{ µW}$$

---

## 2.4 Tissue Impedance Model (Cole–Cole / Randles Circuit)

The electrode–tissue interface is modeled by the **Randles equivalent circuit**:

- $R_s$ = bulk tissue/electrolyte resistance (series) ≈ **10–500 Ω**.
- $C_{dl}$ = double-layer capacitance (Helmholtz) ≈ **1–100 µF/cm²**.
- $R_{ct}$ = charge-transfer resistance (Faradaic) ≈ **1–100 kΩ**.
- $Z_w$ = Warburg impedance (diffusion) = $\sigma (j\omega)^{-1/2}$.

The total impedance magnitude at frequency $\omega$ is:

$$Z(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^{\alpha}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **Cole–Cole exponent** (0.5–1.0).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Units |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$ | Weiss-Lapicque strength-duration | A, s |
| 2 | $V_{m}(t) = V_{th} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$ | Membrane voltage rise to threshold | V, s |
| 3 | $Q_{phase} = I \cdot t_{pulse}$ | Charge per phase | Coulombs |
| 4 | $k_c = \dfrac{I \cdot t_{pulse}}{A_{elec}}$ | Charge density | µC/cm² |
| 5 | $k = \log_{10} k_d + \log_{10} k_c \le 1.85$ | Shannon-Packenjarow safety | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{avg} = I_{stim}^2 \cdot Z_{tissue} \cdot t_{p} \cdot f$ | Stimulation power | W |
| 7 | $E_{pulse} = \int V \cdot I \, dt$ | Energy per pulse | J |
| 8 | $Z(\omega) = R_s + \dfrac{R_{ct}}{1 + (j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^{\alpha}}$ | Randles interface impedance | Ω |
| 9 | $R_{inj} = V_{supply}/I_{max}$ | Compliance-limited load | Ω |
| 10 | $\eta = P_{stim}/P_{total}$ | Energy efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 11 | $SAR = \dfrac{\sigma \vert E \vert^2}{2 \rho}$ | Specific absorption rate | W/kg |
| 12 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Tissue heating (adiabatic) | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas govern the design of every commercial implant — from the **Medtronic Activa PC** (DBS) to the **Cochlear Nucleus 7** (CI) to **Abbott Proclaim XR** (SCS). Power budget determines battery size; charge density determines electrode material; SAR determines antenna placement for telemetry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Strength–Duration Equation

We start from the **cable equation** for a passive nerve membrane, treating the membrane as a parallel RC circuit.

**Step 1 — Membrane Model**

The membrane patch has:
- Membrane resistance $R_m$ (Ω·m²)
- Membrane capacitance $C_m$ (F/m²)

A stimulating current $I_s$ applied extracellularly injects charge onto $C_m$ and through $R_m$:

$$I_s = \frac{V_m}{R_m} + C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the First-Order ODE**

Rearranging into standard form:

$$\frac{dV_m}{dt} + \frac{V_m}{R_m C_m} = \frac{I_s}{C_m}$$

This is a linear ODE. The integrating factor is $e^{t/(R_m C_m)}$. Let $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ (membrane time constant).

Multiplying both sides:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[V_m e^{t/\tau_m}\right] = \frac{I_s}{C_m} e^{t/\tau_m}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$, with $V_m(0) = 0$ (resting):

$$V_m(t) e^{t/\tau_m} - 0 = \frac{I_s}{C_m} \int_0^t e^{\xi/\tau_m} d\xi$$

$$V_m(t) = \frac{I_s R_m \tau_m}{C_m \tau_m} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right) = I_s R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Threshold Condition**

For an action potential to fire, $V_m$ must reach $V_{th}$. For long pulses ($t \gg \tau_m$), the exponential term vanishes:

$$V_{th} = I_{rh} R_m \implies I_{rh} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m}$$

This is the **rheobase current** — the minimum DC value to ever fire the neuron.

**Step 4 — Solve for $I_{th}(t)$**

Setting $V_m(t) = V_{th}$ and $I_s = I_{th}$:

$$V_{th} = I_{th} R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

$$I_{th} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Weiss–Lapicque Form**

Using the identity $1 - e^{-x} = \frac{x}{1 + x/2 + \cdots} \approx \frac{x}{1 + x}$ (for small deviations), and letting $\tau_{ch} = \tau_m / \ln 2 \approx 1.44 \tau_m$:

$$\boxed{\,I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)\,}$$

✅ **Verification:** When $t \to \infty$: $I_{th} \to I_{rh}$ ✔️. When $t = \tau_{ch}$: $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$ ✔️. Q.E.D.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Optimal Pulse Width for Minimum Energy

**Step 1 — Energy Per Phase**

For a monophasic rectangular pulse of current $I$ and width $t$ across load $Z$:

$$E = I^2 Z t = \frac{V^2}{Z} t$$

Substituting $I_{th}(t)$ from Weiss-Lapicque:

$$E_{th}(t) = I_{rh}^2 Z t \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Minimize with Respect to $t$**

Take derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{dE_{th}}{dt} = I_{rh}^2 Z \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)^2 + t \cdot 2\left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right) \left(-\frac{\tau_{ch}}{t^2}\right) \right] = 0$$

Let $u = 1 + \tau_{ch}/t$. Then:

$$u^2 - \frac{2 u \tau_{ch}}{t} = 0 \implies u \left(u - \frac{2\tau_{ch}}{t}\right) = 0$$

Since $u \neq 0$:

$$1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{t} = \frac{2\tau_{ch}}{t} \implies t = \tau_{ch}$$

Wait — let me redo. Substituting $u$ back:

$$1 = \frac{\tau_{ch}/t}{1} \implies \boxed{\,t_{opt} = \tau_{ch}\,}$$

So the **energy-optimal pulse width equals the chronaxie** — a beautiful result verified experimentally for both neural and cardiac stimulation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Stimulation Modalities

| Modality | Target | Typical f (Hz) | Pulse Width | Current | Power | Key Application |
|----------|--------|----------------|--------------|---------|-------|------------------|
| **SCS (Spinal Cord Stim)** | Dorsal column | 40–60 | 60–450 µs | 1–12 mA | 0.5–5 mW | Chronic pain, failed back surgery syndrome |
| **DBS (Deep Brain Stim)** | STN / GPi / VIM | 130–185 | 60–90 µs | 1–5 mA | 0.1–1 mW | Parkinson's, tremor, dystonia |
| **Cochlear Implant** | Auditory nerve | 250–5000 (pPS) | 25–100 µs | 0.1–2 mA | < 10 mW | Sensorineural hearing loss |
| **Retinal Prosthesis** | Retinal ganglion | 30–300 | 0.05–1 ms | 10–600 µA | < 50 mW | Retinitis pigmentosa, AMD |
| **FES (Motor)** | Muscle/nerve | 20–50 | 100–500 µs | 5–100 mA | 5–50 mW | Foot drop, grasp, standing |
| **Cortical Visual** | V1 cortex | 50–200 | 0.1–0.5 ms | 1–5 mA | 5–20 mW | Blindness (experimental) |
| **Vagus Nerve Stim (VNS)** | Left vagus | 20–30 | 250–500 µs | 0.25–3.5 mA | 0.1–1 mW | Epilepsy, depression |
| **Sacral Neuromod.** | S3 nerve root | 10–20 | 210 µs | 1–5 mA | < 1 mW | Incontinence, urinary retention |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Notice that the **smaller and deeper** the target (DBS targets STN ~7 cm deep), the **lower the current and frequency** — to minimize tissue heating and battery drain. The **larger and more superficial** the target (cortical visual, FES), the higher the current and power.

---

## 3.4 Charge Balance Derivation (Why Biphasic is Mandatory)

**Step 1 — DC Residue Problem**

If a monophasic pulse injects net DC charge $\Delta Q$ per cycle, electrolysis occurs:

$$2 H_2 O \xrightarrow{electrolysis} O_2 \uparrow + 4 H^+ + 4 e^- \quad (E^0 = +1.23 V)$$

**Step 2 — Charge-Balanced Biphasic Pulse**

The cathodic phase (stimulation, charge $-Q$) is followed by a charge-balanced anodic phase of opposite charge $+Q$:

$$Q_{cathode} = -I_c \cdot t_c \quad ; \quad Q_{anode} = +I_a \cdot t_a$$

Charge balance requires:

$$I_c \cdot t_c = I_a \cdot t_a \quad \implies \quad \Delta Q = 0$$

**Step 3 — DC Leakage Compensation**

In practice, mismatch $\Delta Q = 1{-}5$ % remains. This is corrected by a **DC-blocking capacitor** $C_{blk}$ in series with the electrode:

$$V_{C,blk} = \frac{\Delta Q}{C_{blk}}$$

Choosing $C_{blk} \ge 10$ µF limits $V_{C,blk}$ to < **100 mV** for $\Delta Q = 1$ µC, well below the water-window of **±1.23 V** vs Ag/AgCl.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Exam Tip:** Always mention *both* the **passive capacitor** method and the **active charge-balancing** (using a current-mirror / Howland source) when answering "explain charge balancing."

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Implantable Pulse Generator (IPG) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Battery Li-Ion 3.7V / Primary Cell] --> B[Voltage Regulator LDO 3.3V]
    B --> C[DSP Controller ARM Cortex-M0]
    C --> D[DAC 16-bit]
    D --> E[Current Source Howland Pump]
    E --> F[DC Block Capacitor 10uF]
    F --> G[Electrode-Tissue Interface]
    G --> H[Tissue C_m R_m Membrane]
    H --> I[Neural Action Potential]
    I --> J[Recording Amplifier 1000x]
    J --> K[ADC 16-bit 100 kSps]
    K --> L[Evoked Response Detector]
    L --> M[Feedback to DSP C]
    C --> N[Inductive Telemetry 400 kHz]
    N --> O[External Clinician Programmer]
    C --> P[Charge Balance Monitor]

    style C fill:#d6e0f9,stroke:#36c
    style G fill:#d6f9d6,stroke:#3a3
    style I fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a80
```

**Caption:** *Bidirectional IPG showing stimulation pathway (red), sensing pathway (blue), and feedback control (green). Note the DC-blocking capacitor, the charge-balance monitor, and the inductive telemetry link.*

---

## 4.2 Tissue–Device Interface — Electrochemical Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrode Metal Pt-Ir / TiN / PEDOT] --> B[Helmholtz Double Layer C_dl 1-100 uF/cm2]
    B --> C[Diffusion Layer Warburg Z_w]
    C --> D[Bulk Electrolyte Interstitial Fluid R_s]
    D --> E[Neural Tissue C_m R_m]
    E --> F[Axon Membrane V_m]

    style A fill:#c9d6e8
    style B fill:#fce5cd
    style C fill:#fff2cc
    style D fill:#d9ead3
    style E fill:#c9d6e8
    style F fill:#f4cccc
```

**Caption:** *Sequential model of the electrode → double layer → diffusion → electrolyte → membrane pathway. The double layer is the principal site of capacitive (non-Faradaic) charge transfer; below ±0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl, no electrolysis occurs.*

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Cochlear Implant (Sensory Restoration)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Microphone External] --> B[Sound Processor DSP]
    B --> C[Bandpass Filter Bank N=22]
    C --> D[Amplitude Envelope Detector]
    D --> E[Coding Strategy ACE / CIS]
    E --> F[RF Telemetry 5 MHz]
    F --> G[Receiver Coil Implanted]
    G --> H[DAC Current Stimulator]
    H --> I[Intra-cochlear Electrode Array 22 contacts]
    I --> J[Auditory Nerve Spiral Ganglion]
    J --> K[Brainstem Cochlear Nucleus]
    K --> L[Cortical Auditory Perception]

    style A fill:#d6e0f9
    style E fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a80
    style I fill:#f9d6d6
    style L fill:#d6f9d6
```

**Caption:** *Cochlear implant signal chain: acoustic → DSP → RF link → electrical → neural → perception. Note the **Continuous Interleaved Sampling (CIS)** strategy which presents non-simultaneous pulses to avoid channel interaction.*

---

## 4.4 Integrated Closed-Loop Neural Prosthesis (e.g., NeuroPace RNS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Brain ECoG Electrodes sEEG Depth] --> B[Analog Front End LNA 1000x]
    B --> C[Bandpass 1-500 Hz]
    C --> D[ADC 250 Hz Sampling]
    D --> E[Feature Extractor Line Length / Band Power]
    E --> F[Classifier SVM / Threshold]
    F --> G{Seizure Detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Trigger Stimulation Pulse 100-200 Hz]
    G -- No --> I[Continue Monitoring]
    H --> J[Current Source 1-12 mA Biphasic]
    J --> K[Electrodes Same ECoG Strip]
    K --> A

    style A fill:#c9d6e8
    style F fill:#fff3b0
    style G fill:#f4cccc
    style K fill:#d9ead3
```

**Caption:** *Closed-loop Responsive Neurostimulation (RNS) — the device senses epileptiform activity on cortical electrodes, classifies it in real-time, and delivers counter-stimulation, breaking the seizure circuit. This is the archetype of an **integrated neural prosthesis** combining sensing, processing, and actuation.*

---

## 4.5 Biomimetic Stimulation Waveform — Pattern Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Natural Neural Spike Train Recording] --> B[Temporal Pattern Analyzer ISI Histogram]
    B --> C[Replicate Engine: Stochastic / Poisson]
    C --> D[Biphasic Pulse Train 20-200 Hz]
    D --> E[Charge Balanced with Anodic First]
    E --> F[Neural Target Cochlear Nucleus / Cortex]
    F --> G[CNS Interprets as Natural]
```

**Caption:** *Biomimetic design philosophy: instead of fixed-frequency tonic stimulation, the device delivers a **stochastic spike train** that mimics the natural interspike-interval (ISI) distribution, producing more physiological CNS responses.*
