# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "chronaxie" and explain its engineering significance in the design of an implantable pulse generator for spinal cord stimulation.**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** | **CO2 | Bloom: Remember/Understand**

**Model Answer:**

The **chronaxie** ($\tau_{ch}$) is the *minimum pulse duration* at which a stimulus of amplitude equal to **twice the rheobase current** is required to excite a nerve fiber. It is the time constant of nerve excitability and is mathematically defined by the **Weiss–Lapicque equation**:

$$I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$$

Setting $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$ yields $t = \tau_{ch}$.

**Engineering Significance:**

1. **Energy-optimal pulse width:** Theoretical minimum stimulation energy is achieved when $t_{pulse} = \tau_{ch}$ (≈ 100–300 µs for SCS). Setting pulse widths shorter wastes energy in pulse-generator overhead; longer wastes energy in tissue heating.
2. **Recruitment selectivity:** Chronaxie varies with **fiber diameter** (large myelinated fibers: ~30 µs; small unmyelinated C-fibers: ~1 ms). Selective fiber recruitment is achieved by tuning $t_{pulse}$ to target a specific fiber population.
3. **Patient-specific titration:** In clinical programming of the Medtronic Intellis or Boston Scientific Spectra WaveWriter, the clinician adjusts pulse width first, then amplitude — based on the chronaxie–rheobase balance.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for engineering significance)

---

### **Q2. What is meant by "charge-balanced stimulation" and why is it mandatory in chronic neural implants?**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** | **CO2 | Bloom: Understand**

**Model Answer:**

**Charge-balanced stimulation** means that the **net DC charge** transferred to the tissue over one complete stimulation cycle is **zero**:

$$\sum_{phases} Q_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_0^{T} i(t)\, dt = 0$$

This is implemented using a **biphasic waveform**, typically **cathodic-first** (excitatory) followed by a **reversal anodic phase** of equal charge ($I_c \cdot t_c = I_a \cdot t_a$).

**Why mandatory:**

- **Electrolysis prevention:** Any DC residue causes water electrolysis, producing $H_2$, $O_2$, $Cl_2$, and pH shifts. Reaction: $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$.
- **Electrode corrosion:** Net DC dissolves platinum, iridium, and other noble metals.
- **Tissue damage:** Shannon-Packenjarow safety boundary; $k = \log_{10} k_c + \log_{10} k_d \le 1.85$.
- **Implant longevity:** Charge balance enables **5–10 year** implant life without electrode degradation.

In practice, perfect balance is impossible; a **DC-blocking capacitor** ($C_{blk} \ge 10$ µF) and **active charge-balancing** (Howland current pump with error amplifier) are used.

(3 marks: 1 for definition + formula, 1 for biphasic description, 1 for safety reasons)

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Q3 (A). (a)** Explain the **biomimetic design philosophy** of neural prostheses. How does it differ from conventional tonic stimulation? Provide two clinical examples. **(7 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model]** | **CO2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze**

### Model Solution:

**Definition (2 marks):**

Biomimetic design is an engineering paradigm in which the **temporal pattern, frequency, amplitude, and spatial distribution** of the stimulating waveform is engineered to **replicate the natural neural code** that the target nucleus would receive in a healthy nervous system. The aim is that the central nervous system (CNS) interprets the artificial input as *physiological* rather than as a *paroxysmal* or *noxious* event.

**Difference from Tonic Stimulation (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Tonic Stimulation | Biomimetic Stimulation |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Frequency | Fixed (e.g., 130 Hz DBS) | Adaptive, mirrors natural ISI |
| Pattern | Regular periodic pulse train | Stochastic, bursting, or naturalistic |
| CNS interpretation | Artificial, may produce paresthesia | More physiological, higher efficacy |
| Side effects | Dysarthria, paresthesia, tolerance | Reduced adaptation, fewer side effects |
| Energy use | Optimized constant | Variable, sometimes higher |

**Clinical Examples (2 marks):**

1. **Cochlear Implant (CIS vs SAS):** The "Continuous Interleaved Sampling" (CIS) strategy, which delivers non-simultaneous pulsatile stimuli to each channel, mimics the *place-frequency mapping* of the basilar membrane — more biomimetic than the older compressed analog (CA) strategy.
2. **Retinal Prosthesis (PRIMA, Pixium):** Subretinal photovoltaic arrays convert incident light → current → stimulation, directly emulating the natural phototransduction cascade lost in age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

---

### **Q3 (A). (b)** Describe the **tissue-device interface** of a chronic microelectrode. Include the **Randles equivalent circuit**, the **Helmholtz double layer**, the **Warburg impedance**, and discuss the **biocompatibility strategies** used to minimize foreign-body response (gliosis). **(7 marks)**

> **[CO2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze | KTU 2024 weightage 7 M]**

### Model Solution:

**Tissue–Device Interface Anatomy (2 marks):**

When a metal microelectrode (e.g., Pt-Ir 80/20, TiN, or iridium oxide) is implanted, the immediate 100 nm–1 mm region around it becomes the **interface**, consisting of:
1. The **electrode surface** (electronic conductor).
2. The **Helmholtz double layer** (ions adsorbed onto the surface, ~0.5 nm thick; behaves as a capacitor $C_{dl}$).
3. The **Gouy-Chapman diffuse layer** (mobile counter-ions, extends ~10 nm).
4. The **bulk electrolyte** (interstitial fluid, saline, $\sigma \approx 1.6$ S/m).
5. The **neural tissue** (cells, glia, extracellular matrix).

**Randles Equivalent Circuit (2 marks):**

$$Z(\omega) = R_s + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^{\alpha}} + Z_w$$

where:
- $R_s$ = series tissue/electrolyte resistance (10–500 Ω).
- $R_{ct}$ = charge-transfer resistance (1–100 kΩ) for Faradaic reactions.
- $C_{dl}$ = double-layer capacitance (1–100 µF/cm²).
- $Z_w = \sigma(j\omega)^{-1/2}$ = Warburg diffusion impedance.
- $\alpha$ = Cole–Cole exponent (0.5–1.0).

**Biocompatibility & Anti-Gliosis Strategies (3 marks):**

1. **Surface coatings:** Conductive polymer **PEDOT:PSS** reduces $Z$ by 10–100× and improves charge injection (up to 3 mC/cm² vs 0.1 mC/cm² for bare Pt).
2. **Drug elution:** Dexamethasone, minocycline, or anti-inflammatory release from hydrogel coatings.
3. **Geometry:** Smaller (< 10 µm) and softer probes (e.g., ultraflexible polymer probes by Rogers group) cause less chronic micromotion damage.
4. **Surface texturing:** Nanoporous TiN, iridium oxide, or carbon nanotube coatings increase effective surface area.
5. **Electrical conditioning:** Sub-threshold pre-pulses prevent faradaic charge buildup.

---

### **Q3 (B). Alternative to (A):**

### **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ultralow-power electronics architecture** of a modern **Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS)** implantable pulse generator (IPG). Include the role of the **Howland current source**, **DC-blocking capacitor**, **charge-balance monitor**, and **inductive telemetry link**. **(7 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / 2025 Model]** | **CO2 | Bloom: Apply**

### Model Solution:

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Battery → LDO → DSP → DAC → Howland Current Source → DC-Block Cap → Electrode → Tissue
                                              ↓
                                    Charge-Balance Monitor
                                              ↓
                                    Inductive Telemetry Coil
                                              ↕
                                    External Programmer
```

**Functional Description (5 marks):**

1. **Battery + LDO (Low-Dropout Regulator):** A primary cell (e.g., Medtronic Activa PC uses 3.0 V Li/CFx) feeds an LDO that produces a clean 1.8–3.3 V rail. Power dissipation $P_{LDO} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I \approx 1$ mW at typical currents.

2. **DSP Controller:** A low-power ARM Cortex-M0 or custom ASIC generates timing, pulse-width modulation, and computes charge balance in real time.

3. **DAC + Howland Current Source:** A 16-bit DAC sets the current setpoint. The Howland pump (improved Howland) provides a **compliance voltage** of up to ±10 V, allowing stimulation across high-impedance tissue ($R_{tissue} \approx 1$ kΩ) at $I = 1{-}5$ mA:

$$I_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{sense}} \quad \text{(improved Howland topology)}$$

4. **DC-Blocking Capacitor ($C_{blk} \ge 10$ µF):** Series capacitor ensures zero net DC even with component mismatch. Its voltage builds up with mismatch: $V_{C,blk} = \Delta Q / C_{blk}$.

5. **Charge-Balance Monitor:** ADC samples the inter-phase voltage and dynamically adjusts the anodic phase amplitude/duration to maintain balance (active balancing).

6. **Inductive Telemetry:** 400 kHz – 13.56 MHz ISM-band link for programming and battery recharge. Faraday's law governs: $V_{induced} = -N \cdot d\Phi/dt$.

**Power budget summary (typical DBS):**
- Stimulation: ~70 µW
- Telemetry (idle): ~5 µW
- Leakage/control: ~10 µW
- **Total: < 100 µW → 5–10 year battery life**

---

### **Q3 (B). (b)** Discuss in detail the **mechanism of action** of **neuromodulation through stimulation** at the cellular level. Use the **Hodgkin–Huxley framework** and explain how DBS suppresses tremor in Parkinson's disease. **(7 marks)**

> **[CO2 | Bloom: Analyze/Evaluate]**

### Model Solution:

**Cellular Mechanism (4 marks):**

When an extracellular cathodic pulse of amplitude $I \ge I_{th}$ is applied, it hyperpolarizes the membrane on one side and depolarizes the other. Depolarization opens **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels**, triggering the Hodgkin–Huxley action potential:

$$C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = -g_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) - g_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) - g_L (V_m - E_L) + I_{stim}$$

For chronic neuromodulation, **high-frequency stimulation (HFS, ≥ 100 Hz)** produces:
- **Na⁺ channel inactivation** (m-gate closed, h-gate open) — neurons become refractory.
- **Depletion of neurotransmitter** vesicles at presynaptic terminals.
- **Inhibition of pathologically-synchronized oscillations** (in the beta-band 13–30 Hz in Parkinson's).

**DBS for Parkinson's (3 marks):**

In Parkinson's disease, the **subthalamic nucleus (STN)** exhibits pathological **beta-band (13–30 Hz) synchronized oscillations**. High-frequency DBS (130–185 Hz) at the STN:
1. **Overrides** pathological beta oscillations with regular high-frequency firing.
2. **Activates inhibitory afferents** to the globus pallidus internus (GPi) and substantia nigra pars reticulata (SNr).
3. **Disrupts the indirect pathway** hyperactivation that causes bradykinesia and rigidity.
4. **Restores normal thalamocortical drive**, reducing tremor within seconds.

Clinical evidence: the **Okun et al. (2012)** and **Deuschl et al. (2006)** trials established DBS as gold-standard therapy for advanced PD.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in Module 2 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *Rheobase* and *Chronaxie* definitions** — remember: Rheobase is a *current*; Chronaxie is a *time*. Students frequently write them as both currents. (-1 to -2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — e.g., writing $P = I^2 R t f$ in mW without converting A→mA, Ω→kΩ, s→µs. Use the "mA × kΩ" trick: gives mW. (-1 mark)
>
> 3. **Omitting the DC-blocking capacitor** in the IPG block diagram — examiners specifically check for it as a charge-balance mechanism. (-2 marks)
>
> 4. **Writing "DBS uses low frequency"** — it is **HIGH frequency (130–185 Hz)**. The low-frequency use (60 Hz) is for SCS. (-1 to -2 marks)
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the Shannon-Packenjarow safety limit** when discussing charge density — this is the KTU Board's signature check. (-1 mark)
>
> 6. **Confusing monophasic vs biphasic waveforms** — the modern implantable device is **ALWAYS biphasic charge-balanced**. Monophasic is for acute lab use only. (-1 mark)
>
> 7. **Skipping the biophysical mechanism** in "explain neuromodulation" — students often write device engineering without discussing the *Hodgkin-Huxley* basis. (-2 marks)

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Neural Prosthesis:** Bioelectronic device that substitutes/modulates nervous system function.
- **Rheobase ($I_{rh}$):** Minimum DC current for excitation; asymptote of strength-duration curve.
- **Chronaxie ($\tau_{ch}$):** Pulse width at threshold = 2 × Rheobase; energy-optimal pulse width.
- **Charge Density ($k_c$):** $I \cdot t_{pulse} / A_{electrode}$ in µC/cm²; safety ≤ ~30 µC/cm².
- **Tissue–Device Interface:** Helmholtz double layer + Faradaic + bulk tissue (Randles circuit).
- **Biomimetic Design:** Mimicking natural neural code for physiological CNS response.
- **Integrated Prosthesis:** Closed-loop, sensing + processing + actuation on one implant.

### ⚙️ Key Numbers (Memorize!)
- DBS frequency: **130–185 Hz** ; pulse width: **60–90 µs** ; current: **1–5 mA**.
- SCS frequency: **40–60 Hz** ; pulse width: **60–450 µs** ; current: **1–12 mA**.
- Chronaxie: **30–500 µs** (myelinated); **0.1–1 ms** (unmyelinated).
- Energy-optimal: $t_{pulse} = \tau_{ch}$.
- SAR limit: **≤ 10 W/kg** (10 g avg, IEEE C95.1).
- Water window: **±1.23 V** vs Ag/AgCl.
- Shannon-Packenjarow limit: $k = \log k_c + \log k_d \le 1.85$.
- IPG power budget: **< 100 µW (DBS) ; < 1 mW (SCS)**.

### 🧪 Key Equations (Top 5)
1. $I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$
2. $Q_{phase} = I \cdot t_{pulse}$
3. $P_{avg} = I^2 Z t f$
4. $Z(\omega) = R_s + \dfrac{R_{ct}}{1 + (j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^{\alpha}}$
5. $E_{pulse,optimum} \text{ at } t = \tau_{ch}$

### 🏗️ Block Diagram Must-Haves
For any IPG: **Battery → LDO → DSP → DAC → Current Source → DC-Block Cap → Electrode → Tissue → (sense back via Amp → ADC → DSP)**.
For any integrated prosthesis: mention **closed-loop** with feedback arrow.

### 🩺 Clinical Examples (Cite At Least One)
- DBS: **Medtronic Activa PC, Abbott Infinity, Boston Scientific Vercise**.
- SCS: **Medtronic Intellis, Nevro Senza, Boston Sci WaveWriter**.
- CI: **Cochlear Nucleus 7, MED-EL Sonnet, Advanced Bionics Marvel**.
- Retinal: **Argus II (epiretinal), PRIMA (subretinal photovoltaic)**.
- FES: **Bioness L300 (foot drop), FreeHand (grasp)**.
- RNS: **NeuroPace RNS-320**.

### 🧠 Biomimetic vs Non-Biomimetic — Always Contrast
- Tonic (130 Hz regular) → simple, may cause adaptation.
- Biomimetic (stochastic / bursting) → matches natural ISI, more physiological.

### 🎯 Examiner's Pattern Recognition
- "Explain charge balance" → **Biphasic + DC-block cap + Shannon limit**.
- "Why biphasic?" → **No electrolysis + No corrosion + No DC tissue damage**.
- "Define chronaxie" → **Pulse width at 2× Rheobase + energy optimum**.
- "DBS mechanism" → **HFS overrides pathological β oscillations in STN**.
- "Tissue-device interface" → **Helmholtz + Randles + biocompatibility + gliosis**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Mile Strategy for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 1. Memorize the **4 numerical constants** (chronaxie range, DBS Hz, SAR, water window).
> 2. Always **draw the block diagram first** for any "explain IPG / stimulator" question — earns 2–3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Mention "charge balance"** in EVERY long answer — even when not explicitly asked. Examiners reward it.
> 4. For "biomimetic" questions, **contrast with tonic** — examiners love comparison tables.
> 5. End every answer with **one clinical example** (Medtronic, Cochlear, Argus II) — shows applied understanding.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — PEEBT864 Rehabilitation Engineering, KTU 2024 Scheme** ✅

topics.title: Types of Hearing Impairment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Hearing Impairment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hearing Impairment (Auditory Disability)** is a partial or total inability to hear sounds in one or both ears, characterized by an elevation in the auditory threshold beyond the statistical normal hearing threshold of **25 dB HL (decibels Hearing Level)** as standardized by the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the **American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA)**.

In the context of KTU 2024 Rehabilitation Engineering, hearing impairment is functionally classified based on:
- **Anatomical site of lesion** (Outer, Middle, Inner ear, Auditory nerve, Central pathways)
- **Degree of threshold shift** (measured in dB HL on a pure-tone audiogram)
- **Onset and progression** (Pre-lingual vs Post-lingual, Congenital vs Acquired)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEEBT864 / Module 3**
> The syllabus explicitly mandates study of: Conductive Hearing Loss, Sensorineural Hearing Loss, Mixed Hearing Loss, and Central Auditory Processing Disorders, along with their clinical/engineering rehabilitation strategies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Damaged Concert Hall"**
> Imagine you are attending a concert inside a massive hall, but the hall has three distinct problems:
> 1. **Conductive Loss** → The doors of the hall are jammed or covered with thick curtains. Sound *enters* but cannot *travel* into the room properly. The loudspeakers (inner ear) are perfect, but the *conduction pathway* is blocked.
> 2. **Sensorineural Loss** → The doors are wide open, but the loudspeakers themselves are damaged or the wiring is broken. Sound *reaches* the room, but it cannot be *decoded* properly by the inner microphone.
> 3. **Mixed Loss** → Both the doors are jammed *and* the speakers are damaged simultaneously.
> 4. **Central (Retrocochlear) Loss** → The hall, doors, and speakers are perfect, but the *audio cable connecting to the city broadcast tower* is severed. The brain cannot interpret what the ear sends.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal Hearing Threshold** | **0–25 dB HL** | Reference baseline (ISO 389) |
| **Audiometric Zero** | **0 dB HL** | Calibrated for each frequency |
| **Critical Speech Band** | **500–4000 Hz** | Frequencies essential for speech comprehension |
| **Speech Reception Threshold (SRT)** | ~20–30 dB HL | Softest level at which speech is intelligible |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Hearing Impairment

Hearing impairment is classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

#### A) By Anatomical Site (Most Important for KTU)

1. **Conductive Hearing Loss (CHL)** — Outer/Middle ear pathology
2. **Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL)** — Inner ear (Cochlea) or Auditory Nerve (Cranial Nerve VIII) pathology
3. **Mixed Hearing Loss (MHL)** — Simultaneous Conductive + Sensorineural components
4. **Central Auditory Processing Disorder (CAPD)** — Brainstem/Cortex pathology
5. **Functional / Psychogenic Hearing Loss** — No organic lesion; psychological origin

#### B) By Degree of Loss (WHO Classification — High Yield for KTU)

| Grade | Threshold (dB HL) | Functional Impact | Rehabilitation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal** | **0 – 25** | No significant difficulty | None |
| **Mild** | **26 – 40** | Difficulty with soft speech, whispers | Hearing aid (HA) |
| **Moderate** | **41 – 55** | Difficulty with normal conversation | Hearing aid + speech reading |
| **Moderately Severe** | **56 – 70** | Difficulty with loud speech | Powerful HA, FM systems |
| **Severe** | **71 – 90** | Cannot hear most speech | Powerful HA / Cochlear Implant (CI) |
| **Profound** | **≥ 91** | Cannot hear speech; only vibrations | Cochlear Implant, Sign language |

#### C) By Onset (Critical for Rehabilitation Planning)

- **Pre-lingual**: Before language acquisition (< 5 years) → Affects speech development
- **Post-lingual**: After language acquisition → Speech is preserved, only perception drops

### 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Type

#### 1. Conductive Hearing Loss (CHL)
- **Pathology Site**: External auditory canal, Tympanic membrane, Ossicular chain (Malleus, Incus, Stapes), Middle ear cavity, Eustachian tube.
- **Common Causes**: Cerumen impaction, Otitis media (middle ear infection), Otosclerosis, Tympanic membrane perforation, Cholesteatoma.
- **Audiometric Signature**: **Air conduction (AC) thresholds are elevated, while Bone conduction (BC) thresholds remain normal**.
- **Air-Bone Gap (ABG)**: Present (typically 20–40 dB).
- **Speech Discrimination**: Usually excellent (because the cochlea is intact).
- **Medical/Surgical Rehabilitation**: Myringoplasty, Stapedectomy, Tympanoplasty.
- **Engineering Rehabilitation**: Conventional **Air-conduction Hearing Aids**, Bone-Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHA) for chronic cases.

#### 2. Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL)
- **Pathology Site**: Cochlea (sensory), Hair cells (Outer & Inner), Auditory nerve (neural), or both.
- **Common Causes**: Presbycusis (age-related), Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL), Ototoxic drugs (Gentamicin, Cisplatin), Ménière's disease, Acoustic neuroma, Congenital (Rubella, Genetic).
- **Audiometric Signature**: **Both Air and Bone conduction thresholds are elevated to a similar degree → No significant Air-Bone Gap**.
- **Speech Discrimination**: Often poor, especially in noise (loss of temporal/frequency resolution).
- **Engineering Rehabilitation**: Digital Hearing Aids, **Cochlear Implants** (for severe-to-profound), Auditory Brainstem Implants (ABI).

#### 3. Mixed Hearing Loss (MHL)
- **Pathology Site**: Both middle ear AND inner ear/auditory nerve.
- **Audiometric Signature**: **Both AC and BC are elevated, BUT AC is more elevated than BC → Air-Bone Gap present + elevated BC thresholds**.
- **Engineering Rehabilitation**: Combined approach (e.g., surgical correction of middle ear + hearing aid/cochlear implant).

#### 4. Central Auditory Processing Disorder (CAPD / Retrocochlear)
- **Pathology Site**: Cranial Nerve VIII (beyond cochlea), Brainstem, Auditory cortex.
- **Audiometric Signature**: **Pure-tone audiogram is often NORMAL**, but speech understanding in noisy environments is severely impaired.
- **Engineering Rehabilitation**: FM systems, Auditory training, Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for KTU 2024 numerical and conceptual questions on hearing impairment.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decibel (Sound Pressure) | $L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | dB SPL | Sound Level Meter calibration |
| 2 | Decibel (Hearing Level) | $L_{\text{HL}} = L_{\text{SPL}} - RETSPL$ | dB HL | Audiometer reference threshold |
| 3 | Pure Tone Average (PTA) | $PTA = \dfrac{f_{500} + f_{1k} + f_{2k} + f_{4k}}{4}$ | dB HL | WHO degree-of-loss classification |
| 4 | Speech Reception Threshold (SRT) | $SRT \approx PTA \pm 5$ | dB HL | Cross-check audiogram validity |
| 5 | Air-Bone Gap (ABG) | $ABG = AC - BC$ | dB | Differentiates CHL vs SNHL |
| 6 | Fletcher Index (Articulation Index) | $FI = \dfrac{f_{500}+f_{1k}+f_{2k}}{3}$ | dB HL | Hearing-aid gain prescription |
| 7 | Most Comfortable Level (MCL) | $MCL \approx PTA + 35$ to $PTA + 50$ | dB HL | Hearing aid fitting target |
| 8 | Loudness Discomfort Level (LDL/UCL) | $UCL \approx MCL + 30$ to $MCL + 50$ | dB HL | Upper limit of amplification |
| 9 | Dynamic Range | $DR = UCL - PTA$ | dB | Defines HA compression ratio |
| 10 | Hearing Aid Gain | $Gain = REIG + CORFIG + 10$ | dB | Target gain (POGO-II rule) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $P$ = Measured sound pressure, $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (auditory reference)
> - $RETSPL$ = Reference Equivalent Threshold SPL (frequency-dependent)
> - $AC$ = Air Conduction threshold, $BC$ = Bone Conduction threshold
> - $REIG$ = Real-Ear Insertion Gain, $CORFIG$ = Real-Ear Coupler Response

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Decibel Hearing Level from Sound Pressure Level

The audiometer is calibrated so that **0 dB HL corresponds to the average threshold of a young otologically normal adult** at each frequency. The conversion from physical SPL to the clinical HL scale is given by:

$$
L_{\text{HL}} = L_{\text{SPL}} - RETSPL(f)
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The fundamental sound pressure level definition in linear acoustic physics is:

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right), \quad P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2:** At a given audiometric frequency $f$, the **Reference Equivalent Threshold SPL ($RETSPL$)** is the median SPL that a group of normal-hearing young adults can just detect. For example:
- $RETSPL(1000\,\text{Hz}) = 7.5$ dB SPL
- $RETSPL(4000\,\text{Hz}) = 9.5$ dB SPL

**Step 3:** By definition, the HL scale assigns the value **0 dB HL** to the threshold SPL $P_{\text{thr}}(f)$:

$$
0 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{thr}}(f)}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right) - RETSPL(f)
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $P_{\text{thr}}(f)$:

$$
P_{\text{thr}}(f) = P_{\text{ref}} \cdot 10^{RETSPL(f)/20}
$$

**Step 5:** For any measured pressure $P_{\text{meas}}$, the Hearing Level in dB is then:

$$
L_{\text{HL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{meas}}}{P_{\text{thr}}(f)}\right)
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $P_{\text{thr}}(f)$ from Step 4:

$$
L_{\text{HL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{meas}}}{P_{\text{ref}} \cdot 10^{RETSPL(f)/20}}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** Using logarithm properties $\log(a/b) = \log a - \log b$:

$$
L_{\text{HL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{meas}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right) - RETSPL(f)
$$

**Step 8:** Recognizing the first term as $L_{\text{SPL}}$:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{\text{HL}} = L_{\text{SPL}} - RETSPL(f)\,}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The HL scale **normalizes the audiometer reading** so that "0 dB HL" is the same normal hearing level for every test frequency, removing the natural ear-canal resonance bias present in raw SPL measurements.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Classifying a Patient

**Problem Statement:**
A patient undergoes pure-tone audiometry. The measured Air Conduction (AC) and Bone Conduction (BC) thresholds are as follows:

| Frequency (Hz) | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 | 8000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AC (dB HL) | 30 | 45 | 55 | 60 | 50 | 40 |
| BC (dB HL) | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 15 | 10 |

**Step 1: Calculate the Pure Tone Average (PTA) for AC.**

$$
PTA_{AC} = \frac{AC_{500} + AC_{1000} + AC_{2000} + AC_{4000}}{4}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
PTA_{AC} = \frac{45 + 55 + 60 + 50}{4} = \frac{210}{4} = 52.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Pure Tone Average (PTA) for BC.**

$$
PTA_{BC} = \frac{BC_{500} + BC_{1000} + BC_{2000} + BC_{4000}}{4}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
PTA_{BC} = \frac{10 + 15 + 20 + 15}{4} = \frac{60}{4} = 15\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Air-Bone Gap (ABG).**

$$
ABG = PTA_{AC} - PTA_{BC} = 52.5 - 15 = 37.5\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4: Identify the Type of Hearing Loss.**

Since $ABG > 10$ dB (significant) AND $PTA_{BC} = 15$ dB HL (within normal range $\le 25$ dB HL):
- Air Conduction is elevated (52.5 dB HL) → **Mild-to-Moderate loss**
- Bone Conduction is normal → **Cochlea is healthy**
- Presence of Air-Bone Gap → **Conductive component present**

**Conclusion:** The patient has a **Moderate Conductive Hearing Loss (CHL)** in the tested ear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** A conventional air-conduction hearing aid with gain $\approx PTA - 15 = 37.5$ dB would be the first-line rehabilitation, with potential surgical referral for middle-ear pathology (e.g., ossicular reconstruction).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Differentiating Hearing Loss Types

| Diagnostic Criterion | Conductive (CHL) | Sensorineural (SNHL) | Mixed (MHL) | Central (CAPD) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AC Threshold** | Elevated | Elevated | Elevated | Often Normal |
| **BC Threshold** | Normal (≤ 25 dB) | Elevated | Elevated | Normal |
| **Air-Bone Gap** | Present (>10 dB) | Absent (≤ 10 dB) | Present | Absent |
| **Speech Discrimination** | Good (with amplification) | Reduced | Reduced | Severely reduced in noise |
| **Weber Test** | Lateralizes to **worse** ear | Lateralizes to **better** ear | Lateralizes to **worse** ear (BC component) | No lateralization |
| **Rinne Test** | **Negative** (BC > AC) | **Positive** (AC > BC) | **Negative** | **Positive** |
| **Recruitment** | Absent | Often Present | Variable | Absent |
| **Primary Rehab Device** | Conventional HA / BAHA | Digital HA / Cochlear Implant | HA + CI / Combined | FM system / ALDs |

> [!TIP]
> **Recruitment** = Abnormal growth of loudness. A small increase in sound intensity causes a disproportionately large increase in perceived loudness — a hallmark of cochlear (outer hair cell) damage in SNHL.
