## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Conductive and Sensorineural Hearing Loss. Mention the audiometric basis of differentiation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Conductive (CHL) | Sensorineural (SNHL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Site of Lesion** | Outer/Middle ear | Inner ear (Cochlea) / Auditory nerve |
| **Air Conduction (AC)** | Elevated | Elevated |
| **Bone Conduction (BC)** | **Normal (≤ 25 dB HL)** | **Elevated** |
| **Air-Bone Gap** | **Present (> 10 dB)** | **Absent (≤ 10 dB)** |
| **Speech Discrimination** | Usually good with amplification | Often poor, especially in noise |
| **Rinne Test** | Negative (BC > AC) | Positive (AC > BC) |
| **Rehabilitation** | Conventional HA, BAHA, surgery | Digital HA, Cochlear Implant |

**Key Audiometric Basis:** The **Air-Bone Gap (ABG)** is the *gold standard audiometric signature* used to differentiate CHL from SNHL. A significant ABG (>10 dB) indicates CHL, whereas its absence with elevated thresholds indicates SNHL.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for site of lesion, 1 mark for audiometric differentiation using ABG, 1 mark for rehabilitation contrast.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Mixed Hearing Loss." When is it typically suspected clinically?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Mixed Hearing Loss (MHL) is a type of hearing impairment in which **both conductive and sensorineural components coexist in the same ear**, meaning pathology is present in the middle ear (or outer ear) **AND** in the cochlea/auditory nerve simultaneously.

**Clinical Suspicion:** MHL is typically suspected in the following scenarios:
1. When the patient has a **long-standing conductive pathology** (e.g., chronic otitis media with ossicular erosion) that has now **extended to or damaged the inner ear**, causing secondary cochlear involvement.
2. When a patient with **presbycusis (age-related SNHL)** acquires an additional **middle-ear infection or otosclerosis**.
3. On the audiogram, MHL is identified by **elevated Bone Conduction (BC) above 25 dB HL (sensorineural component) PLUS the presence of a significant Air-Bone Gap (conductive component)**.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the dual-pathology concept, 1 mark for audiometric identification criteria.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) Classify hearing impairment based on (i) anatomical site of lesion, and (ii) degree of loss as per WHO. Add a note on the rehabilitation engineering approach for each major type. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Classification by Anatomical Site (4 marks):**

1. **Conductive Hearing Loss (CHL)** — Lesion in outer/middle ear (cerumen impaction, otitis media, otosclerosis, tympanic membrane perforation). Air conduction elevated, bone conduction normal. Air-bone gap present.
2. **Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL)** — Lesion in cochlea (hair cells) or auditory nerve (CN VIII). Both AC and BC elevated to similar levels. No ABG. Causes: presbycusis, NIHL, ototoxicity, Ménière's disease.
3. **Mixed Hearing Loss (MHL)** — Combined middle-ear and inner-ear pathology. Both AC and BC elevated, with ABG still present.
4. **Central Auditory Processing Disorder (CAPD)** — Lesion in auditory nerve (retrocochlear), brainstem, or cortex. Pure-tone audiogram often normal; speech-in-noise severely impaired.

**(ii) Classification by Degree (per WHO) — Table (2 marks):**

| Grade | dB HL Range | Rehabilitation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Normal | 0–25 | None required |
| Mild | 26–40 | Conventional HA |
| Moderate | 41–55 | HA + speech reading |
| Moderately Severe | 56–70 | Powerful HA, FM systems |
| Severe | 71–90 | Powerful HA / CI |
| Profound | ≥ 91 | CI / ABI / Sign language |

**(iii) Rehabilitation Engineering Note (1 mark):**
For **CHL** → air-conduction hearing aids or BAHA; for **SNHL** → digital hearing aids with WDRC (Wide Dynamic Range Compression), and for severe-to-profound SNHL → **Cochlear Implants (CI)**; for **MHL** → combination (HA + surgical middle-ear repair); for **CAPD** → FM systems, ALDs, and auditory training.

---

**(b) A 55-year-old factory worker reports bilateral gradual hearing loss. His audiogram shows AC = 55, 60, 65, 70 dB HL and BC = 50, 55, 60, 65 dB HL at 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 4 kHz respectively. Identify the type and degree of hearing loss. Calculate PTA and ABG, and recommend a suitable rehabilitation engineering solution. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Pure Tone Average (PTA) for AC (1.5 marks):**

$$
PTA_{AC} = \frac{55 + 60 + 65 + 70}{4} = \frac{250}{4} = 62.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 2: Pure Tone Average (PTA) for BC (1.5 marks):**

$$
PTA_{BC} = \frac{50 + 55 + 60 + 65}{4} = \frac{230}{4} = 57.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 3: Air-Bone Gap (ABG) (1 mark):**

$$
ABG = PTA_{AC} - PTA_{BC} = 62.5 - 57.5 = 5\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4: Identification of Type (1.5 marks):**

- $PTA_{AC} = 62.5$ dB HL → falls in the **Moderately Severe** category (56–70 dB HL) per WHO.
- $ABG = 5$ dB → **NOT significant** (clinical ABG threshold is 10 dB).
- $PTA_{BC} = 57.5$ dB HL → **ELEVATED above 25 dB HL** → indicates cochlear involvement.

**Conclusion:** **Moderately Severe Bilateral Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL)**, consistent with **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL)** from factory exposure (high-frequency sloping pattern).

**Step 5: Rehabilitation Engineering Recommendation (1.5 marks):**

- **Hearing protection** (earplugs/earmuffs) for ongoing factory noise exposure to prevent further damage.
- **Binaural digital Behind-The-Ear (BTE) hearing aids** with **Wide Dynamic Range Compression (WDRC)** and directional microphones.
- Target HA gain using the POGO-II rule:
$$
\text{Gain} = (0.5 \times PTA) + 5 = (0.5 \times 62.5) + 5 = 36.25\ \text{dB}
$$
- **Auditory training** and speech-reading therapy.
- **Counseling** regarding the permanent, non-curable nature of NIHL and the need for lifelong HA use.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Explain in detail the clinical and audiometric features of Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL). Discuss the role of cochlear implants in its rehabilitation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Clinical Features of SNHL (2 marks):**
- **Gradual or sudden onset** depending on etiology (e.g., sudden in viral labyrinthitis, gradual in presbycusis).
- **Difficulty understanding speech, especially in noisy environments** — disproportionate to the audiometric threshold (a hallmark of cochlear distortion).
- **Tinnitus** (ringing/buzzing) commonly co-exists.
- **Recruitment** — abnormal growth of loudness; soft sounds inaudible, moderate sounds uncomfortably loud.
- **Bilateral and symmetrical** in presbycusis and NIHL; **unilateral** in acoustic neuroma and sudden idiopathic loss.

**Audiometric Features (2.5 marks):**
- **Pure Tone Audiometry:** Both AC and BC thresholds elevated **to a similar degree** (within 10 dB).
- **No significant Air-Bone Gap.**
- **Speech Discrimination Score (SDS)** reduced; **Speech Reception Threshold (SRT)** matches PTA within ±5 dB.
- **Loudness Discomfort Level (LDL/UCL)** is **reduced** (often 80–95 dB HL), producing a **narrowed Dynamic Range (DR = UCL − PTA)**, requiring WDRC compression in hearing aids.
- **OAE (Otoacoustic Emissions):** Absent (outer hair cell dysfunction).
- **ABR (Auditory Brainstem Response):** Prolonged or absent waves in retrocochlear pathology.

**Role of Cochlear Implants (CI) in Rehabilitation (2.5 marks):**
- **Indication:** Severe-to-profound bilateral SNHL (PTA ≥ 70–90 dB HL) where conventional hearing aids provide **no speech discrimination benefit (SDS < 50%)**.
- **Working Principle:** Bypasses the damaged hair cells and **directly stimulates the auditory nerve fibers** via an electrode array surgically placed in the **cochlea**.
- **Components:** External (Microphone, Speech Processor, Transmitter coil) + Internal (Receiver-Stimulator, Electrode array).
- **Coding Strategies:** CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling), ACE (Advanced Combination Encoder), HiRes — convert acoustic signals into electrical pulses.
- **Outcomes:** Restoration of sound awareness, improvement in speech perception (especially with auditory-verbal therapy), better quality of life.
- **Limitations:** Requires **surgical intervention**, **intensive rehabilitation/AVT (Auditory-Verbal Therapy)**, not suitable for all (e.g., absent auditory nerve → requires **Auditory Brainstem Implant ABI**).

---

**(b) A child born to a mother who had rubella during pregnancy shows no startle response to loud sounds. Pure-tone audiometry (under sedation) shows AC = 80, 85, 90, 95 dB HL and BC = 80, 85, 90, 90 dB HL at 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 4 kHz respectively. Classify the hearing loss, calculate PTA and ABG, and justify the choice of cochlear implant. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: PTA for AC (1.5 marks):**

$$
PTA_{AC} = \frac{80 + 85 + 90 + 95}{4} = \frac{350}{4} = 87.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 2: PTA for BC (1.5 marks):**

$$
PTA_{BC} = \frac{80 + 85 + 90 + 90}{4} = \frac{345}{4} = 86.25\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 3: Air-Bone Gap (1 mark):**

$$
ABG = 87.5 - 86.25 = 1.25\ \text{dB (NOT significant)}
$$

**Step 4: Classification (1.5 marks):**

- $PTA_{AC} = 87.5$ dB HL → falls in the **Severe category** (71–90 dB HL) per WHO, bordering on Profound.
- No significant ABG → indicates **SNHL**.
- Etiology: **Congenital rubella syndrome** → cochlear (hair cell) damage.

**Conclusion:** **Severe Bilateral Congenital Sensorineural Hearing Loss (pre-lingual onset).**

**Step 5: Justification for Cochlear Implantation (1.5 marks):**

- The child has **pre-lingual severe SNHL** — critical window for speech and language development is at risk.
- $PTA > 70$ dB HL is the FDA-approved indication threshold for CI candidacy.
- With absent startle reflex, **conventional hearing aids are unlikely to provide sufficient speech discrimination**.
- CI is the gold-standard rehabilitation for pre-lingual severe-to-profound SNHL, ideally implanted **before 2 years of age** for optimal speech and language outcomes.
- Combined with **Auditory-Verbal Therapy (AVT)** post-implantation, near-normal speech development is achievable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Air-Bone Gap" with absolute loss**: A small ABG (<10 dB) is *physiological/test-retest noise*; only ABG > 10 dB is clinically significant.
> 2. **Do NOT misclassify MHL**: Both BC elevation (>25 dB HL) AND a significant ABG must BOTH be present. Elevation of BC alone is SNHL.
> 3. **Do NOT forget the FDA CI candidacy threshold** (PTA ≥ 70 dB HL with poor speech discrimination on HA trial) — students frequently quote the lower limit incorrectly.
> 4. **Do NOT ignore pre-lingual vs post-lingual** distinction in exam answers — it directly affects rehabilitation prognosis and CI timing.
> 5. **In numerical problems, always show all four steps** (PTA-AC, PTA-BC, ABG, Classification) — KTU examiners award 1–1.5 marks per step; skipping a step costs you a full mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Hearing Impairment**

- **Hearing impairment** is officially defined as an auditory threshold **greater than 25 dB HL** in one or both ears.
- **Three classical types**: Conductive (CHL), Sensorineural (SNHL), Mixed (MHL) — the fourth "type" is **Central Auditory Processing Disorder (CAPD)**.
- **CHL** = AC elevated, BC normal, **Air-Bone Gap PRESENT** → Outer/Middle ear.
- **SNHL** = AC and BC BOTH elevated, **NO significant Air-Bone Gap** → Cochlea/Auditory nerve.
- **MHL** = Both AC and BC elevated, **ABG present AND BC > 25 dB HL** → both middle and inner ear.
- **CAPD** = Pure-tone audiogram normal, but **speech-in-noise comprehension severely impaired** → retrocochlear/brainstem/cortex.
- **WHO Degree Classification (Critical for KTU)**: Normal 0–25 | Mild 26–40 | Moderate 41–55 | Mod-Severe 56–70 | Severe 71–90 | Profound ≥ 91 (all in dB HL).
- **PTA Formula**: Average of thresholds at **500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz** — the four speech frequencies.
- **ABG Formula**: $ABG = PTA_{AC} - PTA_{BC}$; threshold of clinical significance is **> 10 dB**.
- **dB HL Conversion**: $L_{HL} = L_{SPL} - RETSPL(f)$ — frequency-specific reference.
- **Dynamic Range**: $DR = UCL - PTA$ — narrow in SNHL due to recruitment; requires WDRC in HA.
- **Rehab Hierarchy**: Mild/Moderate CHL/SNHL → **Air-conduction Hearing Aid**; chronic CHL → **BAHA**; Severe-to-Profound SNHL → **Cochlear Implant**; absent CN VIII → **Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI)**; CAPD → **FM system / ALDs**.
- **Pre-lingual vs Post-lingual** determines timing and outcome of CI: **Implant before age 2** for pre-lingual gives best language outcomes.
- **Etiology shortcuts**: Conductive → think "blockage" (wax, fluid, bone); Sensorineural → think "nerve/hair cell damage" (noise, drugs, age).
- **Most common KTU exam trap**: A student says "loss is mixed" just because AC and BC are both elevated — WRONG. MHL requires **elevated BC PLUS an ABG**.
- **One-liner for viva**: *"The Air-Bone Gap on the audiogram is the single most important audiometric signature for differentiating conductive from sensorineural hearing loss."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hearing Function Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hearing Function Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎧 Hearing Function Assessment — Module 3

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEBT864):** Hearing Function Assessment forms the foundational clinical-engineering bridge between audiology and biomedical instrumentation. This module trains the future rehabilitation engineer to measure, quantify, and classify auditory deficits using calibrated instruments, standardized protocols, and psychoacoustic principles.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hearing Function Assessment** is the systematic, instrument-based evaluation of the human auditory system using acoustic stimuli of calibrated intensity and frequency. The objective is to objectively and subjectively quantify an individual's **hearing threshold** across the audible spectrum (**250 Hz to 8000 Hz**), characterize the **type** and **degree** of auditory impairment, and produce data essential for the prescription of rehabilitation devices such as hearing aids and cochlear implants.

It integrates three domains:

| Domain | Function Measured | Tool |
|--------|-------------------|------|
| **Behavioral** | Subjective threshold | Pure-tone audiometry |
| **Electrophysiological** | Neural pathway integrity | ABR, OAE, ASSR |
| **Immittance** | Middle-ear mechanics | Tympanometry, Acoustic Reflex |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Eye Test of the Ear"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine your ear is a complex stereo system. The outer/middle ear is the speaker cabinet and wiring (conductive parts), and the inner ear (cochlea) plus auditory nerve is the amplifier and the wire to the speaker (sensorineural parts).
>
> A **Hearing Function Assessment** is exactly like a stereo diagnostic test:
> 1. You feed in **standard tones of varying pitch (frequency)** and **volume (intensity)**.
> 2. The patient signals the softest sound they can hear — the **hearing threshold**.
> 3. By comparing each ear and each frequency, the audiologist pinpoints whether the "speaker cabinet" (conductive loss) or the "amplifier" (sensorineural loss) is faulty.
>
> Just as an electrician tests each wire of a stereo, the rehabilitation engineer systematically tests **air conduction**, **bone conduction**, and **middle-ear compliance** to locate the fault.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

The following standardized values (per **ISO 389** and **ANSI S3.6**) are mandatory for KTU numericals:

- **Reference Sound Pressure (p₀):** 20 μPa (= 2 × 10⁻⁵ Pa)
- **Reference Sound Intensity (I₀):** 10⁻¹² W/m²
- **Standard Audiometric Frequencies:** 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000 Hz
- **Reference Zero (0 dB HL):** Average threshold of young, otologically normal adults
- **SPL-to-HL offset (at 1000 Hz):** ≈ 7 dB
- **Conversational Speech Level:** ≈ 60 dB SPL
- **Threshold of Pain:** ≈ 120–130 dB SPL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **dB SPL** (Sound Pressure Level) is an **absolute** physical measurement.
> **dB HL** (Hearing Level) is a **relative, frequency-specific clinical** measurement referenced to the normal-hearing threshold.

---

## 1.4 The Auditory Pathway — Anatomical Quick Reference

```
Outer Ear (Pinna + Ear Canal)  →  Sound Collection (Resonance ~2.7 kHz, +10 to +15 dB)
        ↓
Middle Ear (Tympanic Membrane + Ossicles: Malleus, Incus, Stapes)
        ↓
        Impedance Matching → Transformer Action (≈ 22:1 pressure gain, ≈ +25 to +30 dB)
        ↓
Inner Ear (Cochlea — Basilar Membrane + Organ of Corti)
        ↓
Frequency-to-Place Mapping (Tonotopy)
        ↓
Auditory Nerve (Cranial Nerve VIII)
        ↓
Brainstem → Cortex (Auditory Evoked Potentials generated at each station)
```

> [!NOTE]
> A defect at any stage above produces a **characteristic audiometric signature** — recognizing these patterns is the core skill of a hearing assessment professional.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decibel (dB) — Logarithmic Foundation of Audiometry

Human hearing spans an enormous dynamic range: from the faintest detectable whisper to sounds intense enough to cause pain. Because this range spans **more than a million-fold in pressure** and **a trillion-fold in intensity**, a logarithmic scale is essential.

### Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

The SPL in decibels is defined as:

$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \;\; \text{[dB SPL]}
$$

where:
- $p$ = measured RMS sound pressure (Pa)
- $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa (the standard reference pressure, chosen because it is roughly the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz)

### Sound Intensity Level (SIL)

The intensity level in decibels is defined as:

$$
L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \;\; \text{[dB]}
$$

where:
- $I$ = acoustic intensity (W/m²)
- $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² (standard reference intensity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because intensity is proportional to the **square** of pressure ($I \propto p^2$), the **20** in the SPL equation and the **10** in the SIL equation are mathematically equivalent — they describe the same physical quantity expressed differently.

### Inverse Formula — Recovering Pressure from SPL

$$
p = p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p / 20}
$$

**Example:** A 94 dB SPL tone corresponds to $p = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} \times 10^{94/20} = 20 \times 10^{4.7} \approx 1$ Pa.

---

## 2.2 Hearing Level (dB HL) — Clinical Normalization

To create a clinically meaningful "zero" for each audiometric frequency, the SPL at the threshold of a **young, otologically normal adult** is defined as **0 dB HL** at that frequency.

$$
\text{HL} = \text{SPL}_{\text{stimulus}} - \text{SPL}_{\text{reference threshold for that frequency}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The reference SPL needed for 0 dB HL **varies with frequency** because the ear's sensitivity is frequency-dependent. For example:
> - 0 dB HL at 250 Hz ≈ **25.5 dB SPL**
> - 0 dB HL at 1000 Hz ≈ **7.0 dB SPL**
> - 0 dB HL at 4000 Hz ≈ **9.0 dB SPL**
>
> This is why the audiometer's internal "dial" shows dB HL, but it actually outputs frequency-specific SPL.

---

## 2.3 Pure Tone Average (PTA) — Quantifying Hearing Loss

The PTA is the arithmetic mean of hearing thresholds at the four **speech-frequency octaves**:

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{\text{HL}_{500} + \text{HL}_{1000} + \text{HL}_{2000} + \text{HL}_{4000}}{4}
$$

Units: **dB HL**.

### WHO (2024) Classification of Hearing Loss (by Better-Ear PTA)

| Grade | PTA (dB HL) | Category | Rehabilitation Implication |
|-------|-------------|----------|-----------------------------|
| 0 | ≤ 20 | Normal | None |
| 1 | 21 – 35 | Mild | Counseling, occasional hearing aid |
| 2 | 36 – 50 | Moderate | Hearing aid recommended |
| 3 | 51 – 65 | Moderately Severe | Hearing aid essential |
| 4 | 66 – 80 | Severe | Power hearing aid / CI evaluation |
| 5 | 81 – 95 | Profound | Cochlear implant candidate |
| 6 | ≥ 95 | Total/Profound | CI / ABI / manual communication |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit | Clinical Use |
|---|---------|---------------------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | dB SPL | Calibration, free-field |
| 2 | Intensity Level | $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | dB | Power, intensity |
| 3 | HL Conversion | $\text{HL} = \text{SPL} - \text{RET}_{\text{freq}}$ | dB HL | Audiometer output |
| 4 | Pressure Recovery | $p = p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p/20}$ | Pa | Inverse problem |
| 5 | Pure Tone Average | $\text{PTA} = \tfrac{1}{4}\sum \text{HL}_{1,2,3,4\ \text{kHz}}$ | dB HL | Degree of loss |
| 6 | Speech Intelligibility Index (approx) | $\text{SII} \approx \sum_{i} a_i \cdot \text{SNR}_i$ | 0–1 | Speech audibility |
| 7 | Acoustic Impedance (middle ear) | $Z = p/u$ | acoustic ohm | Tympanometry |
| 8 | Compliance (Y) | $Y = 1/Z$ | mmho / cm³ | Tympanometry peak |
| 9 | Air–Bone Gap (ABG) | $\text{ABG} = \text{AC}_{\text{threshold}} - \text{BC}_{\text{threshold}}$ | dB | Conductive component |
| 10 | Fletcher's Weighted SII bands | Centered at 500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz | dB HL | Hearing-aid fitting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall:** The Air–Bone Gap (ABG) is computed at **each frequency**, not as an average of two PTA values. A normal ABG is **≤ 10 dB**.

---

## 2.5 Three Pillars of Hearing Assessment

### Pillar 1 — Behavioral Tests (Subjective)
- **Pure-Tone Audiometry** (AC + BC)
- **Speech Audiometry** (SRT, SDS/WRS)
- **Behavioral Observation Audiometry (BOA)** — for infants

### Pillar 2 — Immittance Measures (Objective Middle-Ear)
- **Tympanometry** — middle-ear compliance vs. pressure
- **Acoustic Reflex** (stapedial reflex)

### Pillar 3 — Electrophysiological (Objective Neural)
- **OAE** (Otoacoustic Emissions) — outer hair cell function
- **ABR** (Auditory Brainstem Response) — auditory pathway up to brainstem
- **ASSR** (Auditory Steady-State Response) — frequency-specific threshold
- **Cortical ERA** (P1, N1, P2, mismatch negativity)

> [!NOTE]
> The rehabilitation engineer must understand which tool diagnoses which part of the auditory pathway, because this drives **device selection** (e.g., cochlear implant candidacy depends on ABR findings).

---

## 2.6 Classification of Hearing Loss — Pathological Engineering View

| Type | Site of Lesion | AC Threshold | BC Threshold | Air–Bone Gap |
|------|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Conductive** | Outer / middle ear | Elevated | Normal | > 10 dB |
| **Sensorineural** | Cochlea / CN VIII / central | Elevated | Elevated (matches AC) | ≤ 10 dB |
| **Mixed** | Both conductive + sensorineural | Elevated | Elevated (but lower than AC) | > 10 dB + BC also elevated |
| **Functional / Pseudohypoacusis** | Non-organic | Inconsistent | Inconsistent | Inconsistent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A hearing aid primarily compensates for **sensorineural** loss by amplification and frequency shaping. **Conductive** loss is often managed **medically/surgically** (e.g., myringoplasty for TM perforation, ossiculoplasty).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audiometer design** — requires precision dB-attenuator networks calibrated to ISO 389.
- **Hearing Aid fitting** — NAL-NL2, DSL-v5 prescriptive formulas use audiometric thresholds directly.
- **Cochlear Implant mapping** — threshold (T) and comfort (C) levels derived from behavioral and ABR data.
- **Industrial noise monitoring** — dBA-weighted dosimetry uses HL/SPL concepts.
- **Ototoxic drug monitoring** — serial PTA/OAE used to detect early cisplatin/aminoglycoside damage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Linear Pressure to Logarithmic Decibels

We want a scale where the threshold of hearing $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ and the threshold of pain $p_{\text{pain}} \approx 20\ \text{Pa}$ fit into a manageable range.

**Step 1 — Compute the linear ratio:**
$$
R = \frac{p_{\text{pain}}}{p_0} = \frac{20\ \text{Pa}}{20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}} = 1{,}000{,}000 = 10^6
$$

**Step 2 — Take the base-10 logarithm:**
$$
\log_{10}(R) = \log_{10}(10^6) = 6
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 20 (because intensity $\propto p^2$):**
$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}(R) = 20 \times 6 = 120\ \text{dB SPL}
$$

**Conclusion:** The full dynamic range of human hearing, from threshold to pain, is conveniently compressed into a **120 dB** scale — a manageable clinical range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — PTA-Based Degree of Loss

**Worked Example:**

A patient shows the following audiometric thresholds:

| Frequency (Hz) | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 | 8000 |
|----------------|-----|-----|------|------|------|------|
| Right ear AC (dB HL) | 25 | 30 | 45 | 55 | 60 | 70 |
| Left ear AC (dB HL)  | 30 | 35 | 50 | 60 | 65 | 75 |
| Right ear BC (dB HL)  | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |

**Step 1 — Compute the Right-Ear PTA over the speech frequencies (500–4000 Hz):**

$$
\text{PTA}_{\text{Right}} = \frac{30 + 45 + 55 + 60}{4} = \frac{190}{4} = 47.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Right-Ear PTA-BC over the same frequencies:**

$$
\text{PTA}_{\text{Right,BC}} = \frac{10 + 15 + 20 + 25}{4} = \frac{70}{4} = 17.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Air–Bone Gap:**

$$
\text{ABG}_{\text{Right}} = \text{PTA}_{\text{AC}} - \text{PTA}_{\text{BC}} = 47.5 - 17.5 = 30\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret clinically:**

- PTA > 20 → **Abnormal** (hearing loss present)
- ABG > 10 → **Conductive component present**
- BC PTA elevated (17.5 > 0) → **Mild sensorineural component also present**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnosis: Mixed hearing loss, moderately severe, Right ear.**
> This immediately tells the rehabilitation engineer that **medical referral is mandatory** (to address the conductive component) and **amplification may still be needed** post-treatment.

**Step 5 — Repeat for the Left ear:**

$$
\text{PTA}_{\text{Left,AC}} = \frac{35 + 50 + 60 + 65}{4} = 52.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

The Left ear shows greater overall loss.

**Step 6 — Apply WHO grading (Better-Ear PTA):**

$$
\text{PTA}_{\text{Better}} = \min(47.5,\ 52.5) = 47.5\ \text{dB HL}
$$

Per WHO 2024 → **Grade 2: Moderate hearing loss**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — SPL ↔ Pressure Numerical Conversion

**Problem:** A hearing-aid output is rated at **105 dB SPL**. What is the corresponding acoustic pressure?

**Step 1 — Recall the inverse SPL formula:**

$$
p = p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p / 20}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
p = (2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}) \cdot 10^{105 / 20}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent:**

$$
10^{105 / 20} = 10^{5.25}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $10^{5.25}$:**

$$
10^{5.25} = 10^5 \cdot 10^{0.25} = 100{,}000 \times 1.7783 = 177{,}828
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $p_0$:**

$$
p = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 177{,}828 \approx 3.56\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Result:** The hearing aid can deliver an acoustic pressure of **≈ 3.56 Pa** — about **178,000 times** the threshold of hearing. This is well below the pain threshold (≈ 20 Pa), confirming the device is safe for sustained use.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Tympanometric Peak Pressure & Compliance

**Problem:** A tympanogram yields the following data:

| Ear Canal Pressure (daPa) | Compliance (cm³) |
|---------------------------|------------------|
| −300 | 0.4 |
| −200 | 0.7 |
| −100 | 1.4 |
| 0 | 2.1 |
| +100 | 1.5 |
| +200 | 0.8 |
| +300 | 0.4 |

**Step 1 — Identify Peak Compliance:** Maximum value = **2.1 cm³** at **0 daPa** (i.e., ambient atmospheric pressure).

**Step 2 — Classify the Tympanogram (Liden/Jerger):**

- Peak pressure between −100 and +50 daPa → **Type A (Normal)**
- Peak compliance 0.3 – 1.6 cm³ → Normal range

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The middle-ear compliance peaks at **ambient pressure**, and the absolute compliance (2.1 cm³) is **slightly hyper-compliant** — suggestive of a flaccid tympanic membrane or ossicular discontinuity.
> Acoustic reflex testing is recommended next.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection by Pathology

| Clinical Question | Best Test | Why |
|-------------------|-----------|-----|
| Newborn hearing screening? | OAE + ABR | Objective, rapid, no response needed |
| Quantify degree of loss in cooperative adult? | Pure-Tone Audiometry | Gold standard, frequency-specific |
| Differentiate conductive vs sensorineural? | AC + BC audiometry | Air–Bone Gap |
| Middle-ear fluid in a child? | Tympanometry | Detects effusion (Type B) |
| Cochlear implant candidacy? | ABR + behavioral audiometry | Confirms profound loss |
| Retrocochlear lesion (e.g., acoustic neuroma)? | ABR with inter-peak latencies | Wave I–V delay |
| Functional / exaggerated loss? | OAE + ABR vs. behavioral | Cross-check inconsistency |
| Hearing aid fitting verification? | Real-Ear Measurement (REM) | In-situ SPL measurement |

---

## 3.6 Speech Audiometry — Key Derived Metrics

### Speech Recognition Threshold (SRT)

The lowest speech level (in dB HL) at which a person can correctly identify **50%** of spondee (two-syllable, equally stressed) words.

### Speech Discrimination Score (SDS) / Word Recognition Score (WRS)

$$
\text{WRS} (\%) = \frac{\text{Number of phonemes/words correctly repeated}}{\text{Total number presented}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** WRS should be obtained at a level **above SRT** (typically SRT + 30–40 dB). Reporting WRS at SRT is meaningless because audibility — not clarity — is the limiting factor.

**Acceptable WRS values:**

| WRS (%) | Performance | Rehabilitation Strategy |
|---------|-------------|-------------------------|
| 90 – 100 | Excellent | Standard hearing aid |
| 75 – 89 | Good | Hearing aid |
| 60 – 74 | Fair | Hearing aid with directional mic |
| 50 – 59 | Poor | Power aid, communication training |
| < 50 | Very poor | Consider CI, lip-reading |

---

## 3.7 Masking — Engineering of Cross-Hearing Elimination

When the test ear's AC threshold exceeds the non-test ear's BC (or even AC) threshold by an interaural attenuation margin, **cross-hearing** occurs. The non-test ear hears the signal, giving a falsely good threshold.

**Minimum Interaural Attenuation (IA) values:**

| Transducer | IA (dB) |
|------------|---------|
| Headphones (AC) | 40 |
| Insert earphones (AC) | 70 |
| Bone vibrator (BC) | 0 |

**Masking Rule (Plateau Method):**
1. Apply masking noise to the non-test ear at **Effective Masking Level (EM)**.
2. EM = Non-test-ear BC threshold + IA.
3. Increase masker in 10 dB steps until the test-ear threshold **stabilizes** across a 20–30 dB masker range → that is the **true masked threshold**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Overmasking** can shift the masker into the test ear, producing a falsely elevated masked threshold. Always check: $\text{EM}_{\text{max}} = \text{Test-ear AC} - \text{IA}_{\text{earphone}} + \text{IA}_{\text{BC}}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Comprehensive Hearing Assessment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Presents with Hearing Complaint] --> B[History Taking and Otoscopic Examination]
    B --> C{Tympanic Membrane Status}
    C -->|Normal| D[Tympanometry]
    C -->|Abnormal| E[Medical Referral First]
    D --> F{Type A Tympanogram}
    D --> G{Type B or C Tympanogram}
    F --> H[Pure-Tone Audiometry AC plus BC]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute PTA and ABG per Frequency]
    I --> J{ABG greater than 10 dB}
    J -->|Yes| K[Conductive or Mixed Loss]
    J -->|No| L[Sensorineural Loss]
    K --> M[Medical ENT Consultation]
    L --> N[Degree Classification WHO 2024]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Rehabilitation Decision}
    O -->|Mild to Moderate| P[Hearing Aid Trial with NAL-NL2 Fitting]
    O -->|Severe to Profound| Q[Cochlear Implant Evaluation with ABR and ASSR]
    O -->|Profound with no CI access| R[Manual Communication and Tactile Aids]
    P --> S[Real Ear Measurement Verification]
    Q --> T[Mapping and Switch on]
    S --> U[Outcome Validation Aided Audiogram and WRS]
    T --> U
    R --> U
    H --> V[Speech Audiometry SRT and WRS]
    V --> U
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Signal Path Inside a Clinical Audiometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oscillator Bank 250 to 8000 Hz] --> B[Attenuator Network 0 to 120 dB HL]
    B --> C[Modulator Warble or Pulse Tone]
    C --> D[Output Selector AC or BC]
    D --> E[Headphones TDH-39 or Insert ER-3A]
    D --> F[Bone Vibrator B-71 placed on Mastoid]
    E --> G[Patient Response Button]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Threshold Tracking by Modified Hughson Westlake]
    H --> I[Audiogram Plot Frequency vs dB HL]
    I --> J[Interpretation PTA ABG Classification]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR) Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acoustic Stimulus Click or Tone Burst] --> B[Insert Earphone]
    B --> C[Cochlea]
    C --> D[Auditory Nerve Wave I]
    D --> E[Cochlear Nucleus Wave II]
    E --> F[Superior Olivary Complex Wave III]
    F --> G[Lateral Lemniscus Wave IV]
    G --> H[Inferior Colliculus Wave V]
    H --> I[Scalp Electrodes]
    I --> J[Differential Amplifier Gain 100000]
    J --> K[Analog Filter 100 to 3000 Hz]
    K --> L[Analog to Digital Converter]
    L --> M[Signal Averager 1000 to 2000 Sweeps]
    M --> N[Waveform Display Wave I to V Latencies]
    N --> O{Interpretation}
    O -->|All waves present at expected latency| P[Normal ABR]
    O -->|Wave V delayed or absent| Q[Retrocochlear or Severe Loss]
    O -->|No waves at high intensity| R[Profound Hearing Loss]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Diagnostic Decision Tree for Type of Loss

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thresholds Obtained AC and BC] --> B{AC threshold}
    B -->|Within 20 dB HL of normal| C[Normal Hearing]
    B -->|Above 20 dB HL| D{BC threshold}
    D -->|Within 20 dB HL of normal| E[Conductive Loss]
    D -->|Above 20 dB HL| F{ABG greater than 10 dB}
    F -->|Yes| G[Mixed Loss]
    F -->|No| H[Sensorineural Loss]
    H --> I{Wave I to V interpeak latency on ABR}
    I -->|Normal| J[Cochlear Pathology]
    I -->|Delayed| K[Retrocochlear Pathology e.g. Acoustic Neuroma]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Hearing Aid Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microphone] --> B[Pre Amplifier]
    B --> C[Analog to Digital Converter]
    C --> D[Digital Filter Bank Multi Channel]
    D --> E[Noise Reduction Algorithm]
    E --> F[Feedback Cancellation]
    F --> G[Compression Amplifier WDRC]
    G --> H[Digital to Analog Converter]
    H --> I[Receiver Speaker]
    I --> J[Ear Canal Residual SPL]
    J --> K[Probe Microphone for Real Ear Measurement]
    K --> L[Target Curve Match NAL-NL2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each block above is a node where the rehabilitation engineer makes design or fitting decisions — illustrating why **hearing assessment data** (audiogram shape, UCL, WRS) is the *input* that drives every block.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define dB SPL and dB HL. Why are both needed in clinical audiology? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

- **dB SPL (Sound Pressure Level):** A logarithmic measure of acoustic pressure relative to the standard reference pressure $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$.
$$L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$$
- **dB HL (Hearing Level):** A frequency-specific measure that expresses a sound's level relative to the **average threshold of a young, otologically normal adult** at that frequency.
$$\text{HL} = \text{SPL}_{\text{stimulus}} - \text{RET}_{\text{freq}}$$

**Why both are needed:** dB SPL is an absolute physical quantity, while dB HL normalizes the audiometer's display so that "0 dB" represents the boundary of normal hearing at every audiometric frequency. Without HL, audiograms across patients/frequencies would not be comparable.

---

### Q2. What is the Air–Bone Gap, and what does an ABG of 25 dB at 1000 Hz clinically indicate? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Air–Bone Gap (ABG)** is the difference between the air-conduction and bone-conduction thresholds at a given frequency:

$$
\text{ABG} = \text{AC}_{\text{threshold}} - \text{BC}_{\text{threshold}}
$$

A normal ABG is **≤ 10 dB**. An ABG of **25 dB at 1000 Hz** indicates a **conductive component** in the hearing loss, meaning the outer or middle ear is mechanically impairing sound transmission (e.g., otitis media, ossicular fixation, tympanic membrane perforation). It warrants ENT referral in addition to audiologic rehabilitation.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions, marks are heavily lost when students (a) forget to specify units on **every** numerical answer, (b) skip the WHO classification step, (c) compute PTA using non-standard frequencies (e.g., 250 Hz or 8000 Hz), and (d) confuse dB SPL with dB HL. Always present answers in a **tabular + interpretative** format.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a **clinical pure-tone audiometer**. List the standard audiometric frequencies and explain the modified Hughson–Westlake method of threshold estimation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **PTA** and **Speech Recognition Threshold (SRT)**. A patient yields the following audiogram:

| Frequency (Hz) | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 | 8000 |
|----------------|-----|-----|------|------|------|------|
| AC Threshold (dB HL) | 35 | 40 | 50 | 55 | 70 | 75 |

Determine the PTA, classify the hearing loss using WHO 2024, and explain the rehabilitation strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution A(a)

**Working Principle of Pure-Tone Audiometer:**

The audiometer generates **pure-tone sine waves** at calibrated frequencies and intensities, delivers them to the patient's ear via air-conduction (headphones) or bone-conduction (bone vibrator) transducers, and records the patient's responses.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Oscillator Bank → Attenuator → Modulator (Warble/Pulse)
       → Output Selector (AC/BC) → Transducer (Headphone/Bone Vibrator)
       → Patient Response Button → Threshold Tracking Algorithm
       → Audiogram Display
```

**Standard Audiometric Frequencies:** **250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000 Hz** (plus intermediate 3000, 6000 Hz clinically).

**Modified Hughson–Westlake Method (ISO 8253-1):**

1. Present tone at an **audible** level (start ~30 dB above expected threshold).
2. Decrease in **10 dB** steps until no response.
3. Increase in **5 dB** steps until response obtained.
4. Repeat ascending-descending runs.
5. **Threshold** = lowest level at which responses occur in **≥ 50%** of trials (typically 2 out of 3 or 3 out of 5 ascending trials).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution A(b)

**PTA Definition:**

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{\text{HL}_{500} + \text{HL}_{1000} + \text{HL}_{2000} + \text{HL}_{4000}}{4}
$$

**SRT Definition:** Lowest intensity (dB HL) at which a patient can correctly repeat **50%** of spondaic words.

**Step 1 — Compute PTA:**

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{40 + 50 + 55 + 70}{4} = \frac{215}{4} = 53.75\ \text{dB HL}
$$

**Step 2 — WHO 2024 Classification:**

PTA = 53.75 falls in the 51–65 dB HL band → **Grade 3: Moderately Severe hearing loss**.

**Step 3 — Rehabilitation Strategy:**

- Prescribe a **mid-to-high-power digital hearing aid** with multi-channel WDRC.
- Fit using **NAL-NL2** prescriptive targets.
- Counsel on **communication strategies** and **lip-reading** support.
- Verify with **Real-Ear Measurement (REM)**.
- Schedule **aided audiogram** and **WRS testing** at follow-up.
- Consider **assistive listening devices** (FM/loop) for classroom/office use.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** What is **Tympanometry**? With a sketch of a normal **Type A tympanogram**, explain how peak pressure and static compliance are interpreted. Discuss the clinical significance of **Type B** and **Type C** tympanograms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Otoacoustic Emissions (OAE)** and **Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR)** as objective tests of hearing. Compare their strengths, limitations, and specific clinical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution B(a)

**Tympanometry Definition:**

Tympanometry is an **objective immittance test** that measures the **compliance (or acoustic energy transmission) of the middle-ear system** as a function of the air pressure applied to the ear canal (typically swept from +200 daPa to −400 daPa).

**Normal Type A Tympanogram (description):**

The curve peaks sharply at **0 daPa** (ambient pressure), with static compliance between **0.3 and 1.6 cm³**, producing a symmetric "tent-shaped" peak centered on atmospheric pressure.

```
Compliance (cm³)
   1.6 ┤        ╱╲
   1.0 ┤      ╱    ╲
   0.5 ┤    ╱        ╲
   0.0 ┼──┬───┬───┬───┬──→ Pressure (daPa)
      -400 -200   0 +200
```

**Interpretation Parameters:**

1. **Peak Pressure** — air pressure at which compliance is maximum.
2. **Static Compliance** — compliance at peak minus compliance at +200 daPa tail.
3. **Ear Canal Volume** — measured at +200 daPa tail (abnormally high suggests TM perforation).

**Type B (Flat) Tympanogram:**

- Flat trace with no peak.
- Compliance < 0.3 cm³.
- **Clinical significance:** Middle-ear effusion (fluid behind TM), TM perforation, or cerumen occlusion.
- Differentiation via ear-canal volume: small volume → effusion; large volume → perforation.

**Type C Tympanogram:**

- Peak pressure shifted to **negative pressures** (< −100 daPa).
- Normal compliance.
- **Clinical significance:** Eustachian-tube dysfunction; negative middle-ear pressure often precedes effusion.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution B(b)

**Otoacoustic Emissions (OAE):**

- Sounds generated by the **outer hair cells (OHC)** of the cochlea, measurable in the ear canal using a sensitive microphone.
- Two main types: **TEOAE** (Transient Evoked — click stimulus) and **DPOAE** (Distortion Product — two-tone stimulus).
- **Indicator of normal OHC function** (cochlear amplifier intact).
- **Strengths:** Rapid (seconds), non-invasive, frequency-specific (DPOAE), excellent newborn screening tool.
- **Limitations:** Cannot detect retrocochlear pathology; affected by middle-ear pathology (false negatives); requires ear-canal seal.

**Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR):**

- Far-field electrophysiological recording of synchronous neural activity from the auditory nerve and brainstem (Waves I–V), evoked by click or tone-burst stimuli.
- Surface electrodes on scalp (vertex, mastoids, forehead).
- **Strengths:** Objective, frequency-specific thresholds (tone-burst ABR), wave I–V latencies detect retrocochlear pathology, works through sleep (used in infants and difficult-to-test adults).
- **Limitations:** Expensive, time-consuming (30–60 min), skilled interpretation required, click ABR lacks frequency specificity (use tone-burst ABR or ASSR for that).

**Comparative Summary:**

| Parameter | OAE | ABR |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Anatomical site | Outer hair cells (cochlea) | CN VIII → brainstem |
| Stimulus | Click (TEOAE), two tones (DPOAE) | Click / tone burst |
| Recording time | Seconds | 30–60 min |
| Frequency specificity | Yes (DPOAE) | Yes (tone-burst ABR / ASSR) |
| Detects retrocochlear? | No | Yes (wave I–V latency) |
| Newborn screening | Yes (first-line) | Yes (second-line / confirmatory) |
| Sedation needed? | No | Sometimes in infants |

**Clinical Application Examples:**

- **Universal Newborn Hearing Screening (UNHS):** Step 1 — TEOAE/DPOAE; Step 2 — ABR if refer.
- **CI Candidacy:** Tone-burst ABR thresholds supplement behavioral audiometry in young children.
- **Acoustic Neuroma Detection:** ABR shows delayed or absent Wave V on the affected side.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hearing Function Assessment**

- ✅ The **decibel** is logarithmic: $20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ for SPL, $10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ for intensity.
- ✅ Standard reference pressure $p_0 = \mathbf{20\ \mu Pa}$; reference intensity $I_0 = \mathbf{10^{-12}\ W/m^2}$.
- ✅ **0 dB HL ≠ 0 dB SPL** — HL is frequency-normalized to the young-normal-ear threshold.
- ✅ Standard audiometric frequencies: **250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000 Hz** (and intermediate 3 kHz, 6 kHz).
- ✅ **PTA** uses only **500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz** (the speech-frequency octaves).
- ✅ **WHO 2024:** Mild 21–35, Moderate 36–50, Moderately Severe 51–65, Severe 66–80, Profound ≥ 81 dB HL.
- ✅ **Air–Bone Gap (ABG):** Conductive loss shows ABG > 10 dB with normal BC; Sensorineural loss shows AC ≈ BC.
- ✅ **Pure-Tone Audiometry** is the gold standard for frequency-specific behavioral threshold.
- ✅ **Speech Audiometry** yields **SRT** (spondees at 50%) and **WRS** (mono-syllables at suprathreshold).
- ✅ **Tympanometry** is objective, assesses middle-ear compliance vs pressure:
  - Type A → Normal
  - Type B → Flat → Effusion / perforation
  - Type C → Negative peak → Eustachian-tube dysfunction
- ✅ **OAE** → Outer Hair Cell (Cochlear) function; absent in SN loss > 30 dB.
- ✅ **ABR** → Neural pathway (Wave I–V); delayed Wave V suggests retrocochlear lesion.
- ✅ **Interaural Attenuation:** Headphones = 40 dB; Inserts = 70 dB; Bone = 0 dB.
- ✅ **Modified Hughson–Westlake:** Threshold = lowest dB at which ≥ 50% of ascending trials produce responses.
- ✅ **Rehabilitation flow:** Assessment → Diagnosis → Device selection (Hearing Aid / CI) → Fitting (NAL-NL2 / DSL) → Verification (REM) → Validation (aided WRS).
- ✅ Always **mask** the non-test ear when the test-ear threshold exceeds the non-test-ear threshold by ≥ IA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Tympanogram Types:** "**A**mazing **N**ormal, **B**locked by **F**luid, **C**hoked by **E**ustachian" — Type A Normal, Type B Fluid/Perforation, Type C Eustachian dysfunction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hearing Assistance Technology Solutions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hearing Assistance Technology Solutions

# 🔊 Hearing Assistance Technology Solutions

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Hearing Assistance Technology (HAT)** refers to a comprehensive class of electroacoustic, electronic, and biomedical engineering devices designed to amplify, modulate, or directly stimulate the auditory pathway in individuals with hearing impairment. It encompasses conventional air-conduction **hearing aids**, surgically implanted **cochlear implants**, **bone-anchored hearing systems (BAHS)**, and environmental assistive listening systems such as **induction loops** and **FM/RF systems**.

## 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spectacles for the Ear"

Imagine a person with weak eyesight wearing spectacles. The lens does not "cure" the eye; it simply corrects the focal point so the brain receives a sharper image. Similarly, hearing assistance technology does not regenerate damaged hair cells inside the cochlea — it **compensates** by either:

- **Amplifying** the incoming sound (conventional hearing aids),
- **Bypassing** the damaged cochlea entirely and stimulating the auditory nerve electrically (cochlear implants), or
- **Re-routing** sound through bone conduction when the outer/middle ear is impaired (BAHA/Bonebridge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Auditory Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** measured in **dB SPL** (reference: **20 µPa**)
> - **Hearing Level (HL)** measured in **dB HL**
> - **Frequency range of human hearing:** **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz**
> - **Speech banana zone:** 250 Hz – 8 kHz at 30–60 dB SPL
> - **Conversational speech intensity:** ≈ **60 dB SPL at 1 meter**

## 🏗️ Classification of Hearing Assistance Solutions

Hearing assistance technology can be hierarchically classified as:

1. **Personal Amplification Devices (Hearing Aids)**
   - Body-worn (rare today)
   - Behind-The-Ear (BTE)
   - In-The-Ear (ITE)
   - In-The-Canal (ITC)
   - Completely-In-Canal (CIC)
   - Receiver-In-Canal (RIC)

2. **Implantable Devices**
   - Cochlear Implants (CI)
   - Bone-Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHA)
   - Middle Ear Implants (MEI)
   - Auditory Brainstem Implants (ABI)

3. **Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs)**
   - Induction Loop Systems
   - FM Systems
   - Infrared Systems
   - Bluetooth/Wireless Streaming

4. **Alerting & Signal Devices**
   - Visual alert systems (flashing lights)
   - Vibratory alert systems (bed shakers, paging devices)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧩 Functional Architecture of a Conventional Hearing Aid

Every hearing aid — regardless of form factor — contains the following functional blocks in series:

1. **Microphone (Transducer)** — converts acoustic pressure waves into a low-voltage electrical signal.
2. **Pre-amplifier / Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** — adjusts dynamic range to prevent loud sounds from over-amplifying.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — samples the signal (typical sampling rate **16 kHz – 48 kHz**).
4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — applies filtering, compression, noise reduction, feedback cancellation.
5. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — reconstructs the processed waveform.
6. **Receiver (Miniature loudspeaker)** — reconverts the electrical signal into acoustic energy delivered to the ear canal.
7. **Power source (Battery)** — typically a **zinc-air** cell with voltage **1.4 V** (size 312, 13, or 675).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Three Pillars of Hearing-Aid Performance:**
> - **Gain (G):** Amplification factor in **dB**
> - **Maximum Output (OSPL90):** Saturation SPL at 90 dB input
> - **Frequency Response:** Bandwidth of amplification (typically 200 Hz – 8 kHz)

## 📐 Key Equations in Hearing Aid Engineering

Below is the consolidated KTU formula sheet. **Note:** the `|` character is replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Typical Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $G = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | $G$ = Voltage Gain, $V$ = Voltage | **dB** | Express amplification ratio on a logarithmic scale |
| 2 | $G = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | $P$ = Acoustic Power | **dB** | Power-based gain (used for SPL) |
| 3 | $L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{0}}\right)$ | $L_p$ = SPL, $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | **dB SPL** | Convert pressure to dB SPL |
| 4 | $\text{HL} = \text{SPL} - \text{RET SPL}$ | RET = Reference Equivalent Threshold | **dB HL** | Convert SPL to Hearing Level (audiogram axis) |
| 5 | $f_{s} \ge 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = Sampling freq., $f_{max}$ = Highest signal freq. | **Hz** | Nyquist criterion for ADC in digital hearing aids |
| 6 | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | SNR = Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **dB** | Speech intelligibility metric |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{f_{0}}{\Delta f}$ | $Q$ = Quality factor, $f_0$ = Centre freq. | dimensionless | Bandwidth of a bandpass filter in DSP |
| 8 | $C = 1 + \dfrac{R_{f}}{R_{in}}$ | $C$ = Compression ratio | dimensionless | AGC/wide dynamic range compression |
| 9 | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | $E$ = Energy, $I$ = Current, $t$ = Time | **mAh** | Battery life estimation |
| 10 | $\text{DSL} = L_{\text{input}} \cdot C + O_{G}$ | DSL = Desired Sensation Level | **dB** | Pediatric fitting algorithm |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Logarithmic dB Scale?**
> The human ear responds logarithmically to sound intensity. A **10 dB** increase corresponds to a **perceived doubling of loudness**, while a **3 dB** increase corresponds to a **doubling of acoustic power**. Using dB compresses a 1,000,000:1 dynamic range into a manageable 0–120 dB scale.

## 🦻 Types of Hearing Aids — Comparative Analysis

| Type | Power Range (dB HL) | Gain | Battery Size | Suitable For | KTU Note |
|------|---------------------|------|--------------|--------------|----------|
| CIC  | Mild (25–40)        | 25–35 dB | 10         | Mild loss, cosmetic | Smallest, occludes least |
| ITC  | Mild–Moderate (40–55) | 30–45 dB | 312     | Moderate loss | Easier to handle than CIC |
| ITE  | Moderate–Severe (55–70) | 40–60 dB | 13   | Moderate loss | Larger battery, easier controls |
| BTE  | Severe–Profound (70–110) | 50–70 dB | 675 | All degrees | Most powerful, robust |
| RIC  | Mild–Severe (25–80) | 30–60 dB | 312/13 | Most losses | Open-fit comfort, popular |

## 🔄 Cochlear Implant (CI) — The Bionic Ear

A **Cochlear Implant** bypasses damaged hair cells in the cochlea and directly stimulates the **auditory nerve (CN VIII)** via an electrode array inserted into the scala tympani.

### Components:
- **External:**
  - Microphone (behind the ear)
  - Sound processor (DSP unit)
  - Transmitter coil (held magnetically over the receiver)
- **Internal (surgically implanted):**
  - Receiver/Stimulator (decodes RF signal)
  - Electrode array (typically **22 channels** for modern Nucleus/Cochlear devices)

### Signal Chain:
$$ \text{Acoustic Wave} \rightarrow \text{Mic} \rightarrow \text{Processor} \rightarrow \text{Code (CIS strategy)} \rightarrow \text{RF link} \rightarrow \text{Stimulator} \rightarrow \text{Electrode Array} \rightarrow \text{Auditory Nerve} \rightarrow \text{Brain} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know — CI Indications:**
> Bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss (>70 dB HL) where conventional hearing aids give **<50% speech recognition** in quiet. CI is **not** suitable for conductive or retrocochlear pathology.

## 📡 Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs)

| ALD Type | Working Principle | Range | KTU Application |
|----------|-------------------|-------|------------------|
| Induction Loop | Electromagnetic induction at audio frequency | Room-sized | Churches, theatres, lecture halls |
| FM System | 72–76 MHz / 216 MHz radio | 30–100 m | Classrooms for hearing-impaired students |
| Infrared | Modulated IR light (95 kHz sub-carrier) | Line-of-sight | Courtrooms, confidential settings |
| Bluetooth | 2.4 GHz digital streaming | 10 m | TV, mobile, music streaming |

The **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** improvement is typically:

$$ \text{SNR}_{improvement} = \text{SNR}_{with\, ALD} - \text{SNR}_{without\, ALD} \approx +15 \text{ to } +25 \text{ dB} $$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📘 Derivation 1 — Converting Microphone Voltage to SPL Gain

**Problem:** A hearing aid microphone produces $V_{in} = 2.5\,\text{mV}$ from a 60 dB SPL input. The amplifier delivers $V_{out} = 125\,\text{mV}$ to the receiver. Determine the voltage gain in dB.

**Step 1 — Identify knowns**

$$ V_{in} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}, \quad V_{out} = 125 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage gain formula**

$$ G = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right) $$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

$$ G = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{125 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Simplify the ratio**

$$ \frac{125 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{125}{2.5} = 50 $$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm**

$$ G = 20 \log_{10}(50) $$

$$ \log_{10}(50) = \log_{10}(5 \times 10) = \log_{10}(5) + \log_{10}(10) $$

$$ \log_{10}(5) = 0.69897, \quad \log_{10}(10) = 1 $$

$$ \log_{10}(50) = 1.69897 $$

**Step 6 — Final multiplication**

$$ G = 20 \times 1.69897 = 33.98\,\text{dB} $$

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{G \approx 34\,\text{dB}} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 34 dB gain brings a 60 dB SPL conversational input to ~94 dB SPL output — sufficient for a person with moderate hearing loss to perceive normal conversation clearly.

---

## 📘 Derivation 2 — Hearing Threshold Conversion (SPL to HL)

**Problem:** A pure tone audiometer presents a 1 kHz tone. The audiometer dial reads 40 dB HL. Convert this to SPL. (Note: At 1 kHz, the **RET SPL (ISO 8253-1)** is **7 dB**.)

**Step 1 — Recall the conversion equation**

$$ \text{SPL} = \text{HL} + \text{RET SPL} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$ \text{SPL} = 40 + 7 = 47\,\text{dB SPL} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to absolute pressure**

$$ L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \Rightarrow p = p_0 \times 10^{L_p/20} $$

$$ p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{47/20} $$

$$ p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{2.35} $$

$$ p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 223.87 $$

$$ p = 4477 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{Pa} = 4.48 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa} $$

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{\text{SPL} = 47\,\text{dB SPL} \equiv p \approx 4.48\,\text{mPa}} $$

---

## 📘 Derivation 3 — Battery Life Estimation

**Problem:** A BTE hearing aid draws $I = 1.2\,\text{mA}$ from a zinc-air battery rated at $Q = 300\,\text{mAh}$ with voltage $V = 1.4\,\text{V}$. Calculate the operating time in hours.

**Step 1 — Apply the basic battery life equation**

$$ t = \frac{Q}{I} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$ t = \frac{300\,\text{mAh}}{1.2\,\text{mA}} = 250\,\text{h} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to days**

$$ t = \frac{250}{24} \approx 10.4\,\text{days} $$

**Step 4 — Compute stored energy**

$$ E = V \times Q = 1.4 \times 300 = 420\,\text{mWh} $$

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{t \approx 250\,\text{hours} \approx 10.4\,\text{days, with } E = 420\,\text{mWh}} $$

---

## 📘 Derivation 4 — Nyquist Sampling for Digital Hearing Aid

**Problem:** A digital hearing aid must faithfully reproduce speech up to $f_{max} = 8\,\text{kHz}$. Determine the minimum ADC sampling frequency.

**Step 1 — Apply Nyquist criterion**

$$ f_s \geq 2 f_{max} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$ f_s \geq 2 \times 8000 = 16{,}000\,\text{Hz} = 16\,\text{kHz} $$

**Step 3 — Practical engineering margin**

In practice, designers choose $f_s = 1.5 \times$ to $2 \times$ the Nyquist minimum to allow for anti-aliasing filter roll-off:

$$ f_{s,practical} = 1.5 \times 16\,\text{kHz} = 24\,\text{kHz} $$

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{f_{s,min} = 16\,\text{kHz}; \quad f_{s,practical} = 24\,\text{kHz}} $$

---

## 📘 Derivation 5 — SNR Improvement Using Directional Microphone

**Problem:** An omnidirectional hearing aid gives $\text{SNR}_{omni} = +4\,\text{dB}$ in a noisy cafeteria. After switching to a directional microphone, the **Directivity Index (DI)** is **+6 dB**. Compute the new SNR.

**Step 1 — Apply the directivity principle**

$$ \text{SNR}_{directional} = \text{SNR}_{omni} + \text{DI} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$ \text{SNR}_{directional} = 4 + 6 = 10\,\text{dB} $$

**Step 3 — Compute intelligibility gain**

Since a **+10 dB SNR** is the threshold for ~98% speech intelligibility in quiet, the directional microphone restores normal comprehension.

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{\text{SNR}_{directional} = +10\,\text{dB}} $$

---

## 📊 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Device

| Patient Profile | dB HL Loss | Speech Score (PB Max) | Recommended Device | KTU Justification |
|-----------------|------------|------------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Young adult, mild high-frequency loss | 30–40 dB | >80% | RIC / Open-fit BTE | Cosmetic + open comfort |
| Elderly, moderate flat loss | 50 dB | 60–80% | ITE with telecoil | Easy handling, phone loop |
| Child, severe loss | 70–80 dB | 40–60% | Power BTE + FM system | Powerful + classroom ALD |
| Adult, profound loss | >90 dB | <30% with HA | **Cochlear Implant** | HA no longer useful |
| Conductive loss, chronic otitis | 40–60 dB | N/A (conductive) | **BAHA (bone-anchored)** | Bypasses damaged middle ear |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Functional Block Diagram of a Digital Hearing Aid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acoustic Input] --> B[MEMS Microphone]
    B --> C[Pre Amplifier with AGC]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    E --> F[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    F --> G[Receiver Loudspeaker]
    G --> H[Ear Canal Output]
    E -.feedback path.-> C
    I[Zinc Air Battery 1.4V] --> C
    I --> D
    I --> E
    I --> F
    I --> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reading Tip:** Trace signal flow left-to-right. Note the **feedback cancellation** loop (dotted) that prevents the high-gain receiver output from re-entering the microphone — a key KTU viva question.

---

## Diagram 2 — Cochlear Implant System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Sound Waves] --> A2[External Microphone]
    A2 --> A3[Sound Processor DSP]
    A3 --> A4[RF Transmitter Coil]
    A4 -->|Magnetic Induction| A5[Internal Receiver Stimulator]
    A5 --> A6[Electrode Array in Scala Tympani]
    A6 --> A7[Auditory Nerve CN VIII]
    A7 --> A8[Brainstem and Auditory Cortex]
    A9[Battery Pack] --> A3
```

---

## Diagram 3 — Decision Flowchart for Device Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Patient with Hearing Loss] --> S1{Type of Loss?}
    S1 -->|Conductive| S2{Outer Middle Ear Status?}
    S1 -->|Sensorineural| S3{Threshold in dB HL?}
    S1 -->|Mixed| S4[Combine Surgical and Amplification]

    S2 -->|Chronic Otitis Atresia| S5[BAHA / Bonebridge]
    S2 -->|Otosclerosis| S6[Stapedectomy or HA]

    S3 -->|25 to 40 dB| S7[CIC or RIC]
    S3 -->|41 to 70 dB| S8[ITE or BTE]
    S3 -->|71 to 90 dB| S9[Power BTE plus FM]
    S3 -->|Greater than 90 dB| S10{Speech Score?}

    S10 -->|Less than 50 percent| S11[Cochlear Implant]
    S10 -->|More than 50 percent| S9
```

---

## Diagram 4 — Assistive Listening System Coverage Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Speaker Source] --> P2[Microphone or Line Input]
    P2 --> P3[Transmitter]
    P3 --> P4[FM Radio 72 to 76 MHz]
    P3 --> P5[Induction Loop Amp]
    P3 --> P6[IR LED Array]
    P4 --> P7[Personal FM Receiver]
    P5 --> P8[Hearing Aid Telecoil]
    P6 --> P9[IR Receiver Headset]
    P7 --> Q1[User Ears]
    P8 --> Q1
    P9 --> Q1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ✏️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define a hearing aid. List the four basic components of any air-conduction hearing aid.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **hearing aid** is a personal electroacoustic device worn on the ear that amplifies sound to compensate for reduced auditory sensitivity in a hearing-impaired individual.

**Four basic components (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Microphone** — picks up acoustic energy and converts it to an electrical signal.
2. **Amplifier (with AGC/DSP)** — increases signal strength and shapes frequency response.
3. **Receiver (earphone)** — converts the amplified electrical signal back into sound.
4. **Battery (zinc-air 1.4 V)** — powers the circuit.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between a hearing aid and a cochlear implant.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hearing Aid (1.5 m) | Cochlear Implant (1.5 m) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Action | Acoustic amplification (1 m) | Electrical stimulation of auditory nerve (1 m) |
| Indication | Mild–severe sensorineural loss | Severe–profound loss (>70 dB HL) |
| Placement | External | External processor + internal electrode array |
| Surgical | Non-invasive | Invasive (mastoidectomy + cochleostomy) |

---

## ✏️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a digital hearing aid. Discuss the role of the DSP in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working principle of a cochlear implant. List its external and internal components and the patient selection criteria. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Digital Hearing Aid — Block Diagram (3 marks) + DSP Role (4 marks):**

The signal chain is:

$$ \text{Mic} \rightarrow \text{Pre-amp + AGC} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{DSP} \rightarrow \text{DAC} \rightarrow \text{Receiver} \rightarrow \text{Ear} $$

**Working explanation (3 marks):**

1. The **microphone** converts sound pressure variations into a small analog voltage (typically 1–10 mV).
2. The **AGC** automatically adjusts gain for soft vs loud sounds, preventing discomfort.
3. The **ADC** samples the signal at $f_s \geq 16\,\text{kHz}$ per Nyquist.
4. The **DSP** performs the bulk of the signal processing.
5. The **DAC** and **receiver** deliver amplified sound to the ear canal.

**Role of DSP — four key functions (4 marks):**

1. **Frequency-shaping filtering** — boosts the patient's specific deficient frequencies (e.g., 2–4 kHz for presbycusis).
2. **Wide Dynamic Range Compression (WDRC)** — maps the 100+ dB input range into the patient's reduced dynamic range.
3. **Noise Reduction algorithms** — spectral subtraction or modulation-based.
4. **Feedback cancellation** — adaptive notch filtering to prevent oscillation.

---

**(b) Cochlear Implant — Working (3 marks) + Components (2 marks) + Selection (2 marks):**

**Working principle (3 marks):**

A CI bypasses the damaged cochlear hair cells. The external microphone and processor encode speech using a **Continuous Interleaved Sampling (CIS)** strategy, where channels are stimulated sequentially to avoid channel interaction. The coded signal is transmitted via a **transcutaneous RF link** (typically 5 MHz) to the implanted receiver-stimulator, which delivers biphasic current pulses to electrodes placed in the **scala tympani**. These currents depolarize the spiral ganglion neurons, generating action potentials in the auditory nerve that the brain interprets as sound.

**Components (2 marks):**

- *External:* Microphone, sound processor, transmitter coil with magnet.
- *Internal:* Receiver-stimulator hermetically sealed in titanium, 22-channel electrode array, ground electrode.

**Patient selection criteria (2 marks):**

1. Bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss (PTA > 70 dB HL).
2. Limited benefit from conventional hearing aids (speech score < 50% in best-aided condition).
3. Intact auditory nerve and patent cochlea (confirmed by CT/MRI).
4. Realistic expectations and strong rehabilitation commitment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the different types/styles of hearing aids. Compare their gains and indications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of an induction loop system and FM system as assistive listening devices. How do they improve the SNR for the hearing-impaired listener? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Styles of Hearing Aids (7 marks):**

| Style (1 m each = 4 m) | Description & Gain (1 m) | Indication (2 m) |
|------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **CIC** (1 m) | Deepest canal placement, gain 25–35 dB (1 m) | Mild loss, cosmetic priority |
| **ITC** (1 m) | Canal entry, gain 30–45 dB (1 m) | Mild–moderate, easier handling |
| **ITE** (1 m) | Concha-filling, gain 40–60 dB (1 m) | Moderate loss |
| **BTE** (1 m) | Behind ear with earmold, gain 50–70 dB (1 m) | Severe–profound loss |
| **RIC** (1 m) | Receiver in canal, gain 30–60 dB (1 m) | Open-fit, mild–severe |
| **Body-worn** (1 m) | Pocket amplifier with button receiver, gain 60+ dB (1 m) | Profound loss, developing nations |

---

**(b) Induction Loop & FM Systems (7 marks):**

**Induction Loop System (3.5 marks):**

A wire is laid around the perimeter of a room and driven by a loop amplifier connected to the venue's audio source. The current at audio frequency (50 Hz – 8 kHz) creates a **magnetic field** (vertical orientation) within the looped area. A hearing aid switched to the **"T" (telecoil)** position picks up this magnetic field and converts it back to sound. The user receives the audio **directly** into the hearing aid with no ambient noise pickup. The system is governed by **IEC 60118-4** standard specifying a magnetic field strength of **400 mA/m** at 1 kHz reference.

**FM System (3.5 marks):**

The speaker wears (or holds) a wireless **microphone-transmitter** that broadcasts on VHF (72–76 MHz in the US, 173–216 MHz in Europe) or 2.4 GHz digital. The listener wears a personal **receiver** (or a streamer coupled to the hearing aid) that captures the broadcast and delivers it via an earphone or direct-audio-input (DAI) to the hearing aid. Working range is **30–100 m**, ideal for classrooms, where the speaker's voice SNR is improved by **+15 to +20 dB**.

**SNR improvement formula (cross-cutting):**

$$ \text{SNR}_{improved} = \text{SNR}_{direct\, mic} + \Delta_{gain} - \text{Room noise pickup} $$

With both ALDs, the room reverberation and noise pickup at the hearing-aid microphone is eliminated, so:

$$ \text{SNR}_{ALDS} \approx \text{SNR}_{direct} + 18\,\text{dB (typical)} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "dB SPL" with "dB HL"** — they are not the same. Always state the reference (20 µPa for SPL; audiometric zero for HL).
> 2. **Stating CI as "cure for deafness"** — it is a *prosthetic* device, not a cure. The user still needs auditory-verbal therapy.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the block diagram in (a) of Question A** — 3 marks out of 7 are awarded for the diagram alone.
> 4. **Mixing up "telecoil" and "Bluetooth"** — telecoil is an **inductive** analog pickup; Bluetooth is a **digital** 2.4 GHz protocol. Examiners mark this as a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Writing "BAHA" without expansion** — full form is **Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid** (or BAHS = System).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Hearing Assistance Technology Solutions**

- ✅ Hearing aids **amplify**; cochlear implants **stimulate electrically**; BAHA **re-routes via bone**.
- ✅ The **human ear's dynamic range** is **0–120 dB HL**; conversational speech is **~60 dB SPL at 1 m**.
- ✅ **Nyquist rate** for 8 kHz speech = **16 kHz** minimum sampling.
- ✅ **dB SPL = 20 log₁₀(p/20 µPa)**, while **dB HL = SPL − RET SPL**.
- ✅ **A 10 dB increase ≈ perceived doubling of loudness**; a **3 dB increase ≈ doubling of acoustic power**.
- ✅ **CI candidates** have bilateral severe-to-profound loss (>70 dB HL) with <50% aided speech score.
- ✅ **BTE aids** are the most powerful (gain up to 70 dB); **CIC** aids are the most cosmetic but weakest.
- ✅ **ALC = Automatic Level Control** (output limiter); **AGC = Automatic Gain Control** (input adjustment); **WDRC = Wide Dynamic Range Compression** (multi-channel).
- ✅ **ALC** is input protection; **AGC** is output shaping.
- ✅ **Induction loop** uses magnetic field at audio frequency; **FM** uses VHF radio; both cut out reverberation.
- ✅ **IEC 60118-4** specifies loop field strength = **400 mA/m**.
- ✅ **Battery** of a hearing aid is typically a **zinc-air** cell, **1.4 V**, sizes 10/13/312/675.
- ✅ **Telecoil ("T" mode)** picks up induction loops; **"M" mode** is microphone; **"MT"** is both.
- ✅ **Directional microphone** improves SNR by **+3 to +8 dB** depending on Directivity Index.
- ✅ **Directivity Index (DI)** is measured in **dB** and quantifies the microphone's spatial noise rejection.
- ✅ **CI electrode arrays** are inserted into the **scala tympani** of the **basal turn** of the cochlea.
- ✅ **Signal chain of a hearing aid** = Mic → Pre-amp → AGC → ADC → DSP → DAC → Receiver → Ear.
- ✅ **DSL (Desired Sensation Level)** is the gold-standard pediatric fitting formula; **NAL-NL2** is for adults.
- ✅ **Cross-over frequency** between hearing aid and CI candidacy is typically **around 70 dB HL PTA**.
- ✅ **BAHA** is indicated for **conductive or mixed hearing loss** with intact cochlear function.
- ✅ **KEMAR (Knowles Electronics Manikin for Acoustic Research)** is the standard mannequin used to measure hearing aid performance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surgical Approaches - Middle-Ear Implants (MEIs) and Bone Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHAs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Surgical Approaches - Middle-Ear Implants (MEIs) and Bone Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHAs) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Middle-Ear Implants (MEIs) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** A *Middle-Ear Implant (MEI)*, also termed an *Active Middle Ear Implant (AMEI)* or *Middle Ear Transducer (MET)*, is a surgically implanted prosthetic device that directly mechanically vibrates the ossicular chain (malleus, incus, stapes) or the cochlear round/oval window via an electromagnetic or piezoelectric transducer, bypassing the external auditory canal and tympanic membrane to rehabilitate patients with sensorineural, conductive, or mixed hearing loss. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** MEIs are *active* (require power) implants that drive the ossicles mechanically. They are fundamentally different from *passive* middle-ear prostheses (e.g., TORPs/PORPs used in ossiculoplasty), which merely restore anatomical continuity. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a conventional hearing aid as someone *shouting into a microphone placed at the doorway of a noisy room* — much of the amplified sound leaks out or distorts. An MEI, in contrast, is like placing a *subwoofer directly behind the orchestra pit* — it vibrates the stage floor (ossicles) from inside, delivering clean, undistorted mechanical energy straight to the inner ear. The patient essentially receives "private, in-ear amplification" with the speaker hidden behind the eardrum. **Key Components & Operating Frequencies (Standard KTU Metrics):** | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Operating Frequency Range | **250 Hz – 8 kHz** | | Gain | **30 – 65 dB** | | Maximum Output | **110 – 130 dB SPL** | | Power Source | External speech processor (battery) + RF-coupled internal receiver | --- ## 1.2 Bone Anchored Hearing Aids (BAHAs) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** A *Bone Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA)* is a percutaneous or transcutaneous osseointegrated auditory prosthesis that delivers amplified acoustic energy to the cochlea via direct *bone conduction* through a titanium fixture surgically implanted in the temporal bone (mastoid), bypassing the external ear canal, tympanic membrane, and ossicular chain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight — Osseointegration:** Coined by Professor Per-Ingvar Brånemark (1952), osseointegration is the *direct structural and functional connection between living bone and the surface of a load-bearing artificial implant* (typically commercially pure titanium, **3 – 5 µm TiO₂ surface oxide layer**). **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine you are listening to music with headphones on, but instead of the sound entering through your ears, you feel the bass vibrations through the floorboards of a wooden dance floor — *that's bone conduction*. A BAHA is essentially a *tuned "bass-shaker" bolted directly into the skull*. Because the cochlea responds to vibration regardless of where the vibration originates, stimulating the temporal bone is just as effective (often more so) as stimulating the eardrum — particularly when the middle ear is diseased. **Key Operating Parameters:** | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Frequency Range | **250 Hz – 7 kHz** | | Direct Bone Conduction Gain | **30 – 45 dB** | | Osseointegration Period | **3 – 6 months** (two-stage) / **immediate** (single-stage) | | Fixture Diameter | **3.75 mm** or **4.5 mm** | > [!NOTE] > **Distinction:** A BAHA is *not* a cochlear implant. A cochlear implant stimulates the auditory nerve electrically inside the cochlea; a BAHA merely delivers amplified mechanical vibration *to* the cochlea, leaving the natural auditory transduction untouched. --- ## 1.3 Why Surgical Approaches? The Clinical Gap Conventional *air-conduction hearing aids* fail in specific patient populations: - **Chronic otitis media / otitis externa** (drainage disqualifies ear-mold use) - **Aural atresia / microtia** (no ear canal exists) - **Single-sided deafness (SSD)** (CROS aids are cosmetically and acoustically suboptimal) - **Severe mixed hearing loss** (large air-bone gap >30 dB) For these cases, MEIs and BAHAs provide *bypass routes* around the diseased segment. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Middle-Ear Implants — Operational Logic Steps **Step 1 — Sound Capture (External Stage):** The external *sound processor* (worn behind the ear like a conventional BTE aid) captures airborne sound via microphones, performs digital signal processing (DSP), and encodes it as a radio-frequency (RF) signal. **Why?** Encoding as RF avoids percutaneous wires (infection risk) and allows the sound processor to be upgraded without further surgery. **Step 2 — Transcutaneous RF Link:** The external coil transmits the RF signal across intact skin to a *subcutaneous receiver-stimulator* package anchored to the mastoid cortex. **How?** Electromagnetic induction (≈ 5 MHz carrier frequency in the Vibrant Soundbridge system). **Step 3 — Mechanical Transduction:** Inside the receiver-stimulator, a *Floating Mass Transducer (FMT)* — a tiny electromagnetic actuator weighing ~ 25 mg — converts electrical current into *reciprocating mechanical vibration* of an inertial mass. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Floating Mass" Concept:** Because the FMT's inertial mass is *floating* (not anchored to the skull), Newton's Third Law dictates that when the coil drives the mass, the *mass pushes against the surrounding structure* (ossicle or round window), producing mechanical vibration of the target. **Step 4 — Coupling to the Inner Ear:** The FMT's vibrating tip is surgically attached to the: - Long process of the **incus** (most common, e.g., Vibrant Soundbridge), OR - **Stapes** footplate (in revision cases), OR - **Round window** niche (for conductive/mixed loss — RW-VSB technique) **Step 5 — Cochlear Stimulation:** The ossicular vibration is transduced by the inner hair cells of the *organ of Corti* into neural impulses, identical to the natural acoustic pathway. --- ## 2.2 BAHA — Operational Logic Steps **Step 1 — Fixture Implantation (Stage 1 Surgery):** A **3.75 mm** or **4.5 mm diameter** titanium screw is drilled into the mastoid cortex at a 90° angle (perpendicular to bone surface) to a depth of **3 mm** or **4 mm**. **Why titanium?** Titanium forms a stable titanium-oxide (TiO₂) layer that bone tissue integrates with directly, without fibrous encapsulation. **Step 2 — Osseointegration Period (3–6 months):** Bone grows directly into the microscopic surface irregularities of the implant (*osseointegration*).
