```mermaid
    style sub fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define retinal implant. Name the two major retinal degenerative diseases for which it is prescribed.*

**Model Answer:**
A retinal implant is an implantable electronic neural prosthesis that electrically stimulates surviving inner retinal neurons to produce artificial visual perception in patients with outer retinal degeneration. The two primary diseases are: **(1) Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP)** — a hereditary disease causing progressive rod and cone degeneration, and **(2) Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)** — a leading cause of blindness in people over 50, with degeneration of the macula. In both, the photoreceptors die but the bipolar and ganglion cells survive, making electrical stimulation feasible. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is a phosphene? Why is it important in retinal prosthesis design?*

**Model Answer:**
A phosphene is the perception of light (a small luminous spot or pattern) experienced when the visual pathway is stimulated by means other than light (e.g., electrical, mechanical, magnetic). In retinal prosthesis design, phosphene is the fundamental "perceptual pixel" — every electrode, when activated, is intended to elicit one phosphene whose brightness, position, and timing are controlled by the stimulation parameters. The spatial arrangement of phosphenes forms the "pixelated image" perceived by the patient. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Classify retinal implants based on electrode placement. Compare the epiretinal and subretinal approaches with respect to surgical complexity, cells stimulated, and image processing required.**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (2 marks):**
Based on electrode placement, retinal implants are classified into four types:
1. **Epiretinal** — array placed on the inner retinal surface (ILM), in contact with the ganglion cell layer.
2. **Subretinal** — array placed in the space between the retinal pigment epithelium and the outer retina (where photoreceptors once lived).
3. **Suprachoroidal** — array placed between the sclera and the choroid.
4. **Intrascleral** — array embedded within the sclera.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Epiretinal | Subretinal |
|---|---|---|
| **Cells directly stimulated** | Ganglion cells | Bipolar cells (and indirectly ganglion cells) |
| **Surgical procedure** | Pars plana vitrectomy + retinal tack fixation | Subretinal implantation via retinotomy |
| **Surgical complexity** | High (risk of retinal detachment) | High (risk of retinal damage during insertion) |
| **Image processing** | Heavy (must replicate retinal processing) | Minimal (retinal processing preserved) |
| **Power consumption** | Higher | Lower |
| **Visual field mapping** | Direct, 1:1 with electrodes | Natural — uses retinal circuits |
| **Example** | Argus II (60 electrodes) | Alpha IMS / Alpha AMS (1500/1600 electrodes) |
| **Disadvantage** | Bypasses retinal processing | Long-term microglia accumulation |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Subretinal approaches are theoretically superior because they leverage the retina's own neural processing, but epiretinal devices are surgically more established. Both have yielded clinically useful but low-resolution vision (~20/1200).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the role of the inductive RF link in a retinal implant. Why is a charge-balanced biphasic pulse essential, and what is the Shannon safety constraint?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the RF Inductive Link (3 marks):**
The inductive RF link serves two purposes in an implanted retinal prosthesis:
1. **Wireless power transfer** — The external transmitter coil (worn on spectacles) and the implanted receiver coil form an air-core transformer. AC current in the external coil induces an AC voltage in the implanted coil by Faraday's law, typically at 13.56 MHz. This powers the implanted stimulator ASIC without percutaneous wires, eliminating infection risk.
2. **Bidirectional data telemetry** — The same link carries stimulation command data (frame, electrode number, pulse amplitude) encoded by Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) or Load Shift Keying (LSK), and returns impedance and neural recording data back to the external processor.

The link efficiency $\eta$ depends on coil coupling coefficient $k$ and quality factor $Q$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2} \times 100\%$$

**Charge-Balanced Biphasic Pulses (2 marks):**
A biphasic pulse consists of a cathodic (negative) phase followed by an anodic (positive) phase of equal charge $Q = I \cdot t$. This is **essential** because:
- A pure DC current would cause electrolysis of tissue water, producing toxic H₂, O₂, and Cl₂.
- It would shift local pH, killing neurons.
- It would drive irreversible (Faradaic) reactions at the metal-electrolyte interface, corroding the electrode.
The biphasic waveform ensures **net zero charge transfer**, avoiding all these effects while still depolarizing neurons during the cathodic phase.

**Shannon Safety Constraint (2 marks):**
The Shannon packaging limit defines the safe stimulation envelope:

$$\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(k) < 1.85$$

where $D$ is charge density (µC/cm²) and $k$ is current density (A/cm²). If exceeded, the stimulation can damage tissue via mechanisms such as Joule heating, electrochemical corrosion, or pH shift. Designers select pulse parameters (current, duration, electrode size, material) such that the index is always < 1.85.

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the major functional subsystems of an epiretinal visual prosthesis. Explain how the camera image is converted into neural stimulation patterns.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Camera → Image Processor → RF Encoder → [Wireless Inductive Link] →
→ RF Decoder → Stimulator ASIC → Electrode Array → Retina → Optic Nerve → Brain
```

**Subsystem Description (4 marks):**

1. **Image Acquisition Subsystem:** A miniature CMOS camera (e.g., VGA resolution) mounted on the patient's spectacles captures the external scene at 30 frames/second, mimicking the field of view of the natural eye.

2. **Image Processing Subsystem:** A wearable ASIC processor (e.g., in the Argus II, this is a custom DSP on the patient's belt) performs:
   - **Down-sampling** — reduces image to match the electrode array (e.g., 6×10 for Argus II).
   - **Thresholding** — converts grayscale to binary on/off stimulation.
   - **Contrast enhancement** — boosts edges for better recognition.
   - **Frame-rate mapping** — sets stimulation pulse frequency (typically 20–60 Hz).

3. **Telemetry Subsystem:** The processed image data is serialized and transmitted via an RF inductive link (typically 13.56 MHz or 49 MHz) across the intact sclera. Power and data are coupled through matched LC coils.

4. **Stimulator + Electrode Array:** An implanted ASIC decodes the data and generates a **charge-balanced biphasic current pulse** for each "bright" pixel. The Argus II uses a 60-electrode platinum array (6×10 grid) epiretinally attached with a retinal tack. Each electrode produces one phosphene, and the spatial pattern of phosphenes forms the perceived image.

**Stimulus Generation (in-text):** The mapping is direct — pixel (i,j) brightness controls pulse amplitude at electrode (i,j). Brighter pixel → higher current → brighter phosphene. The brain learns to interpret this "pixelated display" as vision.

**Total: 7 marks distributed as 3 (diagram) + 4 (explanation)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A subretinal implant uses a microphotodiode array that converts light directly into stimulation current (no external camera). Discuss the advantages and limitations of this photodiode-driven approach compared to the camera-driven epiretinal approach.**

**Model Answer:**

**Photodiode-Driven Subretinal Concept (2 marks):**
A subretinal implant based on a microphotodiode array (e.g., the earlier Optobionics ASR, and as a component of Alpha IMS) places thousands of photodiodes in the subretinal space. Each photodiode, when illuminated by incoming focused light, generates a small photocurrent proportional to local light intensity. This current is delivered through an IrOx microelectrode directly to the bipolar cells. **No external camera, no image processor, no RF telemetry** is required.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Natural image acquisition** — the eye's own optics (cornea + lens) are used, giving a normal field of view and natural eye movements (saccades). This is a major ergonomic advantage.
2. **Minimal external hardware** — patient appears "normal," wears no spectacles with camera.
3. **Inherent pixel-to-phosphene mapping** — each photodiode corresponds to a retinal location, providing natural retinotopic mapping.
4. **Lower power consumption** — no RF link or external processor needed.
5. **Higher theoretical resolution** — Alpha IMS achieved 1500 electrodes, much more than Argus II's 60.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Insufficient ambient light** — normal room light is orders of magnitude too dim to drive photodiodes to neural threshold. Most subretinal photodiode-only devices failed clinically for this reason (Optobionics ASR Phase II trial).
2. **Heat dissipation** — without external power, energy must come from incident light, limiting stimulation strength.
3. **No image processing** — cannot perform edge enhancement, contrast normalization, or zoom.
4. **Surgical risk** — subretinal insertion can damage the remaining bipolar cells.
5. **Long-term biostability** — encapsulation by Müller cells and microglia reduces effective output over years.

**Conclusion:** The subretinal approach is biologically elegant but technologically harder. The Alpha IMS/AMS solved the power problem by adding an *external* power coil while retaining photodiodes as the light-to-current stage — a hybrid.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "epiretinal" and "subretinal" positions** in your diagram — KTU examiners *deliberately* deduct 1–2 marks for mislabelled cross-sections. Memorize: *Epi = upon retina (vitreous side); Sub = below retina (in photoreceptor space).*
> 2. **Always mention "charge-balanced biphasic"** when describing stimulation pulses. Writing "current pulse" alone will lose you 1 mark.
> 3. **Cite the Shannon limit** with the numerical value **1.85**, not just the formula name. KTU 2024 expects a numerical benchmark.
> 4. **In (a) of any 14-mark question, define the disease (RP/AMD) before defining the implant.** Examiners explicitly reward disease-first presentation.
> 5. **In numerical questions, always show units and conversions** (µA → A, µm → m → cm²). A 1-mark "unit error" deduction is common in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 6. **Never claim retinal implants restore normal vision.** Always use the phrase "partial visual perception / phosphene-based artificial vision / coarse pixelated image." Overpromising in KTU answers is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Retinal implant** = electronic prosthesis that electrically stimulates surviving inner retinal neurons to bypass dead photoreceptors in **RP** and **AMD**.
- [ ] **Phosphene** = perceptual "pixel" of artificial vision; a sensation of light without light.
- [ ] **Three (four) types:** **Epi**retinal, **Sub**retinal, **Supra**choroidal, **Intra**scleral.
- [ ] **Epiretinal** stimulates **ganglion cells** (Argus II — 60 electrodes).
- [ ] **Subretinal** stimulates **bipolar cells** (Alpha IMS/AMS — up to 1600 electrodes).
- [ ] **Suprachoroidal** is **least invasive** but has higher thresholds (BVA).
- [ ] **Four subsystems:** Camera → Image Processor → RF Inductive Link → Stimulator + Electrode Array.
- [ ] **RF link frequency:** typically **13.56 MHz** (ISM band), uses inductive coupling.
- [ ] **Charge-balanced biphasic pulse** is mandatory — prevents electrolysis, pH shift, electrode corrosion.
- [ ] **Shannon safety limit:** $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(k) < 1.85$.
- [ ] **Charge:** $Q = I \cdot t$; **Charge density:** $D = Q/A$; **Current density:** $k = I/A$.
- [ ] **Electrode material:** Pt, IrOx, TiN, or PEDOT:PSS coatings (to increase real surface area).
- [ ] **Surgical fix:** Argus II uses a **retinal tack**; subretinal uses **retinotomy**.
- [ ] **Achieved visual acuity:** ~20/1200 (Argus II) — useful for navigation, large-object detection, but **not face or text recognition**.
- [ ] **Argus II was discontinued in 2020** (commercial reasons, not technical) — important KTU fact.
- [ ] **Alpha AMS** is the highest-resolution clinically tested device (CE marked, Europe).
- [ ] **Future directions:** >1000 electrode systems, subretinal photovoltaic approaches (Stanford/Paris Pixium Vision), and suprachoroidal Australian devices in trials.
- [ ] **Engineering sub-fields touched:** biomaterials, VLSI, image processing, wireless power, neural signal processing, hermetic packaging (titanium can + medical-grade silicone).
- [ ] **Always state in exams:** "The device produces phosphene-based *partial* visual perception, not normal vision."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optic-Nerve Stimulation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optic-Nerve Stimulation

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optic-Nerve Stimulation (ONS)** is a neuromodulation technique classified under *cortical/sub-cortical visual prosthetics* in which an electrode array is surgically wrapped around or implanted into the **optic nerve (CN II)** to deliver controlled, biphasic charge-balanced electrical pulses. The artificially evoked action potentials propagate along the retinofugal pathway, through the **Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN)**, and reach the primary visual cortex (**V1**), where they are interpreted as localized points of light called **phosphenes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Optic-nerve stimulation belongs to the family of *Electrical Visual Prosthesis (EVP)* and is a **sensory-substitution** rehabilitation strategy for patients suffering from severe outer-retinal degeneration (e.g., Retinitis Pigmentosa, Age-related Macular Degeneration) where the optic nerve and visual cortex remain functionally intact.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bypass Telephone Cable" Concept

Imagine the visual pathway as a long **underground telephone cable** carrying millions of electrical signals from a camera (the eye's retina) to a TV screen (the visual cortex). In diseases like Retinitis Pigmentosa, the **camera** (rod and cone photoreceptors) is broken, but the **cable** (optic nerve) and the **TV screen** (V1 cortex) are perfectly fine.

Optic-nerve stimulation is like **tapping directly into the cable** downstream of the broken camera. We inject carefully crafted electrical "taps" into the cable using electrodes, and the TV screen still lights up with a pixel (a phosphene), even though no real light ever hit the camera.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The **Lausanne University (EPFL) C-Sight project** and the early **Veraart group's extraneural cuff electrode** are landmark human implants that used ONS to produce patterned phosphene vision in blind volunteers.

## 1.3 Key Anatomical & Physical Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Optic nerve fiber count | **~1.2 × 10⁶ axons** | Total bandwidth available for stimulation |
| Optic nerve diameter | **~2 mm at orbit**, **~4 mm at chiasm** | Dictates electrode geometry |
| Stimulation threshold | **~20–200 µA** | Depends on electrode–tissue impedance |
| Pulse width typical | **100–500 µs per phase** | Biphasic, charge-balanced |
| Phosphene size | **Varies, ~0.5°–3° visual angle** | Comparable to single pixel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to remember:** The **Shannon safety limit** for chronic neural stimulation requires $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) < k$, where typical $k \approx 1.85$ to **2.0** for safe chronic macro-stimulation of the optic nerve.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theory

The ONS system works through a precise sequence known as the **Stimulate–Propagate–Perceive** loop:

1. **Image Acquisition:** A miniature camera (mounted on spectacle frames) captures the external visual scene.
2. **Signal Processing:** An external Video Processing Unit (VPU) encodes brightness, contrast, and edge information into stimulation parameters.
3. **Wireless Telemetry:** Parameters are transmitted via RF/inductive link through the skin to the implanted stimulator.
4. **Current Steering via Electrode Array:** A multi-contact **cuff or penetrating electrode** wrapped around the optic nerve delivers charge to specific fascicles.
5. **Axon Recruitment:** Each electrode contact activates a small bundle of retinal ganglion cell (RGC) axons, producing a discrete phosphene.
6. **Cortical Interpretation:** The brain perceives the spatial pattern of phosphenes as a low-resolution image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the optic nerve and not just the retina?** In advanced RP, the inner retinal layers (RGCs) may survive, but the photoreceptors are dead. The optic nerve approach **bypasses the entire retina**, recruiting axons directly — making it viable even when bipolar/horizontal cells have also degenerated.

## 2.2 Electrode Architectures Used in ONS

| Electrode Type | Placement | Selectivity | Invasiveness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Extraneural Cuff Electrode** | Wrapped around outer epineurium | Low–Medium (whole nerve activated) | Moderate |
| **Intraneural (Penetrating) Array** | Microelectrodes inserted into nerve fascicles | High (fascicle-specific) | High |
| **Optic-Disc/Lamina Cribrosa Electrode** | Inserted at optic nerve head | Medium | Moderate |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Charge per phase** | $Q = I \cdot t$ | $I$ = current, $t$ = pulse width | Coulombs (C) | Determine injected charge per pulse |
| **Charge density** | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $A$ = electrode area | $\text{C/cm}^2$ | Safety of stimulation |
| **Current density** | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | — | $\text{A/cm}^2$ | Electrode interface limit |
| **Shannon's Safety Limit** | $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) < k$ | $D$ in $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$, $Q$ in $\mu\text{C}$ | — | Avoid tissue damage |
| **Strength–Duration (Weiss)** | $I = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}$ | $I_{rh}$ = rheobase, $\tau$ = membrane time constant | A | Threshold current vs. pulse width |
| **Laplacian charge approximation (Lapicque)** | $I_{th} = I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$ | $\tau_{ch}$ = chronaxie | A | Compute rheobase & chronaxie |
| **Phosphene spatial resolution** | $\theta \approx \dfrac{d_{cortex}}{f}$ | $d_{cortex}$ = cortical distance, $f$ = focal length | degrees | Estimate visual acuity |
| **Coulomb efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{anodic}}{Q_{cathodic}}$ | — | dimensionless | Charge-balance verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KR-U Note:** In ONS biphasic pulses, $\eta$ **must equal 1.0** within 10% tolerance, otherwise **DC residual charge** causes electrochemical corrosion of platinum-iridium electrodes and generates toxic species at the tissue interface.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Direct clinical application** for end-stage RP where retinal implants (Argus II, Alpha-AMS) have failed.
- Serves as a **research test-bed** for understanding retinotopic mapping beyond the retina.
- Validates the **"neural code sufficiency"** hypothesis: does patterned spike trains alone (without retinal processing) generate useful vision?

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Strength–Duration Relationship for Optic-Nerve Stimulation

The optic-nerve axonal membrane behaves as a **leaky capacitor** described by the cable equation. To depolarize a node of Ranvier to threshold, charge must be integrated over the membrane time constant $\tau_m$.

### Step 1 — Membrane as RC Circuit

The nerve membrane is modelled as:

$$V_m(t) = V_{rest} + (V_{th} - V_{rest})\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

Where $V_{rest} \approx -70$ mV and $V_{th} \approx -55$ mV for typical RGC axons.

### Step 2 — Threshold Condition

Setting $V_m(t) = V_{th}$ and solving for injected current $I$ via membrane resistance $R_m$:

$$I_{th} = \dfrac{V_{th} - V_{rest}}{R_m\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Rheobase

The **rheobase current** $I_{rh}$ is defined as the minimum DC current for infinite pulse duration ($t \to \infty$):

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} I_{th} = \dfrac{V_{th} - V_{rest}}{R_m} \equiv I_{rh}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute to Obtain Weiss Equation

$$I_{th}(t) = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Lapicque Form

Let chronaxie $\tau_{ch} = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m$. Then the **chronaxie** is defined as the pulse width at which threshold current is exactly **twice the rheobase**.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Verification: when } t = \tau_{ch} &\Rightarrow I_{th} = I_{rh}(1 + 1) = 2 I_{rh} \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Example for Optic-Nerve Stimulation

Given for a typical extraneural cuff on the human optic nerve:
- $I_{rh} = 50\ \mu\text{A}$
- $\tau_{ch} = 250\ \mu\text{s}$

Compute $I_{th}$ for $t = 100\ \mu\text{s}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= 50\left(1 + \dfrac{250}{100}\right)\ \mu\text{A} \\[4pt]
&= 50 \times (1 + 2.5) \\[4pt]
&= 50 \times 3.5 \\[4pt]
&= 175\ \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This computed $I_{th} = 175\ \mu\text{A}$ is consistent with the **20–200 µA** range reported in the Veraart et al. clinical trials.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon's Safety Boundary

Chronic stimulation of neural tissue must stay below the electrochemical damage threshold of **platinum-iridium electrodes**.

### Step 1 — Combine Density and Charge

Shannon (1992) proposed that the product of charge per phase and charge density governs electrolytic damage:

$$k = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q)$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Maximum Allowed Charge

Given a fixed electrode area $A$ and a chosen safety index $k$:

$$Q_{max} = 10^{k - \log_{10}(D)} = \dfrac{10^{k}}{D}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example

Suppose $A = 0.05\ \text{cm}^2$, $D_{max} = 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$, and $k = 1.85$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{max} &= \dfrac{10^{1.85}}{30}\ \mu\text{C} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{70.79}{30}\ \mu\text{C} \\[4pt]
&\approx 2.36\ \mu\text{C per phase}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of $Q \approx 2.36\ \mu\text{C}$ represents the **upper safe limit** per phase for chronic extraneural ONS using a 0.05 cm² contact.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stimulation Outcome vs. Parameters

| Parameter | Low Value Effect | High Value Effect | Clinical Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pulse width (t) | Higher current needed | Lower current needed | Sweet spot ~ 200–400 µs |
| Current amplitude (I) | No phosphene | Brighter / larger phosphene | Stay under Shannon limit |
| Frequency (f) | Faint flicker | Stable, fused percept | 20–200 Hz typical |
| Electrode contact size | High impedance, focal | Low impedance, broad | Balance selectivity vs. safety |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ONS Implant System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Camera on Spectacle Frame] --> B[Video Processing Unit VPU]
    B --> C[RF Encoder and Transmitter Coil]
    C --> D[Subcutaneous Receiver and Decoder]
    D --> E[Implantable Pulse Generator IPG]
    E --> F[Multicontact Cuff Electrode]
    F --> G[Optic Nerve CN II]
    G --> H[Lateral Geniculate Nucleus LGN]
    H --> I[Primary Visual Cortex V1]
    I --> J[Phosphene Perception in Patient]
    B --> B1[Contrast and Edge Enhancement Filter]
    B1 --> B
    E --> E1[Charge Balancing Circuit]
    E1 --> E
    F --> F1[Return Electrode]
    F1 --> E
```

## 4.2 System Block Diagram — Stimulation Waveform & Safety Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller Stim Engine] --> B[Constant Current Source]
    B --> C[Anodic Phase Pulse]
    C --> D[Interphase Gap]
    D --> E[Cathodic Phase Pulse]
    E --> F[Electrode Tissue Interface]
    F --> G[Optic Nerve Axon]
    A --> H[Shannon Safety Monitor]
    H --> I[Cutoff Relay]
    I --> B
    A --> J[Charge Balance Integrator]
    J --> K[Error Feedback Loop]
    K --> B
```

## 4.3 Anatomical Pathway Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Damaged Photoreceptors in Retina] --> B[Non Functional Outer Retina]
    B --> C[Optic Nerve Head Accessible]
    C --> D[Optic Nerve CN II Stimulated]
    D --> E[Optic Chiasm]
    E --> F[Lateral Geniculate Nucleus LGN]
    F --> G[Optic Radiation]
    G --> H[Primary Visual Cortex V1]
    H --> I[Conscious Phosphene Vision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** A clean and well-labeled block diagram that traces the signal from camera to cortex is often awarded **2 to 3 marks** by itself in 14-mark questions. Always include the *image processing* and *power telemetry* blocks in your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define optic-nerve stimulation. Mention the two principal electrode configurations used.**

**Model Answer:**
Optic-nerve stimulation is a visual rehabilitation technique in which controlled electrical pulses are delivered to the optic nerve (CN II) via implanted electrodes, generating phosphenes in the visual cortex to restore a sense of vision in blind patients with damaged retinas. The two principal electrode configurations are:

1. **Extraneural cuff electrode** — wrapped around the outer epineurium, non-penetrating, lower selectivity.
2. **Intraneural (penetrating) electrode array** — microelectrodes inserted into individual fascicles of the optic nerve, providing higher spatial selectivity at the cost of higher invasiveness.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 electrode types with brief explanation)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is a phosphene? How is it related to optic-nerve stimulation?**

**Model Answer:**
A **phosphene** is the perception of light — a small luminous point or pattern — produced by direct electrical or mechanical stimulation of the visual pathway in the absence of real light. In optic-nerve stimulation, charge-balanced biphasic current pulses applied through the cuff or intraneural electrodes depolarize retinal ganglion cell axons, and the resulting action potentials travel to the primary visual cortex (V1), where they are consciously perceived as a phosphene. The size, brightness, and location of the phosphene depend on the stimulation amplitude, pulse width, and the specific fascicle that is activated.

*(3 marks: definition + cortical origin + parameters affecting phosphene)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete working of an **optic-nerve visual prosthesis system**. Discuss the role of each block from image acquisition to phosphene perception. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **strength–duration relationship** for an optic-nerve axon. A cuff electrode has a rheobase of **60 µA** and a chronaxie of **200 µs**. Calculate the threshold current for a pulse width of **400 µs**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

Refer to **Section 4.1** for the diagram. The explanation must cover:

1. **External camera** (1 mark) — captures the visual scene mounted on spectacle frames.
2. **Video Processing Unit (VPU)** (2 marks) — performs contrast enhancement, edge detection, and down-sampling to match the limited resolution of the phosphene map; outputs stimulation parameters.
3. **Wireless telemetry** (1 mark) — RF/inductive link transmits power and data across the skin to the implant.
4. **Implantable Pulse Generator (IPG)** (1 mark) — generates biphasic, charge-balanced current pulses with controlled amplitude and pulse width.
5. **Cuff / intraneural electrode** (1 mark) — delivers current to the optic nerve, recruiting specific fascicles and producing localized phosphenes.
6. **Cortical pathway** (1 mark) — signals reach LGN → V1, generating conscious visual perception.

---

**(b) Strength–Duration Derivation and Calculation (7 marks)**

From Section 3.1, the Weiss-Lapicque equation is:

$$I_{th} = I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$$

Substitute the given values:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= 60\ \mu\text{A} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{200\ \mu\text{s}}{400\ \mu\text{s}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 60 \times \left(1 + 0.5\right)\ \mu\text{A} \\[4pt]
&= 60 \times 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= 90\ \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $I_{th} = 90\ \mu\text{A}$ for $t = 400\ \mu\text{s}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently forget to convert **µs to consistent units** or write chronaxie in the wrong place in the numerator. **KTU 2024 valuation** strictly deducts **1 mark** for dimensional inconsistency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **anatomy of the visual pathway** and justify why optic-nerve stimulation is preferred over retinal implants in advanced retinal degeneration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **maximum safe charge per phase** for an extraneural cuff electrode of area **0.08 cm²** using the Shannon equation. Assume $k = 1.85$ and a maximum charge density of **30 µC/cm²**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Anatomy and Justification (7 marks)**

1. **Anatomy of the visual pathway** (3 marks):
   Retina (photoreceptors → bipolar → ganglion cells) → **Optic nerve (CN II)** → **Optic chiasm** (nasal fibers cross) → **Optic tract** → **LGN of thalamus** → **Optic radiation** → **V1 (primary visual cortex)**.

2. **Why ONS over retinal implants in advanced RP** (4 marks):
   - In end-stage Retinitis Pigmentosa, **inner retinal neurons (RGCs) may also degenerate**, making retinal implants (Argus II, Alpha-AMS) ineffective.
   - Optic-nerve stimulation **bypasses the entire retina**, recruiting surviving axons directly.
   - Provides a **larger recruitment area** — a single cuff can access ~1.2 million axons.
   - Useful for patients with **optic-nerve-head trauma or severe optic-atrophy** where retinal targeting is impossible.

---

**(b) Shannon Equation Calculation (7 marks)**

From Section 3.2:

$$Q_{max} = \dfrac{10^{k}}{D_{max}}$$

Substitute $k = 1.85$, $D_{max} = 30\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{max} &= \dfrac{10^{1.85}}{30}\ \mu\text{C} \\[4pt]
10^{1.85} &\approx 70.79 \\[4pt]
Q_{max} &= \dfrac{70.79}{30}\ \mu\text{C} \\[4pt]
&\approx 2.36\ \mu\text{C per phase}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (2 marks):** This is a safe upper bound for **chronic extraneural ONS**. For acute experimental stimulation, slightly higher values are tolerated, but chronic operation must remain below this to prevent electrochemical damage and glial scarring.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to write the comment** part in such questions. KTU 2024 board examiners allocate a **dedicated 2-mark band** for "engineering interpretation / clinical comment" — never skip it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before your exam — these are the 12 highest-yield facts.**

- ✅ **Optic-nerve stimulation** is a **sensory-substitution visual prosthesis** that bypasses damaged retinas.
- ✅ The **optic nerve contains ~1.2 million axons** — the primary recruitment target.
- ✅ **Two electrode types:** extraneural **cuff** and **intraneural penetrating** arrays.
- ✅ **Phosphenes** are the perceptual units; their location depends on **fascicle activated**.
- ✅ **Biphasic, charge-balanced pulses** are mandatory to prevent electrochemical tissue damage.
- ✅ **Charge per phase** $Q = I \cdot t$; **charge density** $D = Q/A$.
- ✅ **Shannon limit:** $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) < k$, with $k \approx 1.85$ safe for chronic macro-stimulation.
- ✅ **Rheobase** = minimum DC current; **chronaxie** = pulse width at $2 \times$ rheobase.
- ✅ **Weiss equation:** $I_{th} = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$; **Lapicque form:** $I_{th} = I_{rh}\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_{ch}}{t}\right)$.
- ✅ Typical **stimulation parameters:** 20–200 µA, 100–500 µs pulse width, 20–200 Hz frequency.
- ✅ **Visual pathway:** retina → optic nerve → chiasm → LGN → V1 cortex.
- ✅ Clinical landmark: **Veraart et al. (1998, 2002)** chronic extraneural ONS implant in a blind volunteer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute study tip:** In KTU 2024 scheme ESE, you are expected to **draw the block diagram AND derive the strength–duration equation** in any 14-mark ONS question. Practice combining these two — they appear in almost every variant.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auditory and Tactile Information Display
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Auditory and Tactile Information Display # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Auditory Information Display ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) An **Auditory Information Display** is a Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) subsystem that translates digital or analog data into structured acoustic signals (speech, tones, music, or environmental sounds) so that information can be perceived, recognized, and acted upon by the human auditory system. In rehabilitation engineering, it acts as the **primary non-visual channel** for blind and low-vision users. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** *"An auditory display renders information using sound parameters — pitch, loudness, timbre, duration, and spatial location — such that a person with visual impairment can extract the underlying data without sight."* ### Three Principal Classes | Class | Description | Rehab Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Speech Displays (Earcons / Spoken)** | Synthesized or recorded human voice output. | Screen readers (JAWS, NVDA), TTY/TDD, GPS navigators. | | **Non-Speech Audio Displays (Earcons & Auditory Icons)** | Abstract musical tones or real-world sound metaphors mapped to data. | Sonification of heart rhythm (ICU monitor), process-control alarms. | | **3-D / Spatial Audio Displays** | HRTF-filtered sound giving the illusion of direction in 3-D space. | Virtual cane substitute, head-tracking GPS for the blind. | ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are driving a car with your eyes closed. You cannot see the speedometer, but you can *hear* the engine pitch rise as speed increases, *hear* a beep when fuel is low, and *hear* the click of the indicator. The car is using **sound as an information channel** — that is exactly what an auditory display does for a visually impaired computer user. ### Key Acoustic Ranges (Memorize for KTU) - **Human hearing band:** **20 Hz – 20 kHz** - **Speech intelligibility band:** **300 Hz – 3.4 kHz** (telephony); **80 Hz – 8 kHz** (full fidelity) - **Most sensitive region:** **2 kHz – 5 kHz** (where the ear canal resonates) - **Threshold of pain:** **120 dB SPL**; Threshold of hearing: **0 dB SPL** (ref = **20 µPa**) > [!NOTE] > **Why Hz-matters in rehab:** Designers pick audio frequencies in the 300 Hz–3 kHz band because this range maximizes intelligibility and minimizes listener fatigue in long-duration screen-reader sessions. --- ## 1.2 Tactile Information Display ### Formal Definition A **Tactile Information Display** is a sensory-substitution device that stimulates the cutaneous (skin) receptors — or the kinesthetic receptors in joints and muscles — to convey graphical, textual, or symbolic information through touch. It is the **backbone technology** for deaf-blind communication and a secondary channel for deaf and blind users. ### Four Modalities of Tactile Stimulation | Modality | Mechanism | Example | |---|---|---| | **Mechanical / Vibrotactile** | Pins vibrating against skin | Refreshable Braille display | | **Electrotactile** | Low-current pulses through skin | Optacon, Vision substitution vests | | **Thermal** | Heating/cooling elements | Research prototypes for texture rendering | | **Shape / Pressure / Pin-array** | Static raised dots or actuated pins | Braille cell (8-dot) | ### Conceptual Analogy Run your finger over a printed page. You cannot see the words, but the *raised dots* under your fingertip carry the message. Multiply this by thousands of actuated pins and a refresh controller, and you have a **refreshable Braille display** — a tactile information display in its purest engineering form. ### Key Tactile Sensitivity Ranges (For KTU Viva & Numericals) - **Best vibrotactile sensitivity:** **200 – 300 Hz** (Pacinian corpuscles) - **Best spatial resolution:** **Fingertip ≈ 2–3 mm** two-point threshold - **Back / Forearm resolution:** **30 – 70 mm** two-point threshold - **Static force threshold (fingertip):** **≈ 0.6 mN** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Quick Memory Hook:** *"High frequency (250 Hz) ⇒ feel vibration. Low frequency (< 5 Hz) ⇒ feel pressure. Fingertips are the HD display of the skin."* --- ## 1.3 Why These Two Channels? — The Sensory Substitution Triangle | Sense Lost | Primary Substitute | Secondary Substitute | |---|---|---| | Vision | **Auditory** | **Tactile** | | Hearing | Visual (CART, captions) | **Tactile** (e.g., Tactile Pager) | | Both | **Tactile** (Braille) | Auditory (when available) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sensory Substitution Rule:** *No single channel can replace vision completely; auditory + tactile together cover the information bandwidth required for tasks like reading, navigation, and object recognition.* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Human Auditory System — From Pinna to Cortex The auditory pathway is a frequency-analyzing, time-resolving transducer. For KTU, you must know: 1. **Outer ear (Pinna + Ear canal):** Provides ~10–15 dB gain at 2.5–4 kHz through resonance. 2. **Middle ear (Ossicles):** Impedance matching between air (low Z) and cochlear fluid (high Z). Transformer ratio ≈ **22:1** in pressure gain. 3. **Cochlea:** A hydrodynamic frequency analyzer via the **Basilar Membrane (BM)**. - Base of BM: **high frequency** resonance (~20 kHz). - Apex of BM: **low frequency** resonance (~20 Hz). 4. **Hair cells:** Inner (rows 1) ≈ **3500**, Outer (rows 3) ≈ **12,000** — transduction units. 5. **Auditory nerve & Cortex:** Tonotopic mapping preserved up to A1. ### Place Theory vs. Temporal Theory - **Place Theory (Békésy):** Different frequencies excite different positions along the BM. - **Temporal Theory:** Below ~4 kHz, the firing of auditory neurons phase-locks to stimulus period. - For KTU: *"Above 4 kHz → Place theory dominates. Below 4 kHz → Temporal (volley) theory dominates."* --- ## 2.2 Human Tactile System — Receptor Physiology | Receptor Type | Location | Best Frequency | What It Encodes | |---|---|---|---| | **Meissner Corpuscles** | Hairless skin (fingertip) | 30 – 50 Hz | Light touch, low-freq vibration, flutter | | **Merkel Discs** | Hairless skin | 0.5 – 100 Hz (static) | Pressure, edges, texture | | **Ruffini Endings** | Deep dermis | 100 – 500 Hz | Skin stretch, direction | | **Pacinian Corpuscles** | Subcutaneous | 200 – 1000 Hz (peak ~250 Hz) | High-freq vibration | | **Free Nerve Endings** | All skin | N/A | Pain, temperature | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** To design a tactual display, *choose the actuator frequency to match the receptor* — e.g., voice-coil actuators for Pacinian (vibrotactile) at 200–300 Hz; pin arrays for Merkel/Meissner (static pressure) at 0.5–50 Hz. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 7 equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 numerical / Part B (14-mark) question.** ### Table A — Acoustic & Auditory Display Equations | # | Equation | LaTeX | Variables & Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | P = RMS pressure (Pa), $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Convert pressure to dB | | 2 | **Sound Intensity Level (SIL)** | $\text{SIL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | I = intensity (W/m²), $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | Intensity in dB | | 3 | **Inverse-Square Law** | $I_{2} = I_{1}\!\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}$ | r = distance from source (m) | Distance vs. loudness | | 4 | **Doppler Frequency Shift** | $f' = f\!\left(\dfrac{v \pm v_{\text{obs}}}{v \mp v_{\text{src}}}\right)$ | v = speed of sound $\approx 343$ m/s | Moving-source sonification | | 5 | **Weber's Law (Pitch JND)** | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f} = k$ | f = frequency, k $\approx 0.003$ at 1 kHz | Earcon spacing | | 6 | **Stevens' Power Law (Loudness)** | $S = k \cdot I^{n}$ | I = intensity, n = 0.67 for loudness | Subjective scaling | | 7 | **Phon → Sone Conversion** | $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$ | P = phon level, S = sones | Loudness scaling | ### Table B — Tactile Display Equations | # | Equation | LaTeX | Variables & Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Stevens' Power Law (Vibrotactile)** | $S = k \cdot I^{n}$ | n $\approx 0.6$ for 250 Hz vibration on fingertip | Subjective vibration intensity | | 2 | **Two-Point Threshold** | $\text{TPT} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Spatial Acuity}}$ | mm | Spatial resolution | | 3 | **Vibration Detection Threshold (Pacinian)** | $a_{\min} \approx 0.1\ \mu\text{m}$ at 250 Hz | Peak displacement | Actuator design | | 4 | **JND in Vibration Amplitude** | $\dfrac{\Delta a}{a} \approx 0.2$ | Weber fraction for vibrotactile | Number of distinct levels | | 5 | **Information Transfer (Channel Capacity)** | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{2}\!\left(m_{i}+1\right) \text{ bits}$ | n = points, m = levels | Braille channel capacity | | 6 | **Braille Cell Information Rate** | $I = 6 \log_{2} 2 + 1$ for 8-dot | bits/character | Refreshable Braille bandwidth | | 7 | **Weber Fraction for Force** | $\dfrac{\Delta F}{F} \approx 0.07$ | F = applied force | Pin-force design | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-Symbol Rule Reminder:** In all formula sheets, the absolute-value bar has been rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table structure intact. Do not use plain `|` inside table cells. --- ## 2.4 Earcon Theory — Blattner's Melodic Grammar Dr. Meera Blattner (1989) classified earcons into two families that appear in KTU textbooks: 1. **Musical Earcons** — short melodies with pitch, rhythm, timbre, register. 2. **Compound / Hierarchical Earcons** — built from **motives** (atomic), then grouped into **families** (alarm family, navigation family), then into **hierarchies** (sub-alarms). ### Brewster's Earcon Design Rules (KTU Repeated Question) - **Consistency:** Same timbre for same family. - **Discriminability:** Different rhythms & pitches across families. - **Mapping:** Timbre ↔ category; Pitch ↔ magnitude; Rhythm ↔ urgency. - **Length:** 0.5 – 2.5 seconds (avoid listener fatigue). --- ## 2.5 Auditory Icon Theory — Gaver's Everyday Sounds William Gaver (1989) proposed that *real-world recorded sounds* (running water, knocking, paper crumple) carry **semantic mapping** to data — listeners infer meaning by analogy. | Auditory Icon | Mapped Data | |---|---| | Coin dropping | File download / money transaction | | Liquid pouring | Battery filling / data flow | | Knock | Incoming message | | Crack of glass | Error / warning | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Distinction Question:** > - **Earcon** ⇒ Abstract, synthetic, musical ⇒ requires *learning*. > - **Auditory Icon** ⇒ Naturalistic, recorded, ecological ⇒ *intuitive*, but harder to discriminate at high information density. --- ## 2.6 Tactile Display Engineering Taxonomy ### Class A — Refreshable Braille Displays - **6-dot (Standard Braille)** ⇒ 6 actuated pins per cell. - **8-dot (Computer Braille / Braille 2000)** ⇒ 8 actuated pins (allows ASCII & math). - **Reading rate target:** **80 – 120 wpm** (sighted reading ≈ 250 wpm). - **Refresh rate:** **~10 Hz** (limited by piezo settling time). ### Class B — Electrocutaneous (Electrotactile) Displays - Stimulate sensory nerves directly via surface electrodes. - Parameters: **Frequency 1 – 3000 Hz**, **Pulse width 5 – 500 µs**, **Current 0.5 – 5 mA**. - Advantage: No moving parts; can be miniaturized into vests. ### Class C — Vibrotactile / Pin Arrays - **Voice-coil actuators** (Pacinian range 200–300 Hz). - **Piezoelectric bimorphs** (high bandwidth, low power). - **Shape-memory alloy (SMA)** wires (large displacement, slow response). ### Class D — Sensory Substitution Devices - **Tongue Display Unit (TDU):** 12×12 electrotactile array on the tongue; used by Bach-y-Rita's group for blind navigation. - **Tactile Vision Substitution System (TVSS):** Camera → skin. - **BrainPort:** Camera → 400-electrode tongue array. > [!NOTE] > **Why the tongue?** Because saliva gives low impedance (~200 Ω), the tongue offers high spatial acuity (~0.5 mm) — comparable to the fingertip — but is *always inside the mouth* (privacy + stability). --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Industry | |---|---| | Cockpit auditory displays (sonic altitude, traffic) | Aviation / Defence | | Patient monitor alarms (ICU) | Healthcare | | Stock-market sonification | Finance | | Refreshable Braille notetakers (BrailleNote) | Accessibility | | Haptic surgery simulators (force feedback) | Medical training | | Phone vibration alerts | Consumer electronics | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Number of Discriminable Frequency Levels in an Earcon > **Problem:** A screen-reader emits an alert earcon at 1000 Hz. By Weber's law, the Weber fraction for pitch discrimination is $k = 0.003$ at this frequency. How many discriminable pitch levels exist between 500 Hz and 4000 Hz? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — State Weber's Law for pitch:** $$\dfrac{\Delta f}{f} = k = 0.003$$ This means a *just-noticeable* change in frequency corresponds to a fractional change of 0.003 (i.e., 0.3 %). **Step 2 — Express the next-discriminable frequency $f_2$ from current $f_1$:** $$f_{2} - f_{1} = \Delta f = k \cdot f_{1}$$ $$f_{2} = f_{1}(1 + k)$$ **Step 3 — Recognize geometric progression:** Starting from $f_{1}$, the $n$-th discriminable frequency is: $$f_{n} = f_{1}(1 + k)^{n-1}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for n when $f_{n} = 4000$ Hz, $f_{1} = 500$ Hz:** $$4000 = 500(1.003)^{n-1}$$ Divide both sides by 500: $$8 = (1.003)^{n-1}$$ Take natural logarithm: $$\ln 8 = (n-1)\ln(1.003)$$ $$n - 1 = \dfrac{\ln 8}{\ln(1.003)}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:** $$\ln 8 \approx 2.0794$$ $$\ln(1.003) \approx 0.002995$$ $$n - 1 \approx \dfrac{2.0794}{0.002995} \approx 694.3$$ $$n \approx 695 \text{ levels}$$ **Step 6 — Information-theoretic capacity:** Using $I = \log_{2} N$: $$I = \log_{2}(695) \approx 9.44\ \text{bits}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** Between 500 Hz and 4 kHz the human ear can discriminate about **695 pitch levels**, providing ~**9.4 bits** of earcon bandwidth — more than enough to encode a 26-letter alphabet (log₂ 26 ≈ 4.7 bits). --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Channel Capacity of a Braille Cell > **Problem:** A standard 6-dot Braille cell uses 6 binary pins (up/down). An 8-dot cell uses 6 standard + 2 computer dots. Compute (a) the channel capacity per cell, (b) the bits-per-second at a refresh rate of 20 Hz, and (c) the equivalent reading speed in wpm. ### Part (a) — Capacity per cell **6-dot cell:** $$C_{6} = 6 \log_{2}(2) = 6 \times 1 = 6\ \text{bits per cell}$$ **8-dot cell:** $$C_{8} = 8 \log_{2}(2) = 8 \times 1 = 8\ \text{bits per cell}$$ ### Part (b) — Bits per second at refresh rate $f_r = 20$ Hz $$R_{6} = C_{6} \cdot f_r = 6 \times 20 = 120\ \text{bits/s}$$ $$R_{8} = C_{8} \cdot f_r = 8 \times 20 = 160\ \text{bits/s}$$ ### Part (c) — Equivalent reading speed A typical English word is **5 characters + 1 space = 6 characters**. With 1 character per cell: $$W_{6} = \dfrac{120\ \text{bits/s}}{6\ \text{bits/char}} \times \dfrac{60\ \text{s/min}}{6\ \text{char/word}}$$ $$W_{6} = 20 \times 10 = 200\ \text{words/min}$$ $$W_{8} = \dfrac{160}{8} \times \dfrac{60}{6} = 20 \times 10 = 200\ \text{wpm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Both cells yield the same wpm because the *information density per character* scales with cell capacity — an 8-dot cell can carry 33 % more info per character but with the same word structure. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Sound Pressure Level at Listener's Ear > **Problem:** A speaker outputs **0.05 Pa** RMS at 1 m. Compute (a) SPL in dB, (b) SPL at 4 m assuming inverse-square spreading, (c) effective loudness on the sone scale. ### Part (a) — SPL at 1 m $$\text{SPL}_{1} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.05}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(2500)$$ $$= 20 \times 3.398 = 67.96\ \text{dB SPL}$$ ### Part (b) — SPL at 4 m Intensity drops by $(1/4)^{2} = 1/16$, equivalent to **−12 dB** (since $10\log_{10}(1/16) = -12.04$ dB). $$\text{SPL}_{4} = 67.96 - 12.04 = 55.92\ \text{dB SPL}$$ ### Part (c) — Convert to sone Assuming a 1-kHz tone, 67.96 dB SPL ≈ 67.96 phon. Apply: $$S = 2^{(P - 40)/10} = 2^{(67.96 - 40)/10} = 2^{2.796} \approx 13.9\ \text{sones}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The listener perceives the source as **~13.9 sones** — about 14× louder than a 1-sone reference (40 dB SPL at 1 kHz). For a screen-reader at 4 m, the level (55.9 dB) is still comfortably above the 30-dB ambient recommended for office listening. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Vibrotactile Display JND & Number of Levels > **Problem:** A fingertip-mounted vibrotactile display operates at **250 Hz** with Weber fraction $\Delta a / a = 0.2$. If the actuator has a dynamic range from $a_{\min} = 0.1\ \mu m$ to $a_{\max} = 100\ \mu m$, compute the number of discriminable amplitude levels. ### Step 1 — Weber's law amplitude form: $$a_{n+1} = a_{n}(1 + 0.2) = 1.2 \cdot a_{n}$$ ### Step 2 — Geometric progression: $$a_{n} = a_{\min}(1.2)^{n-1}$$ ### Step 3 — Set $a_{n} = 100\ \mu m$, $a_{\min} = 0.1\ \mu m$: $$100 = 0.1 \times (1.2)^{n-1}$$ $$1000 = (1.2)^{n-1}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve: $$n - 1 = \dfrac{\ln 1000}{\ln 1.2} = \dfrac{6.908}{0.1823} \approx 37.9$$ $$n \approx 39\ \text{levels}$$ ### Step 5 — Information: $$I = \log_{2}(39) \approx 5.3\ \text{bits}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Design Rule:** Beyond ~39 levels, vibrotactile discrimination collapses — this is the empirical bandwidth ceiling for finger-tip voice-coil actuators. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Display Comparison Table for KTU | Parameter | Auditory Display | Vibrotactile Display | Braille Display | |---|---|---|---| | **Channel Capacity** | ~9 bits | ~5 bits | 6 or 8 bits/cell | | **Bandwidth** | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | 20 Hz – 1 kHz | Spatial refresh ~10 Hz | | **Resolution** | Time ~ ms | Time ~ 10 ms | Spatial ~ 2 mm | | **Cognitive Load** | Medium | High if dense | Low (pre-learned) | | **Privacy** | Low (broadcasts) | High (skin-only) | High (silent) | | **Eye Independence** | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Power** | ~1 W | ~0.1 W | ~0.5 W | | **Cost** | Low (speakers) | Medium | High (piezo/pin) | | **Use Case** | Screen readers, alerts | Navigation, gaming | Reading, writing | --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Audio Amplifier for Screen Reader > **Problem:** A pocket-sized screen reader uses a 0.5 W speaker with efficiency 1 % at 1 kHz. Compute (a) acoustic power radiated, (b) intensity at 0.3 m on-axis (assume spherical spreading), (c) SPL at the listener's ear. ### Solution **Step 1 — Acoustic power:** $$P_{\text{ac}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{elec}} = 0.01 \times 0.5 = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Intensity at 0.3 m (spherical):** $$I = \dfrac{P_{\text{ac}}}{4 \pi r^{2}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \pi (0.3)^{2}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.131} = 4.42 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W/m}^2$$ **Step 3 — SPL:** $$\text{SPL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(4.42 \times 10^{9})$$ $$= 10 \times (9 + \log_{10} 4.42) = 10 \times (9 + 0.645) = 96.45\ \text{dB SPL}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Take-away:** A 0.5 W pocket speaker produces ~96 dB SPL at 0.3 m — well above the 60-dB recommended speech level, justifying why screen-reader alarms are *hard to ignore*. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — General Auditory Display Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[Data Source
