# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Define Electromyography (EMG). List any two clinical applications of EMG."**

**Model Answer:**

Electromyography (EMG) is an electrodiagnostic technique used to record the electrical activity of skeletal muscles in response to neural stimulation. It involves the detection, amplification, and display of **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)** generated during voluntary or evoked muscle contraction.

**Two Clinical Applications:**
1. **Diagnosis of Neuromuscular Disorders:** Differentiates between neurogenic conditions (e.g., ALS, peripheral neuropathy) and myopathic conditions (e.g., muscular dystrophy, polymyositis) by analyzing MUAP morphology and recruitment patterns.
2. **Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS):** When combined with electrical nerve stimulation, EMG helps localize the site of peripheral nerve lesions (e.g., carpal tunnel syndrome) by measuring distal latencies and conduction velocities.

*(Other valid applications: biofeedback in stroke rehabilitation, myoelectric prosthetic control, intraoperative monitoring of nerve integrity, kinesiological analysis of gait and movement.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
**"Explain the significance of the Median Frequency (MDF) in EMG signal analysis. Mention its typical range."**

**Model Answer:**

The **Median Frequency (MDF)** is a spectral parameter of the EMG signal, defined as the frequency at which the EMG power spectrum is divided into two equal-energy halves. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$MDF : \int_{0}^{MDF} P(f)\, df = \int_{MDF}^{\infty} P(f)\, df = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} P(f)\, df$$

**Significance:**
- **Fatigue Quantification:** As a muscle fatigues during sustained contraction, its conduction velocity slows, causing a **shift in the power spectrum toward lower frequencies**. The MDF decreases linearly with fatigue.
- **Rehabilitation Monitoring:** Therapists use MDF trends to assess recovery progress in patients with chronic pain, post-surgical muscle atrophy, or neurological injury.
- **Ergonomic Workload Design:** Occupational health studies use MDF decline rates to design safe work-rest cycles.

**Typical Range:** For healthy skeletal muscle during moderate contraction, the MDF lies in the range of **60 Hz to 110 Hz**, with the most common values between **70 Hz and 90 Hz**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete EMG signal acquisition and processing chain. Describe the function of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare Surface EMG and Needle EMG with respect to invasiveness, pickup area, frequency bandwidth, and clinical application. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation [7 Marks]

A standard EMG acquisition system consists of the following cascaded stages:

**1. Biological Source — Muscle Fiber Depolarization [1 Mark]**
When a motor neuron fires, acetylcholine is released at the neuromuscular junction, depolarizing the muscle fiber membrane (sarcolemma). This ionic current spreads along the fiber and can be detected extracellularly.

**2. Electrode Interface [1 Mark]**
**Ag/AgCl electrodes** (for surface) or **concentric needle electrodes** (for clinical) transduce ionic current into electronic current. The skin–electrode impedance must be reduced below **5 kΩ** using skin preparation (alcohol cleaning, mild abrasion, conductive gel).

**3. Differential (Instrumentation) Amplifier [1 Mark]**
The raw signal is in the **μV range**, so a high-gain, high-CMRR (> 100 dB) **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128, AD620) is used. Differential amplification cancels common-mode noise (50/60 Hz interference, ambient electromagnetic pickup).

**4. Band-Pass Filter [1 Mark]**
A 2nd-order (or higher) **band-pass filter** with cutoff frequencies at **20 Hz** (removes motion artifact and DC offset) and **500 Hz** (removes high-frequency thermal noise) is applied.

**5. Notch Filter [0.5 Marks]**
A **50 Hz (or 60 Hz) notch filter** with a Q-factor of 8–10 surgically removes powerline interference without significantly affecting the EMG bandwidth.

**6. Main Amplifier and ADC [1 Mark]**
A secondary gain stage brings the signal to ±5 V range. The **Analog-to-Digital Converter** samples at **≥ 2 kHz** (Nyquist requires > 1 kHz; 2 kHz provides headroom) with 12–16 bit resolution.

**7. Digital Signal Processing [1 Mark]**
Algorithms compute **RMS amplitude**, **ARV**, **MNF/MDF**, and the **interference pattern** for clinical interpretation.

**8. Display and Storage [0.5 Marks]**
The processed signal is displayed on an oscilloscope/PC monitor and stored for longitudinal comparison (outcome measurement).

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table [7 Marks]

| **Parameter** | **Surface EMG (sEMG)** | **Needle EMG (nEMG)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive; skin-surface electrodes | Invasive; penetrates skin into muscle |
| **Pickup Area / Volume** | Large (multiple motor units; entire superficial muscle region) | Small (1–3 motor units within ~1 mm³) |
| **Frequency Bandwidth** | 20–500 Hz | 20 Hz – 10 kHz (wider high-frequency content) |
| **Spatial Selectivity** | Low; subject to **crosstalk** from adjacent muscles | High; can isolate single motor units |
| **Amplitude Range** | 50 μV – 5 mV | 200 μV – 2 mV (per MUAP) |
| **Pain / Patient Comfort** | Painless | Mild discomfort, risk of bruising/infection |
| **Dynamic Motion Use** | Yes — gait, sports, prosthetics | Restricted; needle may dislodge |
| **Deep Muscle Access** | No (superficial only) | Yes |
| **Clinical Application** | Kinesiology, ergonomics, prosthetics control, biofeedback | Diagnosis of neuropathy, myopathy, NMJ disorders |
| **Cost & Setup** | Low; quick setup | Higher; requires trained neurophysiologist |
| **Artifact Sensitivity** | High (motion, sweat, electrode shift) | Low |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** write "EMG measures muscle strength." It measures **electrical activity**, which only **correlates** with force.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **CMAP** (Compound Muscle Action Potential, evoked) with **MUAP** (voluntary single-unit).
> 3. **Do not** state the bandwidth as "0–20 kHz" for sEMG; the **standard clinical answer is 20–500 Hz**.
> 4. **Always** state **CMRR > 100 dB** in the instrumentation amplifier description.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

**(a)** Define Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP). Describe the EMG changes (in MUAP morphology, recruitment, and insertional activity) characteristic of **neurogenic** disorders. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the Root Mean Square (RMS) value of an EMG signal. Why is RMS preferred over peak-to-peak amplitude in clinical reporting? **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — MUAP & Neurogenic EMG Changes [7 Marks]

**Definition [2 Marks]:**
A **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** is the spatio-temporal summation of the action potentials of all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit, recorded extracellularly. It represents the smallest voluntarily recordable unit of muscle activity.

**Normal MUAP Parameters:**
- Duration: **3–15 ms**
- Amplitude: **200 μV – 2 mV**
- Phases: **2–4** (baseline crossings + 1)
- Polyphasic MUAPs: < 12% of all MUAPs in a normal muscle

**EMG Changes in Neurogenic Disorders (e.g., ALS, Radiculopathy, Peripheral Neuropathy) [5 Marks]:**

1. **Insertional Activity — Increased [1 Mark]:** Upon needle insertion, the denervated muscle produces prolonged, repetitive insertional discharges (1–3 seconds), reflecting hyperexcitable, fibrillating fibers.

2. **Spontaneous Activity — Pathological [1 Mark]:**
   - **Fibrillation Potentials:** Brief (1–5 ms), low-amplitude (50–200 μV), biphasic or triphasic spikes firing spontaneously. Indicates denervated single fibers.
   - **Positive Sharp Waves:** Initial positive (downward) deflection followed by slow negative phase; 10–30 ms duration.
   - **Fasciculations:** Large, irregular, involuntary MUAPs visible clinically and on EMG. Hallmark of anterior horn cell disease (ALS).

3. **MUAP Morphology — Altered [1.5 Marks]:**
   - **Amplitude: Increased** (> 2 mV, often 5–10 mV) due to **collateral reinnervation** — surviving motor neurons sprout to adopt orphaned fibers, increasing the territory per motor unit.
   - **Duration: Prolonged** (> 15 ms) because of increased spatial dispersion of the reinnervated fiber group.
   - **Polyphasia: Increased** (≥ 5 phases) due to asynchronous firing of newly adopted fibers.
   - **Unstable / "Jittery" MUAPs:** Vary in shape from discharge to discharge.

4. **Recruitment Pattern — Reduced [1.5 Marks]:**
   - **Reduced recruitment:** Because each motor unit produces higher force (due to reinnervation), fewer MUs are needed for a given force. Result: **discrete single-unit pattern** at low effort, with the active units firing rapidly.
   - **Rapid firing rate** of the few remaining MUs (> 25 Hz at low effort) — the **"picket fence" or single-unit recruitment** pattern.
   - **Interference pattern:** Incomplete / discrete at maximum voluntary effort.

#### Part (b) — RMS Derivation & Clinical Preference [7 Marks]

**Derivation [4 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** The instantaneous power delivered to a load R is:

$$p(t) = \frac{x^{2}(t)}{R}$$

**Step 2:** The average power over a window of duration T is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{x^{2}(t)}{R}\, dt = \frac{1}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x^{2}(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3:** By definition, the RMS value is the equivalent DC value that would deliver the same average power:

$$x_{RMS}^{2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x^{2}(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root:

$$x_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 5:** In discrete form (for N samples):

$$x_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{2}}$$

**Why RMS is Preferred Over Peak-to-Peak Amplitude [3 Marks]:**

1. **Statistical Robustness:** EMG is a stochastic, non-stationary signal. A single peak may be a noise spike or a rare MUAP firing; RMS averages across a window, giving a **stable, representative amplitude**.

2. **Physical Meaning:** RMS is directly proportional to the **average power** of the signal, and (in isometric conditions) correlates approximately linearly with **muscle force** between 20% and 80% MVC. Peak-to-peak has no such physical interpretation.

3. **Computational Smoothness:** RMS eliminates the need for baseline-crossing detection and peak-finding algorithms. It is computationally cheap ($O(N)$) and is the standard input feature for **prosthetic control** and **fatigue monitoring** systems.

4. **Comparison Across Time:** RMS values from different time windows (e.g., before vs after therapy) can be statistically compared (paired t-test, ANOVA) to quantify rehabilitation progress — making it ideal for **outcome measurement**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** stop at the discrete-time formula. Examiners want to see the **continuous-time integral form first**, then the discrete approximation.
> 2. **Do not** state "RMS = average amplitude." That is **ARV** (Average Rectified Value), not RMS. The two are **mathematically different** (RMS ≥ ARV).
> 3. **Do not** confuse RMS with **standard deviation** for a zero-mean signal. For a zero-mean stationary signal, RMS = standard deviation, but EMG is **non-stationary**; the distinction is essential.
> 4. In the neurogenic MUAP description, **always** mention "collateral reinnervation" as the cause of increased amplitude — it demonstrates true understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Neural Repair & Rehabilitation — EMG**

**A. Foundational Concepts**
- ☐ EMG measures **electrical activity** of muscle, not force directly.
- ☐ Source: **Motor Unit** (one alpha motor neuron + all its muscle fibers).
- ☐ Smallest voluntary unit: **MUAP** (Motor Unit Action Potential).
- ☐ Normal MUAP: 3–15 ms, 200 μV – 2 mV, 2–4 phases.

**B. Signal Characteristics**
- ☐ Frequency: **20 Hz – 500 Hz** (sEMG); up to **10 kHz** for needle.
- ☐ Amplitude: **50 μV – 5 mV** (sEMG surface).
- ☐ Standard parameters: **RMS, ARV, MNF, MDF, Conduction Velocity**.

**C. Acquisition System**
- ☐ Electrode types: **Surface (Ag/AgCl), Needle (concentric, monopolar), Single-fiber, Fine-wire**.
- ☐ Front-end: **Instrumentation amplifier** with **CMRR > 100 dB**, input impedance > 10¹² Ω.
- ☐ Band-pass: **20 Hz – 500 Hz**; Notch: **50/60 Hz**.
- ☐ Sampling rate: **≥ 2 kHz**; Resolution: **12–16 bit**.

**D. Clinical Patterns**
- ☐ **Neurogenic:** ↑ amplitude, ↑ duration, polyphasic, ↓ recruitment, fibrillations.
- ☐ **Myopathic:** ↓ amplitude, ↓ duration, polyphasic, early recruitment.
- ☐ **NMJ disorder (e.g., Myasthenia Gravis):** Use **Single-fiber EMG**; look for "jitter."

**E. Outcome Measurement & Rehabilitation Linkage**
- ☐ EMG is a **quantitative instrumented outcome** complementing clinical scales (FIM, Barthel, MRC).
- ☐ Used in **prosthetic control**, **biofeedback therapy**, **exoskeleton intent detection**, **FES**, and **ergonomic workload assessment**.
- ☐ **Iterative** — repeat EMG to monitor rehabilitation progress (closed-loop).

**F. Engineering Skills**
- ☐ Derive and apply **RMS** formula.
- ☐ Explain **MNF/MDF** and its fatigue correlation.
- ☐ Compute **SNR** and **CMRR** in dB.
- ☐ Interpret an **interference pattern** at different effort levels.

**G. Exam-Writing Hacks**
- ☐ Always state the **bandwidth (20–500 Hz)** when describing EMG.
- ☐ Always quote **CMRR > 100 dB** for the amplifier.
- ☐ Always mention **motor unit** when explaining MUAP.
- ☐ Always specify **surface vs needle** when discussing applications.
- ☐ Use the word **"collateral reinnervation"** when explaining neurogenic MUAP amplitude increase.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neuro Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT864 - REHABILITATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neuro Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 Neuro Imaging — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Neuro Imaging (Neurological Imaging)** is a multidisciplinary branch of biomedical engineering and neuroscience that employs physical principles (X-ray attenuation, nuclear magnetic resonance, positron emission, optical absorption, and biomagnetic fields) to non-invasively visualize the **structural, functional, and biochemical architecture** of the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nervous system. In the context of **KTU Rehabilitation Engineering (PEEBT864, 2024 Scheme)**, neuro imaging constitutes the diagnostic and monitoring backbone for designing **neuro-rehabilitation protocols, brain–computer interfaces (BCI), neuroprosthetic control, and motor recovery assessment** following stroke, traumatic brain injury (TBI), and neurodegenerative disorders.

The major modalities studied under this topic are classified as:

| Modality | Physical Principle | Information Type |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **CT** (Computed Tomography) | X-ray attenuation | Structural |
| **MRI** (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) | Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) | Structural / Soft tissue |
| **fMRI** (functional MRI) | BOLD — Blood Oxygen Level Dependent signal | Functional |
| **PET** (Positron Emission Tomography) | Radioactive tracer decay | Metabolic / Functional |
| **EEG** (Electroencephalography) | Electrical potentials on scalp | Electrical / Temporal |
| **MEG** (Magnetoencephalography) | Magnetic fields from neuronal currents | Electromagnetic / Temporal |
| **fNIRS** (functional Near-Infrared Spectroscopy) | Optical absorption of near-IR light by HbO/HbR | Hemodynamic / Functional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Rehabilitation Engineering students must understand *not only the physics* of these imaging systems, but also their **clinical relevance** — i.e., *how the image is interpreted, what pathology is detected, and how the imaging finding drives a rehabilitation decision* (e.g., prescribing an FES device, planning a BCI, or monitoring cortical plasticity post-stroke).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Google Maps of the Brain" 🗺️

Imagine the human brain as a vast, dense city with billions of citizens (neurons), thousands of streets (axons), and constant traffic (electrical signals). We cannot physically enter this city to observe it. **Neuro imaging is our satellite-and-traffic-camera system**:

- **CT scan** → A simple **top-down satellite map** showing buildings (bone, blood) but with poor resolution for soft districts (grey matter).
- **MRI** → A **high-resolution 3D city map** distinguishing parks, residential zones, and industrial areas (grey matter, white matter, CSF).
- **fMRI** → A **real-time traffic heat-map**, showing which districts (brain regions) are "congested" (active) right now.
- **PET** → A **specialist chemical-tracer drone**, finding specific factories (dopamine centres, amyloid plaques) by their emissions.
- **EEG** → A **network of traffic-control microphones** placed on the city's outskirts, listening to the aggregate noise of all traffic — excellent **time resolution** but poor spatial accuracy.
- **MEG** → The same microphones, but recording the *magnetic rumble* of traffic — cleaner signal, less skull distortion.
- **fNIRS** → A **pulsed-light blimp** hovering over the city, detecting oxygen use in different zones (similar to fMRI but cheaper and portable).

> [!TIP]
> **Rehab Engineering Takeaway:** The choice of modality in a rehabilitation setting is governed by the **time–space–portability–cost trade-off**. EEG is portable and fast but spatially coarse; MRI is anatomically beautiful but expensive and immobile. This trade-off directly dictates which tool is used for BCI (EEG/fNIRS) vs. surgical planning (MRI).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard values that KTU examiners love to test:
> - **Static magnetic field of clinical MRI:** $B_0 = 1.5 \text{ T}$ to $3.0 \text{ T}$ (research: up to $7.0 \text{ T}$).
> - **Larmor frequency** at 1.5 T (for hydrogen, $\gamma = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T}$): $f_0 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$.
> - **Typical EEG bandwidth:** $0.5 \text{ Hz}$ to $\sim 100 \text{ Hz}$ (clinical) / up to $500 \text{ Hz}$ (research).
> - **Positron range in tissue** (PET): $\sim 1 \text{ mm}$ (fundamental spatial limit).
> - **fNIRS wavelength window:** **650 nm to 950 nm** (the *optical/NIR window* of biological tissue).
> - **T1 relaxation** (grey matter at 1.5 T): $\sim 1300 \text{ ms}$; **T2 relaxation** (grey matter at 1.5 T): $\sim 90 \text{ ms}$.
> - **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)** safety limit (head, MRI): $\mathbf{4 \text{ W/kg}}$ averaged over 6 minutes (IEC standard).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Computed Tomography (CT) — Hounsfield Units & Reconstruction

CT uses a rotating X-ray source-detector pair. The key parameter is the **linear attenuation coefficient** $\mu(x,y)$ of tissue. After logarithmic transformation, the projection data is reconstructed using the **Radon Transform** and **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**.

The normalised grey-scale index used clinically is the **Hounsfield Unit (HU)**:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

- **Air:** $\approx -1000$ HU
- **Fat:** $\approx -100$ HU
- **Water:** $0$ HU
- **White matter:** $\approx 25$ HU
- **Grey matter:** $\approx 40$ HU
- **Acute blood:** $\approx 60$ to $100$ HU
- **Bone:** $\geq 400$ HU
- **Metal implant:** $> 1000$ HU (causes streak artefact)

**Why it matters for rehabilitation:** CT is the **gold standard for ruling out haemorrhage** in acute stroke before administering thrombolytics — the critical first decision point that determines whether a patient enters a motor-recovery rehab pathway or a neurosurgical pathway.

## 2.2 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) — Relaxation Theory

### 2.2.1 The Physics

When a patient is placed in $B_0$, hydrogen proton spins precess at the Larmor frequency:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

where $\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.58 \text{ MHz/T}$ is the gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen. A 90° RF pulse at $\omega_0$ tips the net magnetisation $M_0$ into the transverse plane. Two simultaneous relaxation processes occur:

- **T1 (longitudinal / spin–lattice) recovery:** the regrowth of $M_z$ back to $M_0$.

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right)$$

- **T2 (transverse / spin–spin) decay:** the loss of transverse coherence $M_{xy}$.

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \, e^{-t/T_2}$$

In reality, local field inhomogeneities cause an even faster decay, **T2\***, used in gradient-echo and fMRI:

$$\frac{1}{T_2^*} = \frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_2'}$$

### 2.2.2 The Signal Equation

For a spin-echo sequence with repetition time **TR** and echo time **TE**, the received signal intensity is:

$$S = k \, \rho \, \left( 1 - e^{-TR/T_1} \right) \, e^{-TE/T_2}$$

where $\rho$ is the **proton (spin) density**.

| Sequence | Weighting | Condition |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Spin Echo, **short TE, short TR** | T1-weighted (T1W) | TE $\ll$ T2, TR $\ll$ T1 |
| Spin Echo, **long TE, long TR** | T2-weighted (T2W) | TE $\approx$ T2, TR $\gg$ T1 |
| Spin Echo, **short TE, long TR** | Proton density (PD) | TE $\ll$ T2, TR $\gg$ T1 |
| Gradient Echo | T2\*-weighted | (basis of fMRI) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exams:** "**WW2** — **W**eak TR + **W**eak TE = **T1**; **S**trong TR + **S**trong TE = **T2**." Memorise this for the 3-mark KTU question on MRI contrast.

## 2.3 Functional MRI (fMRI) — The BOLD Signal

fMRI does **not** measure neural firing directly. It measures the **Blood Oxygen Level Dependent (BOLD)** signal. When a brain region activates:

1. Neural activity increases local oxygen consumption (CMRO₂↑) — but only briefly.
2. Cerebral Blood Flow (CBF) overcompensates, delivering **excess oxygenated haemoglobin (HbO)**.
3. Oxy-haemoglobin is **diamagnetic**; deoxy-haemoglobin is **paramagnetic**.
4. The local reduction in paramagnetic deoxy-Hb causes an **increase in T2\*** and therefore **higher MR signal**.

The BOLD signal change is small — typically **1% to 5%** at 1.5 T and **2% to 10%** at 3 T.

The field-dependence of the BOLD effect is described empirically:

$$\Delta S / S_0 \propto B_0^{\alpha} \quad \text{where } \alpha \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2$$

**Rehab relevance:** fMRI is used to **map cortical reorganisation** after stroke, guiding the choice of therapy (e.g., constraint-induced movement therapy vs. robotic gait training).

## 2.4 PET — Radioactive Decay & Coincidence Detection

A radio-labelled tracer (e.g., $^{18}\text{F}$-FDG, $^{11}\text{C}$-raclopride) emits a **positron** ($\beta^+$). After travelling $\sim 1$ mm, the positron annihilates with an electron, producing **two 511 keV $\gamma$-photons emitted at 180°** to each other.

Detector pairs register these in **coincidence** (within a 6–12 ns timing window). The line connecting the two detectors is the **Line of Response (LOR)**. Tracer concentration $C$ is reconstructed via:

$$C(x,y) = \sum_{\text{all LORs}} N_{\text{coinc}} \cdot \text{System\_Response}^{-1} \cdot \text{Attenuation\_Correction}$$

**Rehab relevance:** PET with amyloid/tau tracers is critical in **Alzheimer's and Parkinson's rehabilitation**; dopamine-transporter PET (DaTscan) helps distinguish Parkinson's from essential tremor before prescribing assistive devices.

## 2.5 EEG — Electrical Source Imaging

EEG measures scalp potentials $V(t)$ produced by summed post-synaptic currents of cortical pyramidal neurons (oriented perpendicular to the scalp). The forward problem is:

$$V(t) = \mathbf{L} \cdot \mathbf{J}(t) + \mathbf{n}(t)$$

where $\mathbf{L}$ is the **Lead-field matrix** (head model), $\mathbf{J}$ is the **current dipole source vector**, and $\mathbf{n}$ is noise.

**Five classical EEG rhythms** (high-yield for KTU):

| Band | Frequency | Associated State |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep, pathological (TBI) |
| Theta ($\theta$) | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, memory encoding |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed (posterior dominant) |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, motor planning (mu rhythm ~ 8–12 Hz over sensorimotor cortex) |
| Gamma ($\gamma$) | > 30 Hz | Cognitive binding, conscious perception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point — Event-Related Desynchronisation (ERD):** When a person *imagines* moving a limb, the **mu rhythm (8–12 Hz) over the contralateral sensorimotor cortex decreases in amplitude** (ERD). This is the **fundamental physiological basis of motor-imagery BCIs** used in stroke rehabilitation. ERD is sometimes quoted as a $\sim 10$ to $20\%$ power drop relative to baseline.

## 2.6 MEG — Biomagnetism

MEG detects the ultra-weak magnetic fields ($\sim 10^{-15}$ T, i.e., femtotesla — about $10^8$ times weaker than Earth's field) generated by the same neuronal currents that produce EEG. Because magnetic fields are **undistorted by the skull and scalp** (unlike electric fields), MEG has superior spatial resolution ($\sim 2$–$3$ mm) compared to EEG ($\sim 2$–$3$ cm).

The relationship between magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ and current source $\mathbf{J}$ is given by the **Biot–Savart law**:

$$\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}') \times (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}')}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert^3} \, dV'$$

MEG requires **SQUIDs (Superconducting Quantum Interference Devices)** operating at $4.2$ K in liquid helium — explaining its very high cost.

## 2.7 fNIRS — Modified Beer–Lambert Law

fNIRS uses near-infrared light (650–950 nm) emitted into the scalp. The modified Beer–Lambert law describes light attenuation:

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I_0}{I} \right) = \varepsilon \, c \, d \, \text{DPF} + G$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the molar extinction coefficient, $c$ is the chromophore concentration, $d$ is the source–detector separation, **DPF** is the **Differential Pathlength Factor** (typically $\sim 6$ for adult head at 800 nm), and $G$ is a geometry-dependent scattering loss.

By using **at least two wavelengths** (e.g., 690 nm and 830 nm), the concentrations of **HbO** (oxygenated haemoglobin) and **HbR** (deoxygenated haemoglobin) can be computed. fNIRS is portable, cheap, and tolerant of movement — making it ideal for **paediatric rehab and gait-lab BCI studies**.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe `\vert` symbol is used for absolute value below instead of the typographic pipe `|` to preserve the markdown table.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Larmor frequency | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | $\gamma_{\text{H}} = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T}$ |
| 2 | MRI signal (spin-echo) | $S = k \rho (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) e^{-TE/T_2}$ | $S$ in arbitrary units |
| 3 | Hounsfield Unit | $HU = 1000 (\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | T2\* decay | $1/T_2^* = 1/T_2 + 1/T_2'$ | Hz |
| 5 | BOLD field scaling | $\Delta S/S_0 \propto B_0^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 1.5$–$2$ | Empirical |
| 6 | Beer–Lambert (fNIRS) | $A = \varepsilon c d \cdot DPF + G$ | Optical Density |
| 7 | EEG forward model | $V = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{J} + \mathbf{n}$ | Vector form |
| 8 | Biot–Savart (MEG) | $\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J} \times \mathbf{\hat{r}}}{r^2} dV'$ | Tesla |
| 9 | PET photon energy | $E_\gamma = 511 \text{ keV}$ | Annihilation line |
| 10 | Positron range | $r_p \sim 1 \text{ mm}$ (tissue) | Spatial limit |
| 11 | BOLD signal magnitude | $\Delta S/S_0 \approx 1\%–5\%$ at 1.5 T | Functional MRI |
| 12 | Mu-rhythm band | $8$–$12$ Hz | Motor cortex, BCI key |
| 13 | MRI safety (head SAR) | $SAR \le 4 \text{ W/kg}$ / 6 min | IEC standard |
| 14 | Optical NIR window | $650$–$950$ nm | fNIRS |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Modality | Clinical Rehab Engineering Use |
|----------|--------------------------------|
| CT | Acute stroke triage, bony anatomy for FES electrode planning |
| MRI | Surgical planning of DBS (Deep Brain Stimulation) electrodes |
| fMRI | Pre-surgical motor mapping, BCI training protocols |
| PET | Differential diagnosis of Parkinsonian syndromes, drug efficacy trials |
| EEG | Motor-imagery BCI, seizure monitoring in TBI rehab, neurofeedback |
| MEG | Epilepsy surgery planning, language lateralisation for rehab prognosis |
| fNIRS | Paediatric BCI, walking-BCI in gait labs, portable cortical monitoring |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive and exam-grade**. Each step is shown in full, with explanatory commentary. **Do not skip steps** when writing your own answers in the KTU exam.

## 3.1 Derivation of the MRI Spin-Echo Signal Equation

We begin with the **Bloch equations** for net magnetisation $\mathbf{M} = (M_x, M_y, M_z)$ in a static field $B_0 \hat{\mathbf{z}}$:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (\mathbf{M} \times \mathbf{B})_x - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (\mathbf{M} \times \mathbf{B})_y - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (\mathbf{M} \times \mathbf{B})_z - \frac{M_z - M_0}{T_1}$$

In the rotating frame at $\omega_0$, $\mathbf{B}_{\text{eff}} = (0, 0, B_0 - \omega_0/\gamma)$. On resonance ($B_0 = \omega_0/\gamma$), $\mathbf{B}_{\text{eff}} = 0$, and the free precession solution is:

**Step 1** — Apply 90° RF pulse at $t = 0$. This tips $M_z$ fully into the transverse plane:
$$M_x(0^+) = M_0, \quad M_y(0^+) = 0, \quad M_z(0^+) = 0$$

**Step 2** — Free precession for time $TE/2$. The transverse component decays with T2 and longitudinal regrows with T1:

$$M_x(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \cos(0) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2}$$

$$M_y(t) = 0$$

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right)$$

**Step 3** — At $t = TE/2$, apply 180° RF pulse. This flips the transverse magnetisation in the rotating frame:
$$M_x(TE/2^+) = -M_0 \, e^{-TE/(2T_2)}, \quad M_y(TE/2^+) = 0$$

**Step 4** — Continue free precession for another $TE/2$. The transverse component reforms as an **echo** at $t = TE$:

$$M_x(TE) = -M_0 \, e^{-TE/(2T_2)} \cdot e^{-TE/(2T_2)} = -M_0 \, e^{-TE/T_2}$$

$$M_y(TE) = 0$$

The negative sign is a phase convention; the **magnitude** of the echo signal is:

$$\vert S_{\text{SE}} \vert = k \, \rho \, \left( 1 - e^{-TR/T_1} \right) \, e^{-TE/T_2}$$

This is the working spin-echo signal equation. To obtain **complete longitudinal recovery** between successive TR intervals, we require $TR \gg T_1$; for T1-weighting, we *deliberately* truncate this by setting $TR \approx T_1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-writing tip:** When asked to "derive" the signal equation, the marks are awarded for: (i) the rotating-frame Bloch equation setup, (ii) initial-condition specification after the 90° pulse, (iii) explicit decay terms with $T_1$ and $T_2$, and (iv) the refocusing action of the 180° pulse. **Do not skip any of these four steps.**

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hounsfield Unit (from Beer–Lambert for X-rays)

The X-ray intensity after traversing a tissue of thickness $x$ with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ is given by the **Beer–Lambert law** for ionising radiation:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 1** — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the **projection line integral**:

$$p = -\ln(I/I_0) = \int_{\text{ray}} \mu(x,y) \, ds$$

**Step 2** — Define the reference line integral for water of the same thickness:

$$p_w = \mu_w \, x_w$$

**Step 3** — Form the ratio relative to water and scale by 1000:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$$

**Step 4** — Show the four canonical values used in clinical practice:

- Air ($\mu \approx 0$): $HU = 1000 \times (0 - \mu_w)/\mu_w = -1000$
- Fat ($\mu \approx 0.85 \mu_w$): $HU = 1000 \times (0.85 - 1) = -150$ (clinical range $-100 \pm 30$)
- Water: $HU = 0$ by definition
- Cortical bone ($\mu \approx 1.9 \mu_w$): $HU = 1000 \times 0.9 = +900$ (clinical range $\ge 400$)

## 3.3 Derivation of the BOLD Signal Sensitivity Scaling

The **intravascular signal change** in GRE-fMRI is related to the change in susceptibility $\Delta \chi$ due to deoxy-haemoglobin change $\Delta[\text{dHb}]$. Using the static dephasing regime:

$$\Delta R_2^* = \gamma \cdot \Delta B_0 \cdot \text{TE}$$

with $\Delta B_0 \propto B_0 \cdot \Delta \chi$, and $\Delta \chi \propto \Delta[\text{dHb}]$, we get:

$$\Delta R_2^* \propto B_0 \cdot \Delta[\text{dHb}]$$

The fractional BOLD signal change is:

$$\frac{\Delta S}{S_0} = - \text{TE} \cdot \Delta R_2^* \cdot e^{-TE/T_2^*} \propto B_0^{\alpha}, \quad \alpha \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2$$

This scaling explains why **3 T MRI is preferred over 1.5 T** for functional imaging — it gives roughly **double the BOLD sensitivity** for the same TE.

## 3.4 Derivation of the fNIRS Concentration Equation

We use two wavelengths $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ to resolve the two chromophores HbO and HbR. The Beer–Lambert system in matrix form is:

$$\begin{aligned}
A(\lambda_1) &= \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_1) \, c_{\text{HbO}} \, d \cdot \text{DPF}(\lambda_1) + \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_1) \, c_{\text{HbR}} \, d \cdot \text{DPF}(\lambda_1) + G_1 \\
A(\lambda_2) &= \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_2) \, c_{\text{HbO}} \, d \cdot \text{DPF}(\lambda_2) + \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_2) \, c_{\text{HbR}} \, d \cdot \text{DPF}(\lambda_2) + G_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1** — Take the temporal difference $\Delta A = A(t) - A(t_0)$ to remove the constant geometry term $G$:

$$\Delta A(\lambda_i) = \left( \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_i) \, \Delta c_{\text{HbO}} + \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_i) \, \Delta c_{\text{HbR}} \right) \, d \cdot \text{DPF}(\lambda_i)$$

**Step 2** — Write as a $2 \times 2$ linear system:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta A(\lambda_1) \\ \Delta A(\lambda_2) \end{bmatrix} &= d \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \text{DPF}(\lambda_1) & 0 \\ 0 & \text{DPF}(\lambda_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_1) & \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_1) \\ \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_2) & \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta c_{\text{HbO}} \\ \Delta c_{\text{HbR}} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3** — Invert the $2 \times 2$ matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta c_{\text{HbO}} \\ \Delta c_{\text{HbR}} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{d} \begin{bmatrix} \text{DPF}(\lambda_1) & 0 \\ 0 & \text{DPF}(\lambda_2) \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_1) & \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_1) \\ \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_2) & \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_2) \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta A(\lambda_1) \\ \Delta A(\lambda_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

The inverse exists because the extinction coefficients at $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are sufficiently different. This gives absolute concentration *changes* in micromolar units, which is the **fNIRS output used in BCI** (typically $\Delta \text{HbO} \sim 0.1$ to $1.0$ $\mu\text{M}$ during a cognitive task).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Time vs. Space vs. Portability

| Modality | Temporal Resolution | Spatial Resolution | Portability | Approx. Cost (INR) |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| CT | minutes | $\sim 0.5$ mm | No (fixed room) | ₹ 1–3 Cr |
| MRI | minutes | $\sim 1$ mm | No | ₹ 5–15 Cr |
| fMRI | seconds | $\sim 3$ mm | No | (with MRI) |
| PET | minutes | $\sim 4$–$5$ mm | No | ₹ 8–20 Cr |
| **EEG** | **milliseconds** | $\sim 2$–$3$ cm | **Yes (wearable)** | ₹ 50 K – 5 L |
| MEG | milliseconds | $\sim 2$–$3$ mm | No (shielded room) | ₹ 30+ Cr |
| **fNIRS** | seconds | $\sim 1$–$2$ cm | **Yes (headband)** | ₹ 5–50 L |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-friendly observation:** EEG and fNIRS are the only modalities offering **portability and millisecond-scale temporal resolution** — this is why they dominate the **BCI-driven rehabilitation** market for stroke and ALS patients.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]`
**Define Hounsfield Unit. List the approximate HU values for air, water, CSF, white matter, grey matter, and bone.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is a normalised dimensionless scale used in CT to express radiodensity relative to water:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

| Tissue | HU (approx.) |
|--------|--------------|
| Air | $-1000$ |
| Fat | $-100$ |
| Water | $0$ |
| CSF | $+15$ |
| White matter | $+25$ |
| Grey matter | $+40$ |
| Acute blood | $+60$ to $+100$ |
| Cortical bone | $\ge +400$ |

(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, table 1)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]`
**What is the BOLD signal in fMRI? Why is it relevant to motor rehabilitation?**

**Model Answer:**

BOLD stands for **Blood Oxygen Level Dependent** signal. It is the contrast mechanism in fMRI based on the differing magnetic properties of oxy-haemoglobin (diamagnetic) and deoxy-haemoglobin (paramagnetic). When a brain region becomes neurally active, an overcompensatory increase in cerebral blood flow produces a local reduction in deoxy-Hb, which increases T2\* and thus the measured MR signal. The fractional signal change is small (1% to 5% at 1.5 T).

**Rehab relevance:** BOLD-fMRI is used to **map cortical reorganisation after stroke**, distinguishing the *lesioned* from *peri-lesional* motor cortex. This map is used to (i) plan surgical corridors that spare eloquent motor areas, (ii) decide which patients are candidates for **constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT)** or **BCI-driven motor imagery training**, and (iii) track longitudinal recovery — a measurable increase in BOLD activation in the ipsilesional hemisphere correlates with better motor outcome.

(3 marks: definition with mechanism 2, rehab relevance 1)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the principle of **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)**. Derive the spin-echo signal equation from the Bloch equations. State the conditions for T1-weighted, T2-weighted, and proton-density-weighted images. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the principle of **functional MRI (fMRI)**. Discuss the BOLD mechanism, the typical signal magnitude, the role of T2\*, and the field-strength dependence of the BOLD sensitivity. Give two rehabilitation-engineering applications of fMRI. (7 marks)

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):** MRI is based on **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** of hydrogen nuclei (protons) abundant in biological tissue. When the patient is placed in a strong static magnetic field $B_0$ (typically 1.5 T or 3 T), proton spins precess at the Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$. A radio-frequency (RF) pulse at $\omega_0$ tips the net magnetisation; the precessing transverse component induces a measurable voltage in a receiver coil. Spatial encoding is achieved using linear **gradient fields** $G_x, G_y, G_z$ in the three orthogonal directions, allowing reconstruction of a 2D or 3D image via the **Inverse Fourier Transform** of the acquired *k-space* data.

**Block diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Magnet B0] --> B[RF Transmit Coil]
    B --> C[Patient Tissue]
    C --> D[Gradient Coils Gx Gy Gz]
    C --> E[RF Receive Coil]
    E --> F[Preamp + ADC]
    F --> G[k-Space Filler]
    G --> H[2D Inverse FFT]
    H --> I[MR Image]
```

**Derivation of the spin-echo signal (3 marks):** See Section 3.1 above. The full step-by-step derivation is reproduced here in summary form:

- Initial condition after 90° pulse: $M_x(0) = M_0, M_z(0) = 0$.
- Free precession for $TE/2$ with T1 and T2 relaxation.
- 180° pulse flips transverse magnetisation: $M_x \to -M_x$.
- Continue for another $TE/2$ to form echo at $t = TE$:

$$S_{\text{SE}} = k \, \rho \, \left( 1 - e^{-TR/T_1} \right) \, e^{-TE/T_2}$$

**Weighting conditions (1 mark):**

| Weighting | TE | TR |
|-----------|----|----|
| T1W | short ($\ll T_2$) | short ($\sim T_1$) |
| T2W | long ($\sim T_2$) | long ($\gg T_1$) |
| PDW | short ($\ll T_2$) | long ($\gg T_1$) |

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Principle of fMRI (2 marks):** fMRI is MRI that is sensitive to **blood oxygenation changes** linked to neural activity. The standard implementation is **Gradient Echo EPI (Echo Planar Imaging)**, which is T2\*-weighted and fast enough ($\sim 1$ to $2$ s per whole-brain volume) to capture dynamic cortical activation.

**BOLD mechanism (2 marks):** Neuronal activity increases local oxygen consumption (CMRO₂), but Cerebral Blood Flow (CBF) overcompensates. This delivers a transient excess of oxy-haemoglobin. Since deoxy-Hb is **paramagnetic** (susceptibility $\Delta \chi \ne 0$) and oxy-Hb is **diamagnetic** ($\Delta \chi \approx 0$), the local susceptibility-induced field inhomogeneity decreases, $T_2^*$ lengthens, and the GRE signal **increases** by **1%–5%** at 1.5 T and **2%–10%** at 3 T.

**T2\* role (1 mark):** The effective transverse relaxation is

$$\frac{1}{T_2^*} = \frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_2'}$$

The extra term $T_2'$ arises from macroscopic field inhomogeneities, including those caused by deoxy-Hb. As deoxy-Hb falls, $T_2^*$ rises, and the GRE signal (which is $e^{-TE/T_2^*}$-weighted) increases.

**Field-strength dependence (1 mark):** The fractional BOLD change scales as

$$\frac{\Delta S}{S_0} \propto B_0^{\alpha}, \quad \alpha \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2$$

Therefore, doubling $B_0$ from 1.5 T to 3 T roughly doubles the BOLD sensitivity, motivating 3 T systems for research rehab studies.

**Two rehabilitation applications (1 mark):**
1. **Pre-surgical motor mapping** before tumour resection near the motor cortex — ensures the rehab plan is anatomically anchored.
2. **Longitudinal monitoring of cortical reorganisation** after stroke to evaluate the efficacy of CIMT, robotic therapy, or BCI training.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Electroencephalography (EEG)**. Describe the five classical EEG rhythms with their frequency bands and clinical significance. What is the **mu rhythm** and why is it the physiological basis of motor-imagery brain–computer interfaces used in stroke rehabilitation? (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the principle of **functional Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (fNIRS)**. State the modified Beer–Lambert law. Show how two wavelengths can be used to compute concentration changes of oxygenated and deoxygenated haemoglobin. Compare fNIRS with fMRI and EEG in terms of spatial resolution, temporal resolution, portability, and cost. (7 marks)

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Principle of EEG (2 marks):** EEG measures the **summed post-synaptic potential differences** between electrodes placed on the scalp. The primary generators are **cortical pyramidal neurons** of layers III and V, whose apical dendrites are oriented perpendicular to the scalp surface, producing dipoles that summate extracellularly. The forward model is $V = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{J} + \mathbf{n}$ where $\mathbf{L}$ is the lead-field matrix derived from the head model (e.g., three-shell sphere or BEM). EEG has excellent **temporal resolution (ms)** but coarse **spatial resolution (2–3 cm)** because of volume conduction through the skull.

**Five classical rhythms (3 marks):**

| Rhythm | Band | State / Significance |
|--------|------|----------------------|
| $\delta$ (delta) | 0.5–4 Hz | Deep sleep; pathological in awake adults (TBI, encephalopathy) |
| $\theta$ (theta) | 4–8 Hz | Drowsiness, memory encoding; frontal midline theta in mental tasks |
| $\alpha$ (alpha) | 8–13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed, posterior dominant rhythm |
| $\beta$ (beta) | 13–30 Hz | Active thinking, motor preparation, anxiety |
| $\gamma$ (gamma) | > 30 Hz | Sensory binding, conscious perception |

**Mu rhythm and motor-imagery BCI (2 marks):** The **mu rhythm** is a 8–12 Hz oscillation recorded over the **sensorimotor cortex (electrodes C3, Cz, C4 in the 10–20 system)** that is *idling* in the relaxed state. When the subject **imagines moving** a limb (e.g., right-hand motor imagery), the mu rhythm over the **contralateral** sensorimotor cortex shows a decrease in band power known as **Event-Related Desynchronisation (ERD)**, typically a **10–20% drop relative to baseline**. The corresponding post-movement or relaxation period shows Event-Related Synchronisation (ERS). Detecting this ERD in real time using a classifier (LDA, SVM, or CNN) allows the subject to **volitionally control an external device** (FES, exoskeleton, cursor). In stroke rehab, this closed-loop BCI drives **Hebbian re-learning**: the patient imagines movement, the BCI detects it, and the FES or robot moves the actual limb. The synchronously activated cortex and limb reinforce surviving corticospinal pathways, promoting **functional recovery**.

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Principle of fNIRS (1.5 marks):** fNIRS illuminates the scalp with **near-infrared light** in the **optical window 650–950 nm**, where biological tissue is relatively transparent. Two main chromophores absorb differentially: **HbO** ($\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 830$ nm) and **HbR** ($\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 760$ nm). The back-scattered light carries information about concentration changes of these chromophores in the **outer 1–3 cm of cortex**.

**Modified Beer–Lambert Law (1.5 marks):**

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I_0}{I} \right) = \varepsilon \, c \, d \, \text{DPF} + G$$

where $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (cm⁻¹ M⁻¹), $c$ = chromophore concentration (M), $d$ = source–detector separation (cm), **DPF** = Differential Pathlength Factor (corrects for scattering, $\sim 6$ at 800 nm in adult head), $G$ = geometry/scatter constant.

**Two-wavelength inversion (2 marks):** Using wavelengths $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ and forming the temporal difference $\Delta A = A(t) - A(t_0)$ to remove $G$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta A(\lambda_1) &= d \cdot \text{DPF}(\lambda_1) \left[ \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_1) \Delta c_{\text{HbO}} + \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_1) \Delta c_{\text{HbR}} \right] \\
\Delta A(\lambda_2) &= d \cdot \text{DPF}(\lambda_2) \left[ \varepsilon_{\text{HbO}}(\lambda_2) \Delta c_{\text{HbO}} + \varepsilon_{\text{HbR}}(\lambda_2) \Delta c_{\text{HbR}} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Solving the $2 \times 2$ linear system yields $\Delta c_{\text{HbO}}$ and $\Delta c_{\text{HbR}}$. Typical values during a cognitive/motor task are $\Delta \text{HbO} \sim +0.1$ to $+1.0 \, \mu\text{M}$ and $\Delta \text{HbR} \sim -0.05$ to $-0.3 \, \mu\text{M}$.

**Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Property | fMRI | EEG | fNIRS |
|----------|------|-----|-------|
| Spatial resolution | $\sim 3$ mm (whole brain) | $\sim 2$–$3$ cm | $\sim 1$–$2$ cm (cortical only) |
| Temporal resolution | $\sim 1$–$2$ s | $\sim 1$ ms | $\sim 1$–$10$ s |
| Depth | whole brain | superficial cortex | $\sim 1$–$3$ cm cortex |
| Portability | No (fixed room) | Yes (wearable) | Yes (headband) |
| Motion tolerance | Low | Moderate | High |
| Approx. cost | ₹ 5–15 Cr | ₹ 50 K – 5 L | ₹ 5–50 L |
| Clinical BCI use | Limited (research) | Extensive | Growing |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks in Neuro Imaging questions:**
> 1. **Confusing T1 and T2 sequences.** "**T1 is longitudinal recovery along $M_z$**; **T2 is transverse decay in $M_{xy}$**." T1W images have **short TE and short TR**; T2W have **long TE and long TR**. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Stating that fMRI measures neural activity directly.** It does **not** — it measures the **BOLD hemodynamic response**, which is delayed by 4–6 s relative to neural firing.
> 3. **Forgetting the constants** $\gamma = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T}$ for hydrogen and the field strength $B_0 = 1.5/3$ T. Examiners *will* test the Larmor formula.
> 4. **Omitting the 180° refocusing pulse** when "deriving" the spin-echo signal. Without it, the echo formation is unexplained — expect 1–2 marks lost.
> 5. **Writing "BOLD = Blood Oxygenation Level Decrease"** — the correct expansion is **Blood Oxygen Level Dependent**. Examiners catch this.
> 6. **In fNIRS, stating absolute concentrations.** The modified Beer–Lambert law only gives *changes* $\Delta c$, not absolute values, because of the unknown geometry factor $G$.
> 7. **Mixing up mu rhythm with alpha rhythm.** Mu (8–12 Hz) is over the **sensorimotor cortex** and reflects motor idling; alpha is over the **occipital cortex** and reflects visual idling.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Bullet List — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **CT** uses X-ray attenuation; the image scale is **Hounsfield Units (HU)**: air $=-1000$, water $= 0$, bone $\ge +400$. Acute blood is hyperdense ($\sim +60$ to $+100$).
- ✅ **MRI** uses nuclear magnetic resonance of **hydrogen protons**; key formula: $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ with $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T. Signal: $S = k\rho(1 - e^{-TR/T_1})e^{-TE/T_2}$.
- ✅ **Sequence weighting mnemonic:** T1W = Short TE, Short TR; T2W = Long TE, Long TR; PDW = Short TE, Long TR.
- ✅ **fMRI (BOLD)** is an *indirect* measure of neural activity via haemodynamic coupling. Signal change $\Delta S/S_0 \propto B_0^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 1.5$–$2$. Typical change is **1%–5%** at 1.5 T.
- ✅ **PET** detects **511 keV coincident photons** from positron annihilation. Spatial resolution is limited by positron range ($\sim 1$ mm) to $\sim 4$–$5$ mm.
- ✅ **EEG** measures scalp potentials, dominated by **pyramidal neurons**; millisecond temporal resolution but 2–3 cm spatial resolution. Forward model: $V = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{J} + \mathbf{n}$.
- ✅ **Five EEG rhythms:** $\delta$ (0.5–4), $\theta$ (4–8), $\alpha$ (8–13), $\beta$ (13–30), $\gamma$ (>30 Hz).
- ✅ **Mu rhythm (8–12 Hz) over sensorimotor cortex** is the BCI key. **ERD (Event-Related Desynchronisation)** during motor imagery = a **10–20% drop** in mu power over the contralateral cortex.
- ✅ **MEG** uses **SQUIDs** to detect femtotesla-scale magnetic fields ($\sim 10^{-15}$ T); Biot–Savart law; better spatial resolution than EEG.
- ✅ **fNIRS** uses **650–950 nm NIR light**; modified Beer–Lambert law with **DPF** $\sim 6$ (adult). Uses **two wavelengths** to resolve HbO and HbR; output is concentration *changes* (not absolute).
- ✅ **Rehab engineering applications:** stroke motor recovery mapping (fMRI), BCI-FES for paralyzed limb (EEG mu-ERD), paediatric BCI (fNIRS), DBS surgical planning (MRI), pre-surgical language/motor mapping (MEG+fMRI), Alzheimer's vs. Parkinson's differential (PET).
- ✅ **Safety:** MRI head SAR $\le 4$ W/kg/6 min; MRI is **contraindicated** with ferromagnetic implants; MEG/fNIRS are non-ionising and safe.
- ✅ **Trade-off principle:** *Time vs. Space vs. Portability vs. Cost* — no single modality wins on all four. Choose based on rehab-engineering requirement.
- ✅ **Fusion:** Modern rehab research combines **EEG + fNIRS** (time + portability), or **fMRI + MEG** (anatomy + time), or **EEG + MRI structural** (BCI with patient-specific head model).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
